items.statement: Ordinary convergence implies Abel summability to the same value.
items.formal_statement: If $\sum_{n=0}^\infty a_n=s$ converges, then $\lim_{r\to1^-}\sum_{n=0}^\infty a_nr^n=s$. For Fourier series, Abel means agree with the ordinary limit wherever ordinary convergence is already valid.
items.intuition: Abel summation is consistent: the gentler radial limiting process does not change answers that already converge honestly.
assumptions: The displayed scalar form is one-sided in $r$, with $0\le r<1$.

items.label: Translation & Modulation
items.statement: Translation in space becomes modulation in frequency, and modulation in space becomes translation in frequency.
items.formal_statement: For suitable $f$, $\widehat{f(\cdot-a)}(\xi)=e^{-2\pi ia\xi}\hat f(\xi)$ and $\widehat{e^{2\pi ibx}f(x)}(\xi)=\hat f(\xi-b)$.
items.intuition: Moving a signal changes phases but not frequency magnitudes; multiplying by a pure wave shifts the frequency content.
assumptions: Uses the graph's $2\pi$-frequency convention.
The formulas hold for $L^1$ functions; Schwartz functions give a safe default setting.
items.ref: SteinŌĆōShakarchi: Fourier Analysis, Ch.5 Proposition 1.2; UC Davis notes on Fourier transform properties

metadata.source: SteinŌĆōShakarchi: Fourier Analysis, Ch.5 Proposition 1.2; UC Davis notes on Fourier transform properties

proof_steps.label: Setup
proof_steps.content: Let $f$ be integrable, or take $f\in\mathcal S(\mathbb R)$ for a safe proof. Use $$\widehat f(\xi)=\int_{\mathbb R}f(x)e^{-2\pi i x\xi}\,dx.$$

proof_steps.label: Translation rule
proof_steps.content: For $a\in\mathbb R$, compute with $u=x-a$: $$\begin{aligned} \widehat{f(\cdot-a)}(\xi)&=\int_{\mathbb R}f(x-a)e^{-2\pi i x\xi}\,dx\\ &=\int_{\mathbb R}f(u)e^{-2\pi i(u+a)\xi}\,du\\ &=e^{-2\pi ia\xi}\widehat f(\xi). \end{aligned}$$ Thus spatial translation changes phase in frequency.

proof_steps.label: Modulation rule
proof_steps.content: For $b\in\mathbb R$, compute directly: $$\begin{aligned} \widehat{e^{2\pi ibx}f(x)}(\xi)&=\int_{\mathbb R}e^{2\pi ibx}f(x)e^{-2\pi i x\xi}\,dx\\ &=\int_{\mathbb R}f(x)e^{-2\pi i x(\xi-b)}\,dx=\widehat f(\xi-b). \end{aligned}$$ Thus multiplying by a pure frequency shifts the Fourier transform.

items.label: Transform of $L^1$ is Continuous & Decays
items.statement: The Fourier transform of an integrable function is continuous and vanishes at infinity.
items.formal_statement: If $f\in L^1(\mathbb R)$, then $\hat f\in C_0(\mathbb R)$: it is continuous and $\hat f(\xi)\to0$ as $|\xi|\to\infty$.
items.definition: $$f\in L^1(\mathbb R)\Longrightarrow \widehat f\in C_0(\mathbb R),\qquad \lim_{|\xi|\to\infty}\widehat f(\xi)=0.$$
items.intuition: Integrability makes high-frequency oscillations average out. This is the Riemann-Lebesgue lemma on the line.
assumptions: The proof uses dominated convergence for continuity and $L^1$ approximation by step functions for decay.
items.ref: SteinŌĆōShakarchi: Fourier Analysis, Ch.5 Proposition 1.4; UC Davis notes on Fourier transform on $L^1$

metadata.source: SteinŌĆōShakarchi: Fourier Analysis, Ch.5 Proposition 1.4; UC Davis notes on Fourier transform on $L^1$

proof_steps.label: Setup
proof_steps.content: Let $f\in L^1(\mathbb R)$ and define $$\widehat f(\xi)=\int_{\mathbb R}f(x)e^{-2\pi i x\xi}\,dx.$$ The integral is absolutely convergent for every $\xi$, and $$|\widehat f(\xi)|\le\|f\|_1.$$

proof_steps.label: Continuity
proof_steps.content: If $\xi_k\to\xi$, then for every $x$, $$f(x)e^{-2\pi i x\xi_k}\to f(x)e^{-2\pi i x\xi}.$$ Also, $$|f(x)e^{-2\pi i x\xi_k}|=|f(x)|,$$ and $f\in L^1(\mathbb R)$. Dominated convergence gives $$\widehat f(\xi_k)\to\widehat f(\xi).$$ Hence $\widehat f$ is continuous.

proof_steps.label: Decay for simple intervals
proof_steps.content: For an interval indicator, direct integration gives $$\widehat{\mathbf 1_{[a,b]}}(\xi)=\int_a^b e^{-2\pi i x\xi}\,dx=\frac{e^{-2\pi ia\xi}-e^{-2\pi ib\xi}}{2\pi i\xi},\qquad \xi\ne0,$$ so $$\widehat{\mathbf 1_{[a,b]}}(\xi)\to0$$ as $|\xi|\to\infty$. By linearity, the same holds for finite linear combinations of interval indicators.

proof_steps.label: Approximate in \(L^1\)
proof_steps.content: Choose a simple interval step function $s$ with $$\|f-s\|_1<\varepsilon.$$ Then $$|\widehat f(\xi)|\le |\widehat{s}(\xi)|+|\widehat{f-s}(\xi)|\le |\widehat{s}(\xi)|+\|f-s\|_1.$$ Since $\widehat{s}(\xi)	o0$, for large $|\xi|$ we get $$|\widehat f(\xi)|<2\varepsilon.$$ Therefore $$\widehat f(\xi)\xrightarrow[|\xi|\to\infty]{}0.$$

proof_steps.label: Conclusion
proof_steps.content: The Fourier transform maps $L^1(\mathbb R)$ into continuous functions vanishing at infinity: $$f\in L^1(\mathbb R)\Longrightarrow \widehat f\in C_0(\mathbb R).$$ This is the real-line Riemann--Lebesgue lemma.

items.label: Band-Limited Function
items.statement: A function whose Fourier transform vanishes outside a bounded frequency band.
items.formal_statement: A function $f$ is band-limited to $[-W,W]$ if $\operatorname{supp}\hat f\subset[-W,W]$. Equivalently, no frequencies with $|\xi|>W$ are present.
items.intuition: Band-limited signals have finite frequency range. That frequency restriction makes exact sampling and analytic continuation possible.
assumptions: Support of $\hat f$ is understood in the appropriate sense: ordinary support for continuous transforms, essential support for $L^2$ transforms.

items.label: PaleyŌĆōWiener Theorem
items.statement: Compact frequency support corresponds to entire extension with controlled exponential growth.
items.formal_statement: For $f\in L^2(\mathbb R)$, $\operatorname{supp}\hat f\subset[-W,W]$ iff $f$ agrees a.e. on $\mathbb R$ with an entire function $F$ such that $F|_{\mathbb R}\in L^2(\mathbb R)$ and $|F(z)|\le C e^{2\pi W|\operatorname{Im}z|}$ in the appropriate $L^2$ Paley-Wiener sense.
items.formula: $$\begin{aligned} f\in L^2(\mathbb R),\ \operatorname{supp}\widehat f\subset[-W,W]&\Longleftrightarrow \exists F\in\mathcal O(\mathbb C):\ F|_{\mathbb R}=f\ \text{a.e.},\\ |F(z)|&\le Ce^{2\pi W|\operatorname{Im}z|},\\ F|_{\mathbb R}&\in L^2(\mathbb R). \end{aligned}$$
items.intuition: Cutting off frequency creates strong analytic structure in space. Band limitation is not just smoothness; it forces complex-analytic continuation.
assumptions: This is a schematic $L^2$ Paley-Wiener formulation; precise versions specify the exact growth and square-integrability condition.

items.label: Heat Kernel on R
items.statement: The Gaussian convolution kernel solving the heat equation on the real line.
items.formal_statement: For $t>0$, $H_t(x)=\frac1{\sqrt{4\pi t}}e^{-x^2/(4t)}$, $\hat H_t(\xi)=e^{-4\pi^2t\xi^2}$, and $u(x,t)=(H_t*f)(x)$ solves $u_t=u_{xx}$ with initial data $f$ in suitable senses.
items.intuition: Heat flow is Gaussian averaging. As $t\downarrow0$, $H_t$ concentrates near $0$; as $t$ grows, it spreads and smooths.
assumptions: The initial-data interpretation depends on the function space, such as $L^p$, $C_0$, or distributions.
items.ref: SteinŌĆōShakarchi: Ch.5 Corollary 1.5; Ch.5 Corollary 3.4; Ch.5 Theorem 3.3

metadata.source: SteinŌĆōShakarchi: Ch.5 Corollary 1.5; Ch.5 Corollary 3.4; Ch.5 Theorem 3.3

items.label: Test Function
items.statement: A smooth compactly supported function used to probe distributions.
items.formal_statement: A test function is $\varphi\in C_c^\infty(\mathbb R)$. The space of test functions is often denoted $\mathcal D(\mathbb R)$, and $C_c^\infty(\mathbb R)\subset\mathcal S(\mathbb R)$.
items.intuition: Test functions are controlled measuring instruments. A distribution is known by how it acts on every such probe.

items.label: Convolution of Distributions
items.statement: Convolution can be extended to distributions when the support hypotheses make the pairing well-defined.
items.formal_statement: For a distribution $T$ and test function $\varphi$, $(T*\varphi)(x)=T(\tau_x\tilde\varphi)$, where $\tilde\varphi(y)=\varphi(-y)$. More generally, $T*S$ is defined under suitable support conditions, and $\delta*T=T$.
items.intuition: Convolution with a test function smooths a distribution. This lets spikes and generalized sources interact with kernels in PDEs.
assumptions: Convolution of two arbitrary distributions is not always defined; support or regularity hypotheses are required.

items.label: Fundamental Solution
items.statement: A distribution $E$ with $LE=\delta$, used to solve linear equations by convolution.
items.formal_statement: For a linear constant-coefficient differential operator $L$, a fundamental solution is a distribution $E$ satisfying $LE=\delta$. If convolution is defined, then $u=E*f$ solves $Lu=f$.
items.intuition: A fundamental solution is the response to a point source. Once the point-source response is known, general forcing is obtained by superposition through convolution.
assumptions: The formula $u=E*f$ requires convolution to be defined and $L$ to commute with convolution in the intended setting.

items.label: Parseval for the DFT
items.statement: Energy is conserved by the DFT up to the chosen normalization factor.
items.formal_statement: With $\hat a(k)=\sum_{j=0}^{N-1}a_j\omega_N^{-jk}$, $\sum_{j=0}^{N-1}|a_j|^2=\frac1N\sum_{k=0}^{N-1}|\hat a(k)|^2$.
items.intuition: The DFT matrix is unitary after scaling by $1/\sqrt N$. This is finite-dimensional Plancherel.
assumptions: Depends on the DFT normalization convention.

items.label: Discrete Cosine Transform
items.statement: A cosine-only transform obtained from an even extension of finite data.
items.formal_statement: One common DCT-II convention is $C_k=\sum_{j=0}^{N-1}a_j\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi}{N}(j+\frac12)k\right)$, $k=0,\dots,N-1$. It can be viewed as a DFT of a symmetrically extended sequence.
items.intuition: Even symmetry removes sine components. The DCT often concentrates signal energy into few low-frequency coefficients, which is why it is useful in compression.
assumptions: Several DCT normalizations and types exist; this item states the common DCT-II convention.

items.label: Separation of Variables
items.statement: A method for solving linear PDEs by expanding in spatial eigenfunctions.
items.formal_statement: Seek $u(x,t)=\sum_n c_n(t)e_n(x)$, where $e_n$ are eigenfunctions of the spatial operator. Orthogonality decouples the PDE into ODEs for the coefficients $c_n(t)$.
items.intuition: The method chooses coordinates that match the operator. In those coordinates, the PDE splits into independent one-frequency motions.
assumptions: Requires a suitable eigenfunction expansion for the spatial problem.

items.label: Dirichlet Problem on the Disk
items.statement: The Poisson kernel gives the harmonic extension of boundary data on the disk.
items.formal_statement: If $f\in C(\mathbb T)$, then $u(r,\theta)=(P_r*f)(\theta)$ is harmonic for $0\le r<1$ and $u(r,\theta)\to f(\theta)$ as $r\to1^-$.
items.intuition: Boundary values are averaged with a kernel that concentrates near the boundary point as one approaches the circle.
assumptions: Boundary convergence is stated for continuous boundary data; weaker data require weaker convergence modes.

items.label: Theta Function & Functional Equation
items.statement: Poisson summation applied to the Gaussian gives the modular identity for the theta function.
items.formal_statement: For $t>0$, $\theta(t)=\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}e^{-\pi n^2t}$ satisfies $\theta(1/t)=\sqrt t\,\theta(t)$.
items.intuition: The Gaussian is essentially fixed by the Fourier transform, so Poisson summation turns a sum at scale $t$ into a sum at reciprocal scale $1/t$.
assumptions: Uses the $2\pi$-Fourier convention where $e^{-\pi x^2}$ is self-dual.

items.label: Free Schr├Čdinger Equation
items.statement: The free Schr├Čdinger equation is solved by multiplying each Fourier mode by a dispersive phase.
items.formal_statement: For $iu_t=-u_{xx}$ on $\mathbb R$ with initial data $u_0$, the Fourier transform satisfies $\hat u(\xi,t)=e^{-4\pi^2 i\xi^2t}\hat u_0(\xi)$.
items.intuition: The Fourier transform turns the spatial second derivative into multiplication by $-4\pi^2\xi^2$. Unlike heat flow, the multiplier has modulus one, so waves disperse rather than decay.
assumptions: Uses the Fourier convention $e^{-2\pi ix\xi}$. Regularity assumptions depend on whether one wants classical, $L^2$, or distributional solutions.

items.label: Fraunhofer Diffraction
items.statement: In the Fraunhofer far-field approximation, the diffraction amplitude is essentially the Fourier transform of the aperture.
items.formal_statement: For an aperture indicator $\mathbf 1_A$ in the aperture plane, the far-field amplitude is proportional to $\widehat{\mathbf 1_A}(\xi)$, and the observed intensity is proportional to $|\widehat{\mathbf 1_A}(\xi)|^2$.
items.intuition: A lens or far-field propagation physically separates spatial frequencies. A slit produces a sinc pattern; a circular aperture produces an Airy-type pattern.
assumptions: This is the scalar Fraunhofer approximation, not a full electromagnetic model.

items.label: Weierstrass Function (Nowhere Differentiable)
items.statement: A continuous nowhere-differentiable periodic function can be built from a lacunary trigonometric series.
items.formal_statement: A classical example is $W(x)=\sum_{n=0}^\infty a^n\cos(b^n x)$, where $0<a<1$, $b$ is an odd integer, and suitable conditions such as $ab>1+3\pi/2$ guarantee that $W$ is continuous and nowhere differentiable.
items.intuition: The coefficients decay enough to give continuity, but the rapidly separated frequencies keep injecting small sharp oscillations at every scale.
assumptions: The displayed condition is a classical sufficient condition, not the sharpest possible theorem.
items.ref: SteinŌĆōShakarchi: Ch.4 Lemma 3.2; Ch.4 Lemma 3.3; Ch.4 Theorem 3.1

metadata.source: SteinŌĆōShakarchi: Ch.4 Lemma 3.2; Ch.4 Lemma 3.3; Ch.4 Theorem 3.1

items.label: Poisson Kernel on the Half-Plane
items.statement: The Poisson kernel solving the Dirichlet problem on the upper half-plane.
items.formal_statement: For $y>0$, $P_y(x)=\frac1\pi\frac{y}{x^2+y^2}$, $\hat P_y(\xi)=e^{-2\pi y|\xi|}$, and $u(x,y)=(f*P_y)(x)$ is harmonic in $\mathbb R^2_+$ with boundary trace $f$ under suitable hypotheses.
items.intuition: It is the real-line version of harmonic extension: moving upward into the half-plane smooths boundary data by a scale $y$.
assumptions: Boundary convergence depends on the function space of $f$.
items.ref: SteinŌĆōShakarchi: Ch.5 Lemma 2.4; Ch.5 Lemma 2.5; Ch.5 Lemma 2.8; Ch.5 Theorem 2.6; Ch.5 Theorem 2.7; Ch.5 Theorem 3.5

metadata.source: SteinŌĆōShakarchi: Ch.5 Lemma 2.4; Ch.5 Lemma 2.5; Ch.5 Lemma 2.8; Ch.5 Theorem 2.6; Ch.5 Theorem 2.7; Ch.5 Theorem 3.5

items.label: Wave Equation in $\mathbb R^d$ (Huygens, Kirchhoff)
items.statement: The multidimensional wave equation is solved in frequency by independent oscillators.
items.formal_statement: For $\partial_t^2u=\Delta u$ with $u(\cdot,0)=f$, $u_t(\cdot,0)=g$, the Fourier transform gives $\hat u(\xi,t)=\cos(2\pi|\xi|t)\hat f(\xi)+\frac{\sin(2\pi|\xi|t)}{2\pi|\xi|}\hat g(\xi)$, with the second term interpreted by its limit at $\xi=0$.
items.intuition: Each frequency oscillates at speed proportional to $|\xi|$. Special odd-dimensional formulas reveal Huygens' principle, where waves propagate sharply without a trailing wake.
assumptions: Formula uses the $2\pi$-frequency convention and requires suitable data or distributional interpretation.
items.ref: SteinŌĆōShakarchi: Ch.6 Lemma 3.3; Ch.6 Lemma 3.4; Ch.6 Lemma 3.5; Ch.6 Theorem 2.4; Ch.6 Theorem 3.1; Ch.6 Theorem 3.2; Ch.6 Theorem 3.6; Ch.6 Theorem 3.7

metadata.source: SteinŌĆōShakarchi: Ch.6 Lemma 3.3; Ch.6 Lemma 3.4; Ch.6 Lemma 3.5; Ch.6 Theorem 2.4; Ch.6 Theorem 3.1; Ch.6 Theorem 3.2; Ch.6 Theorem 3.6; Ch.6 Theorem 3.7

items.label: Radon Transform
items.statement: The Radon transform reconstructs a function from its integrals over hyperplanes.
items.formal_statement: For suitable $f$ on $\mathbb R^d$, $Rf(t,\gamma)=\int_{x\cdot\gamma=t}f(x)\,d\sigma(x)$. The Fourier slice theorem says the one-dimensional Fourier transform in $t$ satisfies $\mathcal F_t(Rf)(s,\gamma)=\hat f(s\gamma)$.
items.intuition: Each X-ray projection reveals one slice of the Fourier transform. Combining slices over many directions reconstructs the object.
assumptions: Requires suitable decay or integrability so the hyperplane integrals and transforms are defined.
items.ref: SteinŌĆōShakarchi: Ch.6 Corollary 5.3; Ch.6 Lemma 5.2; Ch.6 Proposition 5.1; Ch.6 Theorem 5.4

metadata.source: SteinŌĆōShakarchi: Ch.6 Corollary 5.3; Ch.6 Lemma 5.2; Ch.6 Proposition 5.1; Ch.6 Theorem 5.4

items.label: Dual Group
items.statement: The group of all characters of a finite abelian group.
items.formal_statement: For a finite abelian group $G$, its dual group is $\widehat G=\operatorname{Hom}(G,\mathbb C^\times)$, with pointwise multiplication. The group $\widehat G$ is finite abelian and is isomorphic to $G$, though not canonically in general.
items.intuition: The dual group is the frequency side of $G$. Its elements are the waves that detect structure in functions on $G$.
assumptions: For finite groups, characters take values among roots of unity.

items.label: Characters of Finite Abelian Groups
items.statement: Characters form an orthonormal basis for functions on a finite abelian group.
items.formal_statement: Let $G$ be a finite abelian group and use $\langle f,g\rangle=\frac1{|G|}\sum_{x\in G}f(x)\overline{g(x)}$. Then the characters $\chi\in\widehat G$ form an orthonormal basis of $L^2(G)$. Hence $\hat f(\chi)=\frac1{|G|}\sum_{x\in G}f(x)\overline{\chi(x)}$ and $f(x)=\sum_{\chi\in\widehat G}\hat f(\chi)\chi(x)$.
items.intuition: Every finite abelian group has enough pure frequencies to decompose every function on it. The cyclic DFT is just the special case $G=\mathbb Z/N\mathbb Z$.
assumptions: The group is finite and abelian. Nonabelian Fourier analysis requires representations, not just scalar characters.
items.ref: SteinŌĆōShakarchi: Ch.7 Lemma 2.2; Ch.7 Lemma 2.4; Ch.7 Lemma 2.6; Ch.7 Theorem 2.3; Ch.7 Theorem 2.5; Ch.7 Theorem 2.7; Ch.7 Theorem 2.8

metadata.source: SteinŌĆōShakarchi: Ch.7 Lemma 2.2; Ch.7 Lemma 2.4; Ch.7 Lemma 2.6; Ch.7 Theorem 2.3; Ch.7 Theorem 2.5; Ch.7 Theorem 2.7; Ch.7 Theorem 2.8

items.label: Euclidean Algorithm & B├®zout
items.statement: The Euclidean algorithm computes greatest common divisors and gives B├®zout identities.
items.formal_statement: For integers $a,b$ with $b>0$, there are unique integers $q,r$ such that $a=qb+r$ and $0\le r<b$. Iterating division gives $\gcd(a,b)$, and there exist integers $m,n$ such that $\gcd(a,b)=ma+nb$.
items.formula: $$\begin{aligned} a,b&\in\mathbb Z,\\ b>0&\Longrightarrow \exists q,r\in\mathbb Z:\ a=qb+r,\quad 0\le r<b,\\ \gcd(a,b)&=ma+nb\quad(m,n\in\mathbb Z). \end{aligned}$$
items.intuition: Repeatedly subtract the largest possible multiple until no remainder remains. Running the steps backward expresses the gcd as an integer linear combination.
assumptions: Usually stated for integers $a,b$, not both zero.
items.ref: SteinŌĆōShakarchi: Ch.8 Theorem 1.1; Ch.8 Theorem 1.2; Ch.8 Corollary 1.3; Ch.8 Corollary 1.4

metadata.source: SteinŌĆōShakarchi: Ch.8 Theorem 1.1; Ch.8 Theorem 1.2; Ch.8 Corollary 1.3; Ch.8 Corollary 1.4

items.label: Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic
items.statement: Every integer greater than $1$ factors uniquely into primes.
items.formal_statement: For every integer $n>1$, there exist primes $p_1<\cdots<p_k$ and positive integers $\alpha_1,\dots,\alpha_k$ such that $n=p_1^{\alpha_1}\cdots p_k^{\alpha_k}$. This factorization is unique.
items.intuition: Primes are the multiplicative atoms of the integers. Unique factorization lets analytic sums over integers become products over primes.
assumptions: Units and ordering are ignored: uniqueness means the same primes occur with the same exponents.

items.label: Infinitude of Primes
items.statement: There are infinitely many primes.
items.formal_statement: If $p_1,\dots,p_k$ are finitely many primes, then any prime divisor of $p_1\cdots p_k+1$ is not among $p_1,\dots,p_k$. Hence no finite list contains all primes.
items.intuition: Any alleged complete list of primes manufactures a new number that escapes divisibility by every prime on the list.

items.label: Euler Product for the Zeta Function
items.statement: The zeta function factors into local contributions from each prime.
items.formal_statement: For $\operatorname{Re}s>1$, $\zeta(s)=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}n^{-s}=\prod_{p}(1-p^{-s})^{-1}$, with absolute convergence justifying the rearrangement.
items.intuition: Unique factorization turns the sum over all integers into independent geometric series over primes. This is where arithmetic becomes analysis.
assumptions: Initially valid only in the half-plane $\operatorname{Re}s>1$.
items.ref: SteinŌĆōShakarchi: Ch.8 Lemma 1.8; Ch.8 Proposition 1.9; Ch.8 Theorem 1.10; Ch.8 Proposition 3.2

metadata.source: SteinŌĆōShakarchi: Ch.8 Lemma 1.8; Ch.8 Proposition 1.9; Ch.8 Theorem 1.10; Ch.8 Proposition 3.2

items.label: Divergence of the Prime Sum
items.statement: The reciprocal sum over the primes diverges.
items.formal_statement: $\sum_p\frac1p=\infty$. One proof takes logarithms of the Euler product and compares $\log\zeta(s)$ as $s\to1^+$.
items.intuition: There are not merely infinitely many primes; they are numerous enough that their reciprocal weights still accumulate without bound.
assumptions: The theorem is stronger than EuclidŌĆÖs infinitude of primes.

items.label: Residue Class Modulo q
items.statement: Integers are grouped by having the same remainder modulo $q$.
items.formal_statement: For $q\ge2$, $a\equiv b\pmod q$ means $q\mid(a-b)$. The residue class of $a$ is $[a]_q=\{n\in\mathbb Z:n\equiv a\pmod q\}$.
items.definition: $$a\equiv b\pmod q\Longleftrightarrow q\mid(a-b),\qquad [a]_q=\{n\in\mathbb Z:n\equiv a\pmod q\}.$$
items.intuition: A residue class is one lane in modular arithmetic. DirichletŌĆÖs theorem asks whether each reduced lane contains infinitely many primes.

items.label: Euler Phi Function
items.statement: EulerŌĆÖs phi function counts the invertible residue classes modulo $q$.
items.formal_statement: $\varphi(q)=| (\mathbb Z/q\mathbb Z)^\times |=|\{1\le a\le q:\gcd(a,q)=1\}|$.
items.intuition: The number $\varphi(q)$ is the size of the frequency system used by Dirichlet characters modulo $q$.

items.label: Riemann Zeta Function
items.statement: The Dirichlet series whose Euler product encodes the primes.
items.formal_statement: For $\operatorname{Re}s>1$, $\zeta(s)=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}n^{-s}$. It has Euler product $\zeta(s)=\prod_p(1-p^{-s})^{-1}$.
items.intuition: The zeta function packages all positive integers into one analytic object; unique factorization then rewrites it as a product over primes.
assumptions: The displayed Dirichlet series and Euler product are valid for $\operatorname{Re}s>1$.

items.label: Dirichlet Character
items.statement: A Dirichlet character is a group character modulo $q$, extended by zero on non-coprime integers.
items.formal_statement: Modulo $q$, a Dirichlet character $\chi:\mathbb Z\to\mathbb C$ is $q$-periodic, completely multiplicative, agrees with a character on $(\mathbb Z/q\mathbb Z)^\times$, and satisfies $\chi(n)=0$ when $\gcd(n,q)>1$. For $\gcd(a,q)=1$, $\frac1{\varphi(q)}\sum_{\chi\bmod q}\overline{\chi(a)}\chi(n)$ detects the reduced residue class $n\equiv a\pmod q$.
items.intuition: Dirichlet characters are multiplicative Fourier modes for reduced residue classes. They convert congruence conditions into character sums.
assumptions: Characters are extended by zero away from integers coprime to $q$.

items.label: Dirichlet L-Function
items.statement: A Dirichlet L-function is the zeta function twisted by a Dirichlet character.
items.formal_statement: For $\operatorname{Re}s>1$, $L(s,\chi)=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\chi(n)n^{-s}=\prod_p(1-\chi(p)p^{-s})^{-1}$. For a nonprincipal character, $L(s,\chi)$ has a finite value at $s=1$ by analytic continuation or Abel limiting.
items.intuition: The character twist filters integers, and especially primes, by residue class. The behavior near $s=1$ is the analytic lever in DirichletŌĆÖs theorem.
assumptions: Euler product identity is initially valid for $\operatorname{Re}s>1$. The principal character gives a zeta-like pole at $s=1$.
items.ref: SteinŌĆōShakarchi: Ch.8 Theorem 2.4; Ch.8 Proposition 3.1; Ch.8 Proposition 3.4; Ch.8 Lemma 3.5; Ch.8 Proposition 3.6

metadata.source: SteinŌĆōShakarchi: Ch.8 Theorem 2.4; Ch.8 Proposition 3.1; Ch.8 Proposition 3.4; Ch.8 Lemma 3.5; Ch.8 Proposition 3.6

items.label: Non-Vanishing of $L(1,\chi)$
items.statement: Nonprincipal Dirichlet L-functions do not vanish at $s=1$.
items.formal_statement: If $\chi$ is a nonprincipal Dirichlet character modulo $q$, then $L(1,\chi)\ne0$.
items.intuition: The character decomposition of residue classes would fail to force primes in every class if these values could vanish. Non-vanishing blocks that cancellation.
assumptions: The principal character is excluded; its $L$-function has a pole at $s=1$, not a finite nonzero value.
items.ref: SteinŌĆōShakarchi: Ch.8 Theorem 3.7; Ch.8 Theorem 2.3; Ch.8 Lemma 3.8; Ch.8 Lemma 3.9; Ch.8 Proposition 3.10; Ch.8 Proposition 3.11; Ch.8 Proposition 3.13; Ch.8 Theorem 3.12; Ch.8 Lemma 3.14; Ch.8 Lemma 3.15

items.label: Essential Supremum
items.statement: The almost-everywhere version of a maximum size.
items.formal_statement: For a measurable function $f$, $\operatorname*{ess\,sup}|f|$ is the least $M\in[0,\infty]$ such that $|f(x)|\le M$ for almost every $x$.
items.intuition: It ignores values on sets of measure zero, so it is the correct maximum-size notion for $L^\infty$.

items.label: $L^1$ on $\mathbb R$
items.statement: Absolutely integrable functions on the real line.
items.formal_statement: $L^1(\mathbb R)=\{f:\int_{\mathbb R}|f(x)|\,dx<\infty\}$, with functions identified if they agree almost everywhere.
items.definition: $$\begin{aligned} L^1(\mathbb R)&=\left\{f:\mathbb R\to\mathbb C\ \text{measurable}:\int_{\mathbb R}|f(x)|\,dx<\infty\right\}/\sim,\\ f\sim g&\Longleftrightarrow f=g\ \text{a.e.} \end{aligned}$$
items.intuition: This is the natural minimal setting where the Fourier transform integral is defined as an absolutely convergent integral.
assumptions: Functions are identified up to equality almost everywhere.

items.label: $L^2$ on $\mathbb R$
items.statement: Square-integrable functions on the real line.
items.formal_statement: $L^2(\mathbb R)=\{f:\int_{\mathbb R}|f(x)|^2\,dx<\infty\}$, modulo equality almost everywhere, with inner product $\langle f,g\rangle=\int_{\mathbb R}f(x)\overline{g(x)}\,dx$.
items.definition: $$\begin{aligned} L^2(\mathbb R)&=\left\{f:\mathbb R\to\mathbb C\ \text{measurable}:\int_{\mathbb R}|f(x)|^2\,dx<\infty\right\}/\sim,\\ f\sim g&\Longleftrightarrow f=g\ \text{a.e.},\\ \langle f,g\rangle&=\int_{\mathbb R}f(x)\overline{g(x)}\,dx. \end{aligned}$$
items.intuition: This is the energy space for the Fourier transform. Plancherel says the transform preserves this energy exactly.
assumptions: Functions are identified up to equality almost everywhere.

items.label: Support
items.statement: The region where a function or distribution is nonzero.
items.formal_statement: For a continuous function, $\operatorname{supp}f=\overline{\{x:f(x)\ne0\}}$. For measurable functions, support is usually understood up to almost-everywhere changes.
items.intuition: Support tells where an object lives. Frequency support tells which waves are present.

items.label: Sinc Function
items.statement: The interpolation kernel in the sampling theorem.
items.formal_statement: $\operatorname{sinc}(t)=\frac{\sin(\pi t)}{\pi t}$ for $t\ne0$, with $\operatorname{sinc}(0)=1$.
items.intuition: Sinc is the ideal reconstruction pulse: it equals one at its own sample point and zero at the other integer sample points.
assumptions: This graph uses the normalized sinc convention $\operatorname{sinc}(t)=\sin(\pi t)/(\pi t)$.

items.label: Entire Function
items.statement: A complex function holomorphic on the whole complex plane.
items.formal_statement: A function $F:\mathbb C\to\mathbb C$ is entire if it is complex-differentiable at every point of $\mathbb C$.
items.intuition: Entire functions are globally analytic. Paley-Wiener connects compact frequency support with this strong complex-analytic regularity.
assumptions: This is a complex-analysis prerequisite used only where analytic continuation matters.

items.label: Fourier Transform Convention
items.statement: The sign and 2ŽĆ-normalization used for the Fourier transform.
items.formal_statement: $\hat f(\xi)=\int_{\mathbb R}f(x)e^{-2\pi i x\xi}\,dx$, with inverse $f(x)=\int_{\mathbb R}\hat f(\xi)e^{2\pi i x\xi}\,d\xi$ when inversion is valid.
items.definition: $$\widehat f(\xi)=\int_{\mathbb R}f(x)e^{-2\pi ix\xi}\,dx,\qquad f(x)=\int_{\mathbb R}\widehat f(\xi)e^{2\pi ix\xi}\,d\xi.$$
items.intuition: Fourier constants are bookkeeping with teeth. Fixing the convention prevents sign and 2ŽĆ errors across inversion, Plancherel, PDEs, and uncertainty.

items.label: Variance and Standard Deviation
items.statement: The centered second moment measuring spread.
items.formal_statement: For a normalized density $\rho$, $\mu=\int x\rho(x)\,dx$ and $\Delta_x^2=\int (x-\mu)^2\rho(x)\,dx$. In uncertainty estimates, $\rho(x)=|f(x)|^2/\|f\|_2^2$.
items.intuition: The uncertainty principle is about spread around the center of mass, not just raw distance from zero.

items.label: Moderate Decrease Class $\mathcal M(\mathbb R)$
items.statement: Continuous functions on $\mathbb R$ bounded by a constant multiple of $\frac{1}{1+x^2}$; Stein--Shakarchi's basic decay class for defining improper integrals and Fourier transforms on the real line.
items.formal_statement: A continuous function $f:\mathbb R\to\mathbb C$ has \textbf{moderate decrease} if there exists $A>0$ such that $|f(x)|\le \dfrac{A}{1+x^2}$ for every $x\in\mathbb R$. The class is denoted $\mathcal M(\mathbb R)$. In course notation, this is the same decay class as $C_2(\mathbb R)$ when $C_2$ is defined using the bound $(1+|x|)^{-2}$.
items.definition: $$\mathcal M(\mathbb R)=\left\{f\in C(\mathbb R):\exists A>0\ \text{s.t.}\ |f(x)|\le \frac{A}{1+x^2}\ \forall x\in\mathbb R\right\}.$$
items.notation: $$\mathcal M(\mathbb R),\qquad C_2(\mathbb R)\ \text{(equivalent course notation)}$$
items.intuition: Moderate decrease is a concrete tail condition: the function may live on all of $\mathbb R$, but far from the origin its size is dominated by a multiple of $1/x^2$. This is strong enough to make the improper integral and the Fourier transform integral well-defined in Stein--Shakarchi's elementary setup, while still allowing many non-smooth functions. The point is not smoothness; the point is that the tails are small enough not to leak infinite mass.
assumptions: $$\mathcal M(\mathbb R)\subset L^1(\mathbb R)\cap L^2(\mathbb R),\qquad (1+x^2)^{-1}\in L^1(\mathbb R)\cap L^2(\mathbb R).$$
$$1+x^2\le (1+|x|)^2\le 2(1+x^2)\quad\Longrightarrow\quad \mathcal M(\mathbb R)=C_2(\mathbb R)\ \text{as sets, up to equivalent constants}.$$

items.label: Polynomial Decay Class $C_m(\mathbb R^d)$
items.statement: For $m>0$, the class $C_m(\mathbb R^d)$ consists of continuous functions whose magnitude is bounded by a constant multiple of $(1+|x|)^{-m}$.
items.formal_statement: Let $d\ge 1$ and $m>0$. The polynomial decay class $C_m(\mathbb R^d)$ consists of continuous functions $f:\mathbb R^d\to\mathbb C$ such that there exists $A>0$ with $|f(x)|\le A(1+|x|)^{-m}$ for every $x\in\mathbb R^d$.
items.definition: $$C_m(\mathbb R^d)=\left\{f\in C(\mathbb R^d):\exists A>0\ \text{s.t.}\ |f(x)|\le \frac{A}{(1+|x|)^m}\ \forall x\in\mathbb R^d\right\}.$$
items.intuition: The class $C_m(\mathbb R^d)$ measures polynomial tail decay. Larger $m$ means the function must fade faster at infinity. The class is useful because it gives quick thresholds for integrability: in $\mathbb R^d$, decay faster than $|x|^{-d}$ gives $L^1$ control, and decay faster than $|x|^{-d/2}$ gives $L^2$ control. So $C_m(\mathbb R^d)$ is a bookkeeping device for how much tail mass a function is allowed to carry, because apparently infinity was not already inconvenient enough.
assumptions: $$m_2\ge m_1>0\quad\Longrightarrow\quad C_{m_2}(\mathbb R^d)\subset C_{m_1}(\mathbb R^d).$$
$$m>0\quad\Longrightarrow\quad C_m(\mathbb R^d)\subset C_0(\mathbb R^d),\quad \text{where }C_0(\mathbb R^d)\text{ denotes continuous functions vanishing at infinity}.$$
$$m>d\quad\Longrightarrow\quad C_m(\mathbb R^d)\subset L^1(\mathbb R^d).$$
$$2m>d\quad\Longrightarrow\quad C_m(\mathbb R^d)\subset L^2(\mathbb R^d).$$
$$\mathcal M(\mathbb R)=C_2(\mathbb R)\ \text{when }C_2(\mathbb R)\text{ is defined using }(1+|x|)^{-2}\text{ decay}.$$
items.ref: Course notation for polynomial decay classes; compare Stein--Shakarchi Ch.5 ┬¦1.1 functions of moderate decrease.

metadata.source: Course notation; Stein--Shakarchi Ch.5 ┬¦1.1 for the corresponding $d=1$, $m=2$ moderate decrease class

items.label: Heat Kernel on $\mathbb R^d$
items.statement: The Gaussian convolution kernel that solves the heat equation in $\mathbb R^d$; an approximate identity as $t\to0^+$.
items.formal_statement: For $t>0$, $\mathcal H_t^{(d)}(x)=(4\pi t)^{-d/2}e^{-|x|^2/(4t)}$ with $\widehat{\mathcal H_t^{(d)}}(\xi)=e^{-4\pi^2 t|\xi|^2}$. Then $u(x,t)=(\mathcal H_t^{(d)}*f)(x)$ solves $\partial_t u=\Delta u$ with $u(\cdot,t)\to f$ as $t\to0^+$.
items.intuition: A product of one-dimensional Gaussians, one per coordinate; total mass $1$, concentrating at the origin as $t\downarrow0$.
assumptions: Separability: $\mathcal H_t^{(d)}(x)=\prod_{j=1}^d \mathcal H_t^{(1)}(x_j)$, so all one-dimensional identities tensor up.
items.ref: SteinŌĆōShakarchi: Ch.6 ┬¦2 (the heat kernel in R^d)

metadata.source: SteinŌĆōShakarchi: Ch.6 ┬¦2 (the heat kernel in R^d)

items.label: Absolute Coefficients Ō¤╣ Uniform Convergence
items.statement: If the Fourier coefficients are absolutely summable, the Fourier series converges absolutely and uniformly; for continuous $f$ the limit is $f$.
items.formal_statement: If $\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}|\hat f(n)|<\infty$ then $\sum_n \hat f(n)e^{inx}$ converges absolutely and uniformly to a continuous limit $g$. If moreover $f\in C(\mathbb T)$ (or $f$ is continuous), then $g=f$, so $S_N[f]\to f$ uniformly.
items.formula: $$\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}|\hat f(n)|<\infty\ \Longrightarrow\ S_N[f]\rightrightarrows g\in C(\mathbb T),\quad\text{and}\quad f\in C(\mathbb T)\Rightarrow g=f.$$
items.intuition: The Weierstrass M-test: bound each harmonic by $|\hat f(n)|$; a summable bound forces uniform convergence. Uniqueness pins the limit to $f$.
items.ref: SteinŌĆōShakarchi: Ch.2 ┬¦1, Ch.2 Cor. 2.3ŌĆō2.4 (Weierstrass M-test + uniqueness)

metadata.source: SteinŌĆōShakarchi: Ch.2 ┬¦1, Ch.2 Cor. 2.3ŌĆō2.4 (Weierstrass M-test + uniqueness)

items.label: Lacunary Function / Series
items.statement: A power series or Fourier series whose nonzero terms occur only at a sparse sequence of exponents or frequencies.
items.formal_statement: A lacunary series is a series whose active exponents or frequencies form a strictly increasing sequence with large gaps. In the standard Hadamard-gap case, the frequencies satisfy $$\lambda_1<\lambda_2<\cdots,\qquad \frac{\lambda_{k+1}}{\lambda_k}\ge q>1.$$ For Fourier analysis, the typical form is $$\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} a_k e^{i\lambda_k x}$$ or its real trigonometric version.
items.intuition: A lacunary series is a series with most of its possible terms missing. Instead of using every frequency, it jumps through a sparse list such as $$1,2,4,8,16,\ldots$$. These gaps make the series behave less like an ordinary Fourier series and more like a sum of weakly independent oscillations. In complex analysis, the gaps can force a natural boundary; in Fourier analysis, they strongly affect almost everywhere convergence and probabilistic behavior.
items.notation: $$\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} a_k z^{\lambda_k},\qquad \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} a_k e^{i\lambda_k x},\qquad \lambda_{k+1}/\lambda_k\ge q>1.$$
assumptions: $$\lambda_k\in\mathbb N,\qquad \lambda_1<\lambda_2<\cdots.$$
$$\exists q>1\ \text{ such that }\ \lambda_{k+1}/\lambda_k\ge q\ \text{ for all }k.$$

metadata.warning: In Fourier analysis, the more precise label is usually lacunary series rather than lacunary function.

items.label: Exam 2025 ┬Ę 1a ŌĆö Triangular-wave coefficients
items.statement: Compute the Fourier coefficients of the triangular wave $f(x)=1-|x|/\pi$ on $[-\pi,\pi]$.
items.formal_statement: Let $f$ be $2\pi$-periodic with $f(x)=1-\frac{|x|}{\pi}$ for $x\in[-\pi,\pi]$. Show that for $n\neq0$, $\ \hat f(n)=\frac{2}{\pi^2 n^2}$ if $n$ is odd and $\hat f(n)=0$ if $n$ is even.
items.intuition: Even + continuous + a single corner per half-period gives real coefficients decaying like $1/n^2$, with even harmonics annihilated by the symmetry $f(x+\pi)$.

solution_steps.label: Argument
solution_steps.content:
$f$ is the \textbf{triangular wave}: even, continuous (note $f(\pm\pi)=0$, so the periodic extension has no jumps), piecewise linear with corners at integer multiples of $\pi$. Because $f$ is even and real, $\hat f(n)=\hat f(-n)\in\mathbb R$ and the sine part of $e^{-inx}$ drops out:
$$\hat f(n)=\frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}\Big(1-\tfrac{|x|}{\pi}\Big)\cos(nx)\,dx=\frac1\pi\int_0^\pi\Big(1-\tfrac{x}{\pi}\Big)\cos(nx)\,dx,$$
where evenness folds $[-\pi,\pi]$ onto $[0,\pi]$. For $n\neq0$, $\int_0^\pi\cos(nx)\,dx=\frac{\sin(n\pi)}{n}=0$, and integration by parts gives
$$\int_0^\pi x\cos(nx)\,dx=\Big[\tfrac{x\sin(nx)}{n}\Big]_0^\pi-\frac1n\int_0^\pi\sin(nx)\,dx=\frac{1}{n^2}\big[\cos(nx)\big]_0^\pi=\frac{(-1)^n-1}{n^2}.$$
Hence
$$\hat f(n)=\frac1\pi\Big(0-\frac1\pi\cdot\frac{(-1)^n-1}{n^2}\Big)=\frac{1}{\pi^2}\cdot\frac{1-(-1)^n}{n^2}.$$
For $n$ even, $1-(-1)^n=0\Rightarrow\hat f(n)=0$; for $n$ odd, $1-(-1)^n=2\Rightarrow\hat f(n)=\dfrac{2}{\pi^2 n^2}$.

solution_steps.label: Remark
solution_steps.content: The mean value is $\hat f(0)=\frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^\pi(1-|x|/\pi)\,dx=\tfrac12$ ŌĆö the average height of the triangle (used in 1c).

items.label: Exam 2025 ┬Ę 1b ŌĆö Uniform convergence
items.statement: Prove the Fourier series of the triangular wave converges uniformly to $f$.
items.formal_statement: With $f$ and $\hat f$ as in 1a, prove that the Fourier series of $f$ converges uniformly to $f$.
items.intuition: Continuity + a single corner Ō¤╣ $1/n^2$ decay Ō¤╣ absolutely summable coefficients Ō¤╣ uniform convergence; uniqueness fixes the limit at $f$.

solution_steps.label: Argument
solution_steps.content:
From 1a, $|\hat f(n)|\le \dfrac{2}{\pi^2 n^2}$ for $n\neq0$, so the coefficients are \textbf{absolutely summable}:
$$\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}|\hat f(n)|=\tfrac12+\frac{4}{\pi^2}\sum_{\substack{n\ge1\\ n\ \mathrm{odd}}}\frac1{n^2}<\infty$$
by comparison with $\sum 1/n^2$. Since $|\hat f(n)e^{inx}|=|\hat f(n)|$, the \textbf{Weierstrass M-test} makes
$$g(x):=\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}\hat f(n)e^{inx}$$
converge absolutely and uniformly on $\mathbb R$; a uniform limit of continuous trigonometric polynomials is continuous, so $g\in C(\mathbb T)$.

solution_steps.label: Conclusion
solution_steps.content:
To identify $g=f$: uniform convergence lets us integrate term by term, so for every $m$,
$$\hat g(m)=\frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^\pi g(x)e^{-imx}\,dx=\sum_n\hat f(n)\cdot\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^\pi e^{i(n-m)x}\,dx=\hat f(m).$$
Thus $f$ and $g$ are both continuous with the same Fourier coefficients; by the uniqueness theorem (applied to $f-g$), $f\equiv g$. Therefore $S_N[f]\to f$ uniformly.

items.label: Exam 2025 ┬Ę 1c ŌĆö Sum $1/(2n+1)^4 = \pi^4/96$
items.statement: Use Parseval to prove $\sum_{n\ge0}1/(2n+1)^4=\pi^4/96$.
items.formal_statement: With $f$ as in 1a, prove $\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^\infty \frac{1}{(2n+1)^4}=\frac{\pi^4}{96}$.
items.intuition: Parseval converts an integral of $f^2$ (easy: $1/3$) into a sum over coefficients; only odd harmonics survive, isolating the odd-index zeta value.

solution_steps.label: Argument
solution_steps.content: Apply \textbf{Parseval's identity} $\sum_{n}|\hat f(n)|^2=\frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^\pi|f|^2$.

solution_steps.label: Right side
solution_steps.content:
Using evenness and $u=1-x/\pi$,
$$\frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^\pi\Big(1-\tfrac{|x|}\pi\Big)^2dx=\frac1\pi\int_0^\pi\Big(1-\tfrac{x}\pi\Big)^2dx=\frac1\pi\cdot\pi\int_0^1u^2\,du=\frac13.$$

solution_steps.label: Left side
solution_steps.content:
With $\hat f(0)=\tfrac12$ and $\hat f(\pm n)=\tfrac{2}{\pi^2n^2}$ for odd $n$ (zero for even $n\neq0$),
$$\sum_n|\hat f(n)|^2=\frac14+2\sum_{\substack{n\ge1\\ n\ \mathrm{odd}}}\Big(\frac{2}{\pi^2n^2}\Big)^2=\frac14+\frac{8}{\pi^4}\sum_{k=0}^\infty\frac{1}{(2k+1)^4}.$$

solution_steps.label: Step 4
solution_steps.content:
Equating, $\frac14+\frac{8}{\pi^4}S=\frac13$ with $S=\sum_{k\ge0}(2k+1)^{-4}$, so $\frac{8}{\pi^4}S=\frac1{12}$ and
$$\boxed{\ \sum_{n=0}^\infty\frac{1}{(2n+1)^4}=\frac{\pi^4}{96}.\ }$$

solution_steps.label: Check
solution_steps.content: $\zeta(4)=\frac{\pi^4}{90}$; odd/even split gives $S=\zeta(4)-\frac{1}{16}\zeta(4)=\frac{15}{16}\cdot\frac{\pi^4}{90}=\frac{\pi^4}{96}$.

items.label: Exam 2025 ┬Ę 2 ŌĆö Modes of convergence (MC)
items.statement: For four functions on the circle, decide which of: uniform / $L^2$ / pointwise-at-0 / Ces├Āro convergence hold.
items.intuition: $L^2$ convergence is free for integrable $f$; pointwise/uniform need smoothness or absolute summability; at a jump everything converges to the midpoint, not the assigned value.

solution_steps.label: Three tools settle everything
solution_steps.content: (i) \emph{Mean-square convergence} holds for every Riemann-integrable $f$ ŌĆö so \textbf{B is true in all four rows}. (ii) \emph{Absolutely summable coefficients} on a continuous $f$ Ō¤╣ uniform (hence pointwise) convergence to $f$. (iii) \emph{Pointwise/Fej├®r:} if $f$ is integrable and differentiable at $x_0$ then $S_N[f](x_0)\to f(x_0)$; and (Fej├®r) if the one-sided limits $f(x_0^\pm)$ exist then the Ces├Āro means converge to $\tfrac12(f(x_0^+)+f(x_0^-))$. Crucially, \emph{mere continuity at a point ŌĆö or even everywhere ŌĆö does not give pointwise or uniform convergence} (du Bois-Reymond).

solution_steps.label: Row 1
solution_steps.content: (RI, continuous only at $0$): A Ō£Ś ($f$ may be discontinuous elsewhere, and a uniform limit of continuous functions is continuous), B Ō£ō, C Ō£Ś (continuity at a point is not enough), D Ō£ō (Fej├®r: $f(0^\pm)=1$). $\Rightarrow$ \textbf{B, D}.

solution_steps.label: Row 2
solution_steps.content: (also $f'(0)$ exists): adds C Ō£ō (differentiability at $0$ forces $S_N(0)\to f(0)=1$); A still Ō£Ś. $\Rightarrow$ \textbf{B, C, D}.

solution_steps.label: Row 3
solution_steps.content: ($f$ continuous, $\sum|\hat f(n)|<\infty$): uniform convergence to $f$, so A Ō£ō, and then B, C Ō£ō; a convergent series is Ces├Āro summable to the same value, D Ō£ō. $\Rightarrow$ \textbf{A, B, C, D}.

solution_steps.label: Row 4
solution_steps.content: ($f=\mathbf 1_{[-\pi,0]}$, a jump at $0$): $f(0^-)=1,\ f(0^+)=0$. Both the partial sums (Dirichlet) and the Ces├Āro means (Fej├®r) converge to the average $\tfrac12\neq1$, so C Ō£Ś and D Ō£Ś; A Ō£Ś (discontinuous), B Ō£ō. $\Rightarrow$ \textbf{B}.

items.label: Exam 2025 ┬Ę 3a ŌĆö sinc Ōłł L┬▓ with box transform
items.statement: Show the sinc function lies in $L^2(\mathbb R)$ and has Fourier transform $\chi_{[-1/2,1/2]}$.
items.formal_statement: For $\psi(x)=\frac{\sin(\pi x)}{\pi x}$ ($\psi(0)=1$), show $\psi\in L^2(\mathbb R)$ and $\hat\psi(\xi)=\chi_{[-1/2,1/2]}(\xi)$ (transform convention $\hat f(\xi)=\int f(x)e^{-2\pi i x\xi}dx$).
items.intuition: Box and sinc are a Fourier pair; the box is the easy ($L^1$) side, and unitarity of $\mathcal F$ on $L^2$ transports the identity back to the sinc.

solution_steps.label: $\psi\in L^2$
solution_steps.content: $\psi$ is continuous and $|\psi(x)|^2=\frac{\sin^2(\pi x)}{\pi^2x^2}$ is bounded near $0$ (limit $1$); for $|x|\ge1$, $|\psi(x)|^2\le\frac{1}{\pi^2x^2}$, which is integrable. Hence $\int_{\mathbb R}|\psi|^2<\infty$. (Note $\psi\notin L^1$: it decays only like $1/|x|$, so the transform must be taken in the $L^2$/Plancherel sense.)

solution_steps.label: Identify $\hat\psi$
solution_steps.content:
Let $b:=\chi_{[-1/2,1/2]}\in L^1\cap L^2$, even. Compute its transform directly:
$$\hat b(x)=\int_{-1/2}^{1/2}e^{-2\pi i x\xi}\,d\xi=\frac{e^{-\pi ix}-e^{\pi ix}}{-2\pi ix}=\frac{\sin(\pi x)}{\pi x}=\psi(x).$$
So $\hat b=\psi$. The Fourier transform extends to a \textbf{unitary} map on $L^2$ with $\mathcal F\mathcal F\,b=b(-\,\cdot)$ (double transform = reflection). Since $b$ is even, $b(-\cdot)=b$, hence
$$\hat\psi=\mathcal F[\hat b]=\mathcal F\mathcal F\,b=b(-\cdot)=b=\chi_{[-1/2,1/2]}.$$
Equivalently: $\psi$ is the inverse transform of the unit box, so by inversion the forward transform of $\psi$ is that box; membership $\psi\in L^2$ is exactly what makes Plancherel applicable despite $\psi\notin L^1$.

items.label: Exam 2025 ┬Ę 3b ŌĆö Shannon orthonormal sets
items.statement: Show the integer translates of sinc, and their transforms, are orthonormal in $L^2(\mathbb R)$.
items.formal_statement: With $\psi_k(x)=\psi(x+k)$ and $\hat\psi_k=\mathcal F[\psi_k]$, show $\{\hat\psi_k\}_{k\in\mathbb Z}$ and $\{\psi_k\}_{k\in\mathbb Z}$ are orthonormal sets in $L^2(\mathbb R)$.
items.intuition: On the frequency side the translates become pure exponentials on $[-1/2,1/2]$, whose orthonormality is classical; unitarity carries it back to the sincs.

solution_steps.label: Argument
solution_steps.content:
Translation by $+k$ multiplies the transform by a phase:
$$\hat\psi_k(\xi)=\mathcal F[\psi(\cdot+k)](\xi)=e^{2\pi i k\xi}\,\hat\psi(\xi)=e^{2\pi i k\xi}\,\chi_{[-1/2,1/2]}(\xi).$$

solution_steps.label: $\{\hat\psi_k\}$ orthonormal
solution_steps.content:
For $k,\ell\in\mathbb Z$,
$$\langle\hat\psi_k,\hat\psi_\ell\rangle=\int_{-1/2}^{1/2}e^{2\pi i(k-\ell)\xi}\,d\xi=\frac{\sin(\pi(k-\ell))}{\pi(k-\ell)}=\delta_{k\ell},$$
the classical orthonormality of $\{e^{2\pi i k\xi}\}$ on an interval of length $1$.

solution_steps.label: $\{\psi_k\}$ orthonormal
solution_steps.content:
The Fourier transform is \textbf{unitary} on $L^2$ (Plancherel), so it preserves inner products:
$$\langle\psi_k,\psi_\ell\rangle_{L^2}=\langle\hat\psi_k,\hat\psi_\ell\rangle_{L^2}=\delta_{k\ell}.$$

solution_steps.label: Interpretation (Shannon sampling)
solution_steps.content: The $\psi_k$ form an orthonormal basis of the PaleyŌĆōWiener space $\mathrm{PW}_{1/2}=\{f\in L^2:\hat f\ \text{supported in}\ [-\tfrac12,\tfrac12]\}$ of band-limited functions; on the transform side this is just the Fourier basis $e^{2\pi i k\xi}$ on the band, and the reconstruction $f(x)=\sum_k f(-k)\psi(x+k)$ is the sampling theorem.

items.label: Exam 2025 ┬Ę 4 ŌĆö FT mapping properties (MC)
items.statement: Decide, for $f$ Schwartz / moderate-decrease / $L^2$ / a jump function, which target spaces $\hat f$ lands in.
items.formal_statement:
Classify $\hat f$ for each $f$ against: A ($\hat f\in C_0$), B ($\hat f\in C_m$, moderate decrease), C ($\hat f\in\mathcal S$), D ($\hat f\in L^1$), E ($\hat f\in L^2$), where
(1) $f\in\mathcal S(\mathbb R)$; (2) $f\in C_m(\mathbb R)$; (3) $f\in L^2(\mathbb R)$; (4) $f(x)=x^{-2}\chi_{[1,\infty)}(x)$.
items.intuition: L┬╣ buys continuity-and-decay (A); L┬▓ buys L┬▓ (E); rapid decay of $\hat f$ (B, C, D) is bought only by smoothness of $f$ ŌĆö a jump caps decay at $1/|\xi|$.

solution_steps.label: Facts
solution_steps.content: RiemannŌĆōLebesgue: $f\in L^1\Rightarrow\hat f\in C_0$ (gives A). Plancherel: $f\in L^2\Rightarrow\hat f\in L^2$ (gives E). $\mathcal F:\mathcal S\to\mathcal S$ bijectively, and $\mathcal S\subset C_m\subset L^1\cap L^2$, $\mathcal S\subset C_0$. \emph{Decay of $\hat f$ is governed by smoothness of $f$}: a jump in $f$ Ō¤╣ $\hat f\sim 1/|\xi|$ only.

solution_steps.label: Row 1
solution_steps.content: ($f\in\mathcal S$): $\hat f\in\mathcal S$ (C Ō£ō), and $\mathcal S$ sits inside all the others, so \textbf{A, B, C, D, E}.

solution_steps.label: Row 2
solution_steps.content: ($f\in C_m$): $C_m\subset L^1\Rightarrow$ A Ō£ō (RŌĆōL); $C_m\subset L^2\Rightarrow$ E Ō£ō (Plancherel). But moderate decrease says nothing about smoothness of $f$, so $\hat f$ need not decay faster than $C_0$: B Ō£Ś, C Ō£Ś, D Ō£Ś. $\Rightarrow$ \textbf{A, E}.

solution_steps.label: Row 3
solution_steps.content: ($f\in L^2$ only): Plancherel gives E Ō£ō; without $f\in L^1$ there is no continuity/decay, so A, B, C, D Ō£Ś. $\Rightarrow$ \textbf{E}.

solution_steps.label: Row 4
solution_steps.content:
($f=x^{-2}\chi_{[1,\infty)}$): $\int_1^\infty x^{-2}=1$ and $\int_1^\infty x^{-4}<\infty$, so $f\in L^1\cap L^2$ Ō¤╣ A Ō£ō, E Ō£ō. But $f$ has a jump at $x=1$; integrating by parts,
$$\hat f(\xi)=\frac{e^{-2\pi i\xi}}{2\pi i\xi}-\frac{1}{\pi i\xi}\int_1^\infty x^{-3}e^{-2\pi i x\xi}\,dx,$$
so $\hat f\sim \frac{1}{2\pi|\xi|}$. Since $1/|\xi|$ is neither integrable nor of moderate decrease, B Ō£Ś, D Ō£Ś, C Ō£Ś. $\Rightarrow$ \textbf{A, E}.

items.label: Exam 2025 ┬Ę 5 ŌĆö Heisenberg core inequality
items.statement: For Schwartz $\phi$, prove $\|\phi\|_2^2\le 2\|x\phi\|_2\|\phi'\|_2$.
items.formal_statement: For $\phi\in\mathcal S(\mathbb R)$ with $\|\phi\|_2\neq0$, show $\|\phi\|_2^2\le 2\,\|x\phi\|_2\,\|\phi'\|_2$.
items.intuition: Integration by parts trades $\|\phi\|_2^2$ for a product of position-spread $\|x\phi\|_2$ and gradient-size $\|\phi'\|_2$; CauchyŌĆōSchwarz closes it ŌĆö a function cannot be sharp in both position and frequency.

solution_steps.label: Computation
solution_steps.content:
Differentiate $|\phi|^2$: $\ \frac{d}{dx}|\phi(x)|^2=\phi'\overline\phi+\phi\overline{\phi'}=2\,\mathrm{Re}\big(\overline{\phi}\,\phi'\big)$. Integrate $\|\phi\|_2^2=\int 1\cdot|\phi|^2$ by parts with $u=|\phi|^2$, $v=x$:
$$\int_{\mathbb R}|\phi|^2\,dx=\Big[x|\phi(x)|^2\Big]_{-\infty}^{\infty}-\int_{\mathbb R}x\,\frac{d}{dx}|\phi(x)|^2\,dx.$$
The boundary term vanishes because $\phi\in\mathcal S$ decays faster than any power, so $x|\phi(x)|^2\to0$. Therefore, using $-2\,\mathrm{Re}(z)\le2|z|$ and then CauchyŌĆōSchwarz on $|x\phi|$ and $|\phi'|$,
$$\|\phi\|_2^2=-2\int_{\mathbb R}x\,\mathrm{Re}\big(\overline\phi\,\phi'\big)\,dx\le 2\int_{\mathbb R}|x\phi|\,|\phi'|\,dx\le 2\,\|x\phi\|_2\,\|\phi'\|_2.$$

solution_steps.label: Why this is Heisenberg
solution_steps.content:
Plancherel gives $\|\phi'\|_2=\|\widehat{\phi'}\|_2=\|2\pi i\xi\,\hat\phi\|_2=2\pi\|\xi\hat\phi\|_2$, so
$$\|x\phi\|_2^2\,\|\xi\hat\phi\|_2^2\ge\frac{\|\phi\|_2^4}{16\pi^2},$$
which, normalised to $\|\phi\|_2=1$, is the uncertainty principle in the problem's header.

items.label: Exam 2025 ┬Ę 6 ŌĆö 2-D heat kernel approximate identity
items.statement: Show $(\mathcal H_t^{(2)}*f)(x)\to f(x)$ as $t\to0^+$ for $f\in\mathcal S(\mathbb R^2)$.
items.formal_statement: For $\mathcal H_t^{(2)}(x)=\frac{1}{4\pi t}e^{-|x|^2/(4t)}$ and $f\in\mathcal S(\mathbb R^2)$, show $(\mathcal H_t^{(2)}*f)(x)\to f(x)$ as $t\to0^+$ for every $x\in\mathbb R^2$.
items.intuition: A Gaussian of shrinking width and unit mass becomes a delta; convolving against it returns the function. Split into a near part (continuity) and a far part (vanishing tail mass).

solution_steps.label: Setup
solution_steps.content: We show $\{\mathcal H_t^{(2)}\}$ is an \textbf{approximate identity} as $t\to0^+$.

solution_steps.label: (i) Mass $1$
solution_steps.content:
With $\int_{\mathbb R}e^{-s^2/4t}ds=\sqrt{4\pi t}$,
$$\int_{\mathbb R^2}\mathcal H_t^{(2)}=\frac{1}{4\pi t}\Big(\int_{\mathbb R}e^{-y_1^2/4t}dy_1\Big)\Big(\int_{\mathbb R}e^{-y_2^2/4t}dy_2\Big)=\frac{1}{4\pi t}(4\pi t)=1.$$

solution_steps.label: (ii) Positivity
solution_steps.content: $\mathcal H_t^{(2)}\ge0$, so $\|\mathcal H_t^{(2)}\|_{L^1}=1$ uniformly.

solution_steps.label: (iii) Concentration
solution_steps.content:
For $\delta>0$, substitute $y=\sqrt t\,u$:
$$\int_{|y|\ge\delta}\mathcal H_t^{(2)}(y)\,dy=\frac{1}{4\pi}\int_{|u|\ge\delta/\sqrt t}e^{-|u|^2/4}\,du\xrightarrow[t\to0^+]{}0,$$
since $\delta/\sqrt t\to\infty$ and $e^{-|u|^2/4}$ is integrable.

solution_steps.label: Conclusion
solution_steps.content:
Fix $x,\ \varepsilon>0$. Using (i),
$$\big(\mathcal H_t^{(2)}*f\big)(x)-f(x)=\int_{\mathbb R^2}\mathcal H_t^{(2)}(y)\big[f(x-y)-f(x)\big]\,dy.$$
$f\in\mathcal S$ is bounded and uniformly continuous; choose $\delta$ with $|f(x-y)-f(x)|<\varepsilon$ for $|y|<\delta$. Splitting and using $\mathcal H_t^{(2)}\ge0$,
$$\big|\mathcal H_t^{(2)}*f(x)-f(x)\big|\le \varepsilon\cdot1+2\|f\|_\infty\int_{|y|\ge\delta}\mathcal H_t^{(2)}(y)\,dy.$$
By (iii) the second term $\to0$, so $\limsup_{t\to0^+}|\cdots|\le\varepsilon$ for all $\varepsilon>0$; the limit is $0$.

solution_steps.label: Remarks
solution_steps.content: The bound used only uniform continuity and boundedness, so convergence is in fact uniform in $x$. On the transform side $\widehat{\mathcal H_t^{(2)}}(\xi)=e^{-4\pi^2t|\xi|^2}\to1$, i.e. $u(x,t)=\mathcal H_t^{(2)}*f$ solves the heat equation with initial data $f$.

items.label: SS 2.6.2 ŌĆö Symmetries and Fourier coefficients
items.statement: Translate evenness/oddness/half-period/reality of $f$ into symmetries of $\hat f(n)$.
items.formal_statement: Let $f$ be $2\pi$-periodic and Riemann integrable. (a) Write $f(\theta)\sim\hat f(0)+\sum_{n\ge1}[\hat f(n)+\hat f(-n)]\cos n\theta+i[\hat f(n)-\hat f(-n)]\sin n\theta$. (b) $f$ even $\Rightarrow\hat f(n)=\hat f(-n)$ (cosine series). (c) $f$ odd $\Rightarrow\hat f(n)=-\hat f(-n)$ (sine series). (d) $f(\theta+\pi)=f(\theta)\Rightarrow\hat f(n)=0$ for odd $n$. (e) $f$ real $\iff\hat f(-n)=\overline{\hat f(n)}$.
items.intuition: Each symmetry of $f$ becomes a linear constraint on its spectrum: evenŌåöreal cosine, oddŌåöimaginary sine, half-periodŌåökills odd harmonics, realŌåöHermitian coefficients.

solution_steps.label: (a)
solution_steps.content:
Group the symmetric tails of $\sum_n\hat f(n)e^{in\theta}$ and use $e^{\pm in\theta}=\cos n\theta\pm i\sin n\theta$:
$$\hat f(n)e^{in\theta}+\hat f(-n)e^{-in\theta}=[\hat f(n)+\hat f(-n)]\cos n\theta+i[\hat f(n)-\hat f(-n)]\sin n\theta,$$
and summing over $n\ge1$ with the $n=0$ term $\hat f(0)$ gives the stated form.

solution_steps.label: (b)
solution_steps.content:
If $f$ is even, substitute $\theta\mapsto-\theta$ in $\hat f(-n)=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^\pi f(\theta)e^{in\theta}d\theta$:
$$\hat f(-n)=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^\pi f(-\theta)e^{-in\theta}d\theta=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^\pi f(\theta)e^{-in\theta}d\theta=\hat f(n).$$
Then the $\sin$ coefficients $i[\hat f(n)-\hat f(-n)]$ vanish: a pure cosine series.

solution_steps.label: (c)
solution_steps.content: If $f$ is odd the same substitution gives $\hat f(-n)=-\hat f(n)$, so the $\cos$ coefficients vanish: a pure sine series.

solution_steps.label: (d)
solution_steps.content:
Substituting $\theta\mapsto\theta+\pi$ and using $f(\theta+\pi)=f(\theta)$,
$$\hat f(n)=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^\pi f(\theta+\pi)e^{-in(\theta+\pi)}d\theta=e^{-in\pi}\hat f(n)=(-1)^n\hat f(n),$$
so $(1-(-1)^n)\hat f(n)=0$; for odd $n$ this forces $\hat f(n)=0$.

solution_steps.label: (e)
solution_steps.content: $f$ is real $\iff f=\overline f$. Now $\widehat{\overline f}(n)=\frac1{2\pi}\int\overline{f}e^{-in\theta}=\overline{\frac1{2\pi}\int f e^{in\theta}}=\overline{\hat f(-n)}$. Matching coefficients, $f=\overline f\iff \hat f(n)=\overline{\hat f(-n)}$, i.e. $\hat f(-n)=\overline{\hat f(n)}$.

items.label: SS 2.6.3 ŌĆö Plucked string sine series
items.statement: Compute the sine series of the plucked-string tent profile.
items.formal_statement: For the plucked string of Chapter 1, the initial profile $f$ is the tent with peak height $h$ at $x=p$ on $[0,\pi]$. Show $f(x)=\sum_{m\ge1}A_m\sin mx$ with $A_m=\dfrac{2h\,\sin(mp)}{m^2\,p(\pi-p)}$.
items.intuition: A piecewise-linear pluck has a single corner, so coefficients decay like $1/m^2$ ŌĆö fast enough for the sine series to reproduce $f$ exactly and uniformly.

solution_steps.label: Argument
solution_steps.content: The plucked profile is $f(x)=\frac{h}{p}x$ on $[0,p]$ and $f(x)=\frac{h(\pi-x)}{\pi-p}$ on $[p,\pi]$, extended as an odd $2\pi$-periodic function. Its sine coefficients are $A_m=\frac2\pi\int_0^\pi f(x)\sin mx\,dx$.

solution_steps.label: Conclusion
solution_steps.content:
Two integrals (by parts):
$$\int_0^p x\sin mx\,dx=-\frac{p\cos mp}{m}+\frac{\sin mp}{m^2},\qquad \int_p^\pi(\pi-x)\sin mx\,dx=\frac{(\pi-p)\cos mp}{m}+\frac{\sin mp}{m^2},$$
(the second via $x\mapsto\pi-u$ and $\sin(m\pi-mu)=-(-1)^m\sin mu$, with the powers of $(-1)^m$ cancelling). Therefore
$$A_m=\frac{2h}{\pi}\Big[\frac1p\Big(-\frac{p\cos mp}{m}+\frac{\sin mp}{m^2}\Big)+\frac1{\pi-p}\Big(\frac{(\pi-p)\cos mp}{m}+\frac{\sin mp}{m^2}\Big)\Big].$$
The $\cos mp/m$ terms cancel, leaving
$$A_m=\frac{2h}{\pi}\cdot\frac{\sin mp}{m^2}\Big(\frac1p+\frac1{\pi-p}\Big)=\frac{2h}{\pi}\cdot\frac{\sin mp}{m^2}\cdot\frac{\pi}{p(\pi-p)}=\frac{2h\sin mp}{m^2\,p(\pi-p)}.$$
Finally $|A_m|\le \frac{2h}{p(\pi-p)}\,\frac1{m^2}$, so $\sum_m A_m\sin mx$ converges absolutely and uniformly; $f$ is continuous (and odd), so it equals its sine series.

items.label: SS 2.6.4 ŌĆö Sine series of $\theta(\pi-\theta)$
items.statement: Find the odd Fourier series of $\theta(\pi-\theta)$.
items.formal_statement: Let $f$ be the odd $2\pi$-periodic function with $f(\theta)=\theta(\pi-\theta)$ on $[0,\pi]$. Show $f(\theta)=\dfrac8\pi\displaystyle\sum_{k\ \mathrm{odd}\ge1}\frac{\sin k\theta}{k^3}$.
items.intuition: A smooth-looking parabola arch, odd-extended, is very smooth except at the joins, giving rapid $1/k^3$ decay and only odd harmonics by the half-wave symmetry.

solution_steps.label: Solution
solution_steps.content:
Since $f$ is odd, $f(\theta)=\sum_{k\ge1}b_k\sin k\theta$ with $b_k=\frac2\pi\int_0^\pi(\pi\theta-\theta^2)\sin k\theta\,d\theta$. Compute the two pieces:
$$\int_0^\pi\theta\sin k\theta\,d\theta=-\frac{\pi(-1)^k}{k},\qquad \int_0^\pi\theta^2\sin k\theta\,d\theta=-\frac{\pi^2(-1)^k}{k}-\frac{2\big(1-(-1)^k\big)}{k^3}.$$
Hence
$$\int_0^\pi(\pi\theta-\theta^2)\sin k\theta\,d\theta=\pi\Big(-\frac{\pi(-1)^k}{k}\Big)-\Big(-\frac{\pi^2(-1)^k}{k}-\frac{2(1-(-1)^k)}{k^3}\Big)=\frac{2(1-(-1)^k)}{k^3}.$$
So $b_k=\frac2\pi\cdot\frac{2(1-(-1)^k)}{k^3}=\frac{4(1-(-1)^k)}{\pi k^3}$, which is $0$ for even $k$ and $\frac{8}{\pi k^3}$ for odd $k$. Thus
$$f(\theta)=\frac8\pi\sum_{k\ \mathrm{odd}\ge1}\frac{\sin k\theta}{k^3}.$$
The $1/k^3$ decay gives absolute, uniform convergence; $f$ is continuous and odd, so equality holds pointwise.

items.label: SS 2.6.5 ŌĆö Triangular-tent cosine series
items.statement: Cosine series of a triangular tent of half-width $\delta$.
items.formal_statement: On $[-\pi,\pi]$ let $f(\theta)=1-|\theta|/\delta$ for $|\theta|\le\delta$ and $0$ otherwise. Show $f(\theta)=\dfrac{\delta}{2\pi}+2\displaystyle\sum_{n\ge1}\frac{1-\cos n\delta}{n^2\pi\delta}\cos n\theta.$
items.intuition: A narrow tent of width $\delta$: width controls the spectral envelope through the factor $1-\cos n\delta$, while the corner fixes the $1/n^2$ decay.

solution_steps.label: Solution
solution_steps.content:
$f$ is even and supported on $[-\delta,\delta]$. The mean is $\hat f(0)=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\delta}^\delta(1-|\theta|/\delta)\,d\theta=\frac{1}{2\pi}\cdot\delta=\frac{\delta}{2\pi}$ (the tent has area $\delta$). For $n\neq0$, using evenness,
$$\hat f(n)=\frac1\pi\int_0^\delta\Big(1-\frac{\theta}{\delta}\Big)\cos n\theta\,d\theta.$$
Now $\int_0^\delta\cos n\theta\,d\theta=\frac{\sin n\delta}{n}$ and, by parts,
$$\int_0^\delta\frac\theta\delta\cos n\theta\,d\theta=\frac{\sin n\delta}{n}-\frac{1-\cos n\delta}{\delta n^2}.$$
Subtracting, $\int_0^\delta(1-\theta/\delta)\cos n\theta\,d\theta=\frac{1-\cos n\delta}{\delta n^2}$, so $\hat f(n)=\frac{1-\cos n\delta}{\pi\delta n^2}$. Since $f$ is even, $\hat f(n)=\hat f(-n)$ and the real series is
$$f(\theta)=\hat f(0)+2\sum_{n\ge1}\hat f(n)\cos n\theta=\frac{\delta}{2\pi}+2\sum_{n\ge1}\frac{1-\cos n\delta}{n^2\pi\delta}\cos n\theta.$$
Coefficients are $O(1/n^2)$, so convergence is uniform to the continuous $f$.

items.label: SS 2.6.7 ŌĆö Summation by parts & Dirichlet's test
items.statement: Summation by parts and the resulting Dirichlet convergence test.
items.formal_statement: (a) Prove summation by parts: with $B_k=\sum_{n=1}^k b_n$, $B_0=0$, $\sum_{n=M}^N a_nb_n=a_NB_N-a_MB_{M-1}-\sum_{n=M}^{N-1}(a_{n+1}-a_n)B_n$. (b) Deduce Dirichlet's test: if the partial sums of $\sum b_n$ are bounded and $a_n\downarrow0$, then $\sum a_nb_n$ converges.
items.intuition: The discrete integration-by-parts trades oscillation (bounded $B_k$) against monotone decay ($a_n\downarrow0$) ŌĆö the discrete analogue of $\int u\,dv$ and the engine behind conditional convergence.

solution_steps.label: (a)
solution_steps.content:
Write $b_n=B_n-B_{n-1}$. Then
$$\sum_{n=M}^N a_nb_n=\sum_{n=M}^N a_n(B_n-B_{n-1})=\sum_{n=M}^N a_nB_n-\sum_{n=M-1}^{N-1}a_{n+1}B_n.$$
Peel the top term $a_NB_N$ from the first sum and the bottom term $a_MB_{M-1}$ from the second; the overlapping range $M\le n\le N-1$ gives $\sum_{n=M}^{N-1}(a_n-a_{n+1})B_n$. Hence
$$\sum_{n=M}^N a_nb_n=a_NB_N-a_MB_{M-1}-\sum_{n=M}^{N-1}(a_{n+1}-a_n)B_n.$$

solution_steps.label: (b)
solution_steps.content:
Suppose $|B_k|\le L$ for all $k$ and $a_n\downarrow0$. In (a) with $M$ fixed and $N\to\infty$: the term $a_NB_N\to0$ (since $a_N\to0$, $|B_N|\le L$). The series $\sum_{n\ge M}(a_{n+1}-a_n)B_n$ converges absolutely because
$$\sum_{n\ge M}|a_{n+1}-a_n|\,|B_n|\le L\sum_{n\ge M}(a_n-a_{n+1})=L\,a_M<\infty$$
(telescoping, using monotonicity $a_n\ge a_{n+1}$). Therefore the partial sums $\sum_{n=M}^N a_nb_n$ converge as $N\to\infty$, i.e. $\sum a_nb_n$ converges.

items.label: SS 2.6.8 ŌĆö Sawtooth series converges everywhere
items.statement: Identify and prove everywhere-convergence of the sawtooth series.
items.formal_statement: Verify that $\frac1{2i}\sum_{n\neq0}\frac{e^{inx}}{n}$ is the Fourier series of the $2\pi$-periodic sawtooth $f$ with $f(0)=0$, $f(x)=-\frac\pi2-\frac x2$ on $(-\pi,0)$ and $\frac\pi2-\frac x2$ on $(0,\pi)$. Show the (symmetric) series converges for every $x$, with value $0$ at the origin (the average of the one-sided limits).
items.intuition: A jump discontinuity drops decay to $1/n$ ŌĆö too slow for absolute convergence, but Dirichlet's test still gives conditional convergence everywhere, landing on the jump's midpoint.

solution_steps.label: Coefficients
solution_steps.content:
For $n
eq0$, integrating each linear piece and the $-x/2$ term by parts,
$$\hat f(n)= rac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^\pi f(x)e^{-inx}\,dx= rac1{2in}.$$
(The two constant pieces contribute $\pi rac{(-1)^n-1}{-in}$ and $-	frac12\int_{-\pi}^\pi xe^{-inx}dx=- rac{\pi(-1)^n}{-in}$; summing gives $ rac{\pi}{in}\cdot rac1{2\pi}= rac1{2in}$.) Also $\hat f(0)=0$. Hence the Fourier series is $\sum_{n
eq0} rac1{2in}e^{inx}= rac1{2i}\sum_{n
eq0} rac{e^{inx}}n$.

solution_steps.label: Convergence
solution_steps.content: Pairing $\pm n$, $\frac1{2i}\big(\frac{e^{inx}}n+\frac{e^{-inx}}{-n}\big)=\frac{\sin nx}{n}$, so the symmetric partial sums equal $\sum_{n=1}^N\frac{\sin nx}n$. Apply Dirichlet's test (Exercise 7) with $a_n=1/n\downarrow0$ and $b_n=\sin nx$: for $x\notin2\pi\mathbb Z$ the partial sums $\sum_{n=1}^N\sin nx=\frac{\cos(x/2)-\cos((N+1/2)x)}{2\sin(x/2)}$ are bounded by $1/|\sin(x/2)|$, so the series converges. For $x\in2\pi\mathbb Z$ every term is $0$. Thus the series converges for all $x$.

solution_steps.label: Conclusion
solution_steps.content: At $x=0$ the sum is $0$, which equals $\tfrac12\big(f(0^+)+f(0^-)\big)=\tfrac12(\tfrac\pi2-\tfrac\pi2)=0$ ŌĆö the midpoint of the jump, as expected for a piecewise-$C^1$ function.

items.label: SS 2.6.10 ŌĆö $C^k$ smoothness $\Rightarrow$ $O(1/|n|^k)$ decay
items.statement: Smoothness of $f$ controls the decay rate of $\hat f(n)$.
items.formal_statement: If $f$ is $2\pi$-periodic and of class $C^k$, show $\hat f(n)=O(1/|n|^k)$ as $|n|\to\infty$.
items.intuition: Differentiation multiplies the $n$-th coefficient by $in$; running it backwards, $k$ orders of smoothness force $k$ powers of decay.

solution_steps.label: Solution
solution_steps.content:
Integrate by parts; periodicity kills every boundary term:
$$\hat f(n)=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^\pi f(\theta)e^{-in\theta}\,d\theta=\frac1{2\pi}\,\frac1{in}\int_{-\pi}^\pi f'(\theta)e^{-in\theta}\,d\theta=\frac1{in}\,\widehat{f'}(n).$$
Iterating $k$ times (valid since $f\in C^k$),
$$\hat f(n)=\frac{1}{(in)^k}\,\widehat{f^{(k)}}(n).$$
Because $f^{(k)}$ is continuous on the circle it is bounded, so $|\widehat{f^{(k)}}(n)|\le\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^\pi|f^{(k)}|=:C$ for all $n$. Therefore
$$|\hat f(n)|\le\frac{C}{|n|^k},\qquad\text{i.e. }\hat f(n)=O(|n|^{-k}).$$
This is the quantitative half of the decayŌĆōsmoothness duality: each derivative buys one power of $1/|n|$.

items.label: SS 2.6.11 ŌĆö L┬╣ convergence Ō¤╣ uniform coefficient convergence
items.statement: $L^1$ convergence of functions gives uniform convergence of coefficients.
items.formal_statement: If $\{f_k\}$ are Riemann integrable on $[0,1]$ with $\int_0^1|f_k-f|\,dx\to0$, show $\hat f_k(n)\to\hat f(n)$ uniformly in $n$.
items.intuition: The Fourier coefficient is an $L^1$-bounded functional ($|\hat g(n)|\le\|g\|_1$ uniformly in $n$), so $L^1$ closeness forces uniform spectral closeness.

solution_steps.label: Solution
solution_steps.content:
For every $n$,
$$|\hat f_k(n)-\hat f(n)|=\Big|\int_0^1\big(f_k(x)-f(x)\big)e^{-2\pi inx}\,dx\Big|\le\int_0^1|f_k(x)-f(x)|\,|e^{-2\pi inx}|\,dx=\int_0^1|f_k-f|\,dx.$$
The right-hand side is independent of $n$ and tends to $0$. Hence
$$\sup_{n\in\mathbb Z}|\hat f_k(n)-\hat f(n)|\le\int_0^1|f_k-f|\,dx\xrightarrow[k\to\infty]{}0,$$
which is exactly uniform convergence of the coefficients.

items.label: SS 2.6.14a ŌĆö Tauber's theorem (Ces├Āro)
items.statement: A Tauberian converse: Ces├Āro + $o(1/n)$ implies convergence.
items.formal_statement: If $\sum c_n$ is Ces├Āro summable to $\sigma$ and $c_n=o(1/n)$ (i.e. $nc_n\to0$), prove $\sum c_n$ converges to $\sigma$.
items.intuition: Ces├Āro averaging can only smear, not invent, a limit: a mild decay condition on the terms forces the averaged limit back onto the genuine partial sums.

solution_steps.label: Argument
solution_steps.content:
Let $s_n=\sum_{k=1}^n c_k$ and $\sigma_n=\frac1n\sum_{k=1}^n s_k\to\sigma$. A direct computation rearranges the Ces├Āro mean:
$$\sigma_n=\frac1n\sum_{k=1}^n s_k=\frac1n\sum_{j=1}^n(n-j+1)c_j\quad\Longrightarrow\quad s_n-\sigma_n=\frac1n\sum_{j=1}^n(j-1)c_j.$$
Set $a_j=(j-1)c_j$. By hypothesis $jc_j\to0$, hence $a_j\to0$. The Ces├Āro mean of a null sequence is null:
$$\frac1n\sum_{j=1}^n a_j\to0,$$
so $s_n-\sigma_n\to0$. Since $\sigma_n\to\sigma$, we conclude $s_n\to\sigma$, i.e. $\sum c_n=\sigma$.

solution_steps.label: Remark
solution_steps.content: The Tauberian side condition $nc_n\to0$ is what blocks pathologies (e.g. $\sum(-1)^n$ is Ces├Āro summable but divergent ŌĆö there $nc_n\not\to0$).

items.label: SS 2.6.15 ŌĆö Closed form of the Fej├®r kernel
items.statement: Derive the squared-sine closed form of the Fej├®r kernel.
items.formal_statement: Prove the Fej├®r kernel satisfies $F_N(x)=\dfrac1N\,\dfrac{\sin^2(Nx/2)}{\sin^2(x/2)}$.
items.intuition: Averaging the oscillating Dirichlet kernels produces a perfect square ŌĆö positivity is exactly why Ces├Āro means behave where raw partial sums fail.

solution_steps.label: Solution
solution_steps.content:
By definition $N F_N(x)=\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}D_n(x)$ where $D_n(x)=\sum_{k=-n}^n e^{ikx}=\dfrac{\sin((n+\tfrac12)x)}{\sin(x/2)}$. Hence
$$N F_N(x)=\frac1{\sin(x/2)}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\sin\big((n+\tfrac12)x\big).$$
Evaluate the sum as an imaginary part of a geometric series ($\omega=e^{ix}$):
$$\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\sin\big((n+\tfrac12)x\big)=\operatorname{Im}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\omega^{\,n+1/2}=\operatorname{Im}\frac{\omega^{1/2}(\omega^N-1)}{\omega-1}=\operatorname{Im}\frac{e^{iNx}-1}{e^{ix/2}-e^{-ix/2}}=\frac{1-\cos Nx}{2\sin(x/2)}=\frac{\sin^2(Nx/2)}{\sin(x/2)},$$
using $1-\cos Nx=2\sin^2(Nx/2)$. Therefore
$$N F_N(x)=\frac{\sin^2(Nx/2)}{\sin^2(x/2)},\qquad F_N(x)=\frac1N\frac{\sin^2(Nx/2)}{\sin^2(x/2)}.$$
In particular $F_N\ge0$, $\frac1{2\pi}\int F_N=1$, and the mass concentrates at $0$ ŌĆö the three properties making $\{F_N\}$ a good kernel.

items.label: SS 2.6.16 ŌĆö Weierstrass approximation via Fej├®r
items.statement: Prove Weierstrass approximation from Fej├®r's theorem.
items.formal_statement: Prove the Weierstrass approximation theorem: a continuous $f$ on $[a,b]$ is uniformly approximable by polynomials. Use Fej├®r's Corollary 5.4 and the polynomial approximability of $e^{ix}$.
items.intuition: Two approximations in series: Fej├®r makes trig polynomials dense in continuous functions, and Taylor makes ordinary polynomials dense in trig polynomials.

solution_steps.label: Argument
solution_steps.content: Rescale so that $[a,b]\subset(-\pi,\pi)$, say into $[-\pi+\eta,\pi-\eta]$, and extend $f$ to a continuous $2\pi$-periodic function $g$ (taper to $0$ near $\pm\pi$). Let $\varepsilon>0$.

solution_steps.label: Step 1 (trigonometric approximation)
solution_steps.content: By Fej├®r's theorem (Cor. 5.4), the Ces├Āro means $\sigma_N(g)$ converge uniformly to $g$ on the circle; so there is a trigonometric polynomial $T(x)=\sum_{|k|\le M}c_ke^{ikx}$ with $\sup_x|g(x)-T(x)|<\varepsilon/2$.

solution_steps.label: Step 2 (polynomial approximation of $T$)
solution_steps.content: Each $e^{ikx}=\sum_{j\ge0}\frac{(ikx)^j}{j!}$ is entire; its Taylor partial sums converge uniformly on the bounded interval $[a,b]$. Choose a polynomial $P$ with $\sup_{[a,b]}|T(x)-P(x)|<\varepsilon/2$ (approximate each of the finitely many exponentials).

solution_steps.label: Conclusion
solution_steps.content: On $[a,b]$, $|f-P|\le|g-T|+|T-P|<\varepsilon$.

solution_steps.label: Step 5
solution_steps.content: This deduces the polynomial (real-analysis) density theorem from the trigonometric one, with Fej├®r's positive kernel doing the analytic work.

items.label: SS 2.6.17b ŌĆö Ces├Āro sum at a jump
items.statement: Fej├®r means converge to the midpoint at a jump discontinuity.
items.formal_statement: If $f$ is integrable with a jump discontinuity at $\theta$ (one-sided limits $f(\theta^\pm)$ exist), show its Fourier series is Ces├Āro summable at $\theta$ to $\tfrac12\big(f(\theta^+)+f(\theta^-)\big)$.
items.intuition: Fej├®r's positive, concentrating kernel reads off the symmetric average of the two one-sided values ŌĆö so Ces├Āro summation tames jumps where ordinary partial sums (Gibbs) overshoot.

solution_steps.label: Solution
solution_steps.content:
The Ces├Āro mean is $\sigma_N(f)(\theta)=(f*F_N)(\theta)=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^\pi f(\theta-t)F_N(t)\,dt$. Since $F_N$ is even and $\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^\pi F_N=1$, we have $\frac1{2\pi}\int_0^\pi F_N=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^0F_N=\tfrac12$. Split the convolution into $t>0$ and $t<0$ (substituting $t\mapsto-t$ in the latter):
$$\sigma_N(f)(\theta)=\frac1{2\pi}\int_0^\pi F_N(t)\big[f(\theta-t)+f(\theta+t)\big]\,dt.$$
Subtract the target using the half-masses:
$$\sigma_N(f)(\theta)-\frac{f(\theta^+)+f(\theta^-)}2=\frac1{2\pi}\int_0^\pi F_N(t)\Big[\big(f(\theta-t)-f(\theta^-)\big)+\big(f(\theta+t)-f(\theta^+)\big)\Big]dt.$$
Fix $\varepsilon>0$. By the one-sided limits choose $\delta$ so the bracket is $<\varepsilon$ for $0<t<\delta$; that part contributes $\le\varepsilon\cdot\frac1{2\pi}\int_0^\pi F_N\le\varepsilon$. On $\delta\le t\le\pi$, $F_N(t)=\frac1N\frac{\sin^2(Nt/2)}{\sin^2(t/2)}\le\frac1{N\sin^2(\delta/2)}\to0$ uniformly, so (with $f$ integrable, hence the bracket integrable) that part $\to0$. Thus $\limsup_N|\sigma_N(f)(\theta)-\tfrac12(f(\theta^+)+f(\theta^-))|\le\varepsilon$ for all $\varepsilon$, giving the claim.

items.label: SS 2.7.1 ŌĆö Integrable functions with dense discontinuities
items.statement: Build Riemann-integrable functions with a dense set of discontinuities.
items.formal_statement: (a) With $f(x)=0$ for $x<0$, $f(x)=1$ for $x\ge0$ and $\{r_n\}$ dense in $[0,1]$, show $F(x)=\sum_{n}\frac1{n^2}f(x-r_n)$ is Riemann integrable and discontinuous exactly at each $r_n$. (b) Show $F(x)=\sum_n 3^{-n}g(x-r_n)$, $g(x)=\sin(1/x)$ ($g(0)=0$), is integrable, discontinuous at every $r_n$, and monotone on no subinterval.
items.intuition: Riemann integrability tolerates a dense (but measure-zero) discontinuity set; geometric weights $3^{-n}$ keep each oscillation from being drowned out by the tail.

solution_steps.label: (a)
solution_steps.content: Each summand $\frac1{n^2}f(x-r_n)$ is a nondecreasing step of height $\frac1{n^2}$ at $x=r_n$. The series converges uniformly (Weierstrass $M$-test, $\sum n^{-2}<\infty$) to a bounded, \emph{nondecreasing} $F$. A monotone bounded function on $[0,1]$ has at most countably many discontinuities and is Riemann integrable. At $x=r_m$, $F$ jumps: $F(r_m^+)-F(r_m^-)\ge\frac1{m^2}>0$ (the $m$-th term jumps there; the others, being continuous from one side appropriately, do not remove it). Hence $F$ is discontinuous exactly on the dense set $\{r_n\}$ yet still integrable.

solution_steps.label: (b)
solution_steps.content: Now $g(x)=\sin(1/x)$ is bounded ($|g|\le1$) and continuous except at $0$, hence Riemann integrable on any interval; the series $\sum_n3^{-n}g(x-r_n)$ converges uniformly, so $F$ is bounded and integrable. \emph{Discontinuity at $r_m$:} as $x\to r_m$, the $m$-th term $3^{-m}\sin\big(1/(x-r_m)\big)$ oscillates with amplitude $3^{-m}$, while the remaining terms vary by at most $\sum_{n\neq m}3^{-n}|{\Delta g}|$; using $3^{-m}>\sum_{n>m}3^{-n}=\tfrac12 3^{-m}$ and continuity of the other terms near $r_m$, the oscillation of the $m$-th term is not cancelled, so $\limsup_{x\to r_m}F-\liminf_{x\to r_m}F>0$: a genuine discontinuity. \emph{No monotonicity:} every subinterval contains some $r_m$ (density), near which the $m$-th term oscillates up and down with un-cancelled amplitude, so $F$ cannot be monotone there.

items.label: SS 2.7.2 ŌĆö Lebesgue constants and divergence at a point
items.statement: Lebesgue constants grow like $\log N$, forcing divergence at a point.
items.formal_statement: For $D_N(\theta)=\frac{\sin((N+\frac12)\theta)}{\sin(\theta/2)}$ and $L_N=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^\pi|D_N(\theta)|\,d\theta$: (a) prove $L_N\ge c\log N$; (b) deduce that for each $n$ there is a continuous $f_n$ with $|f_n|\le1$ and $|S_n(f_n)(0)|\ge c'\log n$.
items.intuition: The $\log N$ growth of the Lebesgue constants is the precise obstruction to pointwise convergence: the Dirichlet kernel is not a good kernel, and its $L^1$ norm blows up.

solution_steps.label: (a)
solution_steps.content:
Since $|\sin(\theta/2)|\le|\theta|/2$, $|D_N(\theta)|\ge\frac{2|\sin((N+\frac12)\theta)|}{|\theta|}$. Thus, using evenness,
$$L_N=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^\pi|D_N|\ge\frac{c}{\,}\int_0^\pi\frac{|\sin((N+\tfrac12)\theta)|}{\theta}\,d\theta\overset{\phi=(N+\frac12)\theta}{=}c\int_0^{(N+\frac12)\pi}\frac{|\sin\phi|}{\phi}\,d\phi.$$
Bound the integral below by summing over the humps $[k\pi,(k+1)\pi]$:
$$\int_\pi^{N\pi}\frac{|\sin\phi|}{\phi}\,d\phi\ge\sum_{k=1}^{N-1}\frac1{(k+1)\pi}\int_{k\pi}^{(k+1)\pi}|\sin\phi|\,d\phi=\frac2\pi\sum_{k=1}^{N-1}\frac1{(k+1)\pi}\ge c\log N.$$
Hence $L_N\ge c\log N$ (a sharper analysis gives $L_N=\frac4{\pi^2}\log N+O(1)$).

solution_steps.label: (b)
solution_steps.content: Note $S_n(f)(0)=(f*D_n)(0)=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^\pi f(-\theta)D_n(\theta)\,d\theta$. The choice $g_n=\operatorname{sgn}(D_n)$ gives $\frac1{2\pi}\int g_nD_n=L_n\ge c\log n$, but $g_n$ is only piecewise constant. Approximate $g_n$ in the $L^1$ norm by continuous $h_k$ with $|h_k|\le1$ (Lemma 3.2); since $|\frac1{2\pi}\int(g_n-h_k)D_n|\le\|D_n\|_\infty\|g_n-h_k\|_1\to0$, some $f_n:=h_k$ satisfies $|S_n(f_n)(0)|\ge \tfrac12 c\log n=:c'\log n$ with $|f_n|\le1$.

solution_steps.label: Consequence
solution_steps.content: The partial-sum operators $f\mapsto S_n(f)(0)$ are unbounded on $C(\mathbb T)$ ($\|S_n\|=L_n\to\infty$); by uniform boundedness this is the seed of du Bois-Reymond's continuous function with a divergent Fourier series.

items.label: SS 3.3.1 ŌĆö $\mathbb R^d$ and $\mathbb C^d$ are complete
items.statement: Completeness of the basic finite-dimensional inner product spaces.
items.formal_statement: Show that the inner product spaces $\mathbb R^d$ and $\mathbb C^d$ are complete.
items.intuition: Finite-dimensional completeness is just coordinate-wise completeness of $\mathbb R$ or $\mathbb C$ ŌĆö a finite sum of limits.

solution_steps.label: Solution
solution_steps.content:
Let $(v_k)$ be a Cauchy sequence in $\mathbb C^d$ (the case $\mathbb R^d$ is identical), $v_k=(v_k^{(1)},\dots,v_k^{(d)})$. For each coordinate $j$,
$$|v_k^{(j)}-v_\ell^{(j)}|\le\Big(\sum_{i=1}^d|v_k^{(i)}-v_\ell^{(i)}|^2\Big)^{1/2}=\|v_k-v_\ell\|,$$
so $(v_k^{(j)})_k$ is Cauchy in $\mathbb C$, which is complete; let $w^{(j)}=\lim_k v_k^{(j)}$ and $w=(w^{(1)},\dots,w^{(d)})$. Then
$$\|v_k-w\|^2=\sum_{j=1}^d|v_k^{(j)}-w^{(j)}|^2\to0,$$
a finite sum of null sequences. Hence $v_k\to w\in\mathbb C^d$: every Cauchy sequence converges, so the space is complete.

items.label: SS 3.3.2 ŌĆö Ōäō┬▓(Ōäż) is complete
items.statement: Completeness of the sequence space $\ell^2(\mathbb Z)$.
items.formal_statement: Prove that $\ell^2(\mathbb Z)$ is complete.
items.intuition: The classic Hilbert-space completeness recipe: extract the coordinatewise limit, then upgrade to norm convergence on finite windows and let the window grow.

solution_steps.label: Argument
solution_steps.content: Let $A_k=(a_{k,n})_{n\in\mathbb Z}$ be a Cauchy sequence in $\ell^2(\mathbb Z)$.

solution_steps.label: Pointwise limit
solution_steps.content: For each fixed $n\in\mathbb Z$, we have $$|a_{k,n}-a_{\ell,n}|\le \|A_k-A_\ell\|_{\ell^2}.$$ Since $(A_k)$ is Cauchy in $\ell^2(\mathbb Z)$, it follows that $(a_{k,n})_{k\in\mathbb N}$ is Cauchy in $\mathbb C$. Since $\mathbb C$ is complete, the limit $$b_n:=\lim_{k\to\infty}a_{k,n}$$ exists for every $n\in\mathbb Z$. Define $$B=(b_n)_{n\in\mathbb Z}.$$

solution_steps.label: Convergence in norm
solution_steps.content: Let $\varepsilon>0$. Since $(A_k)$ is Cauchy, there exists $K\in\mathbb N$ such that $$\|A_k-A_\ell\|_{\ell^2}<\varepsilon \qquad \text{for all } k,\ell\ge K.$$ Fix $k\ge K$. For every $M\in\mathbb N$ and every $\ell\ge K$, $$\sum_{|n|\le M}|a_{k,n}-a_{\ell,n}|^2\le \|A_k-A_\ell\|_{\ell^2}^2<\varepsilon^2.$$ Since the sum over $|n|\le M$ is finite, we may pass to the limit as $\ell\to\infty$ inside the sum. Hence $$\sum_{|n|\le M}|a_{k,n}-b_n|^2\le \varepsilon^2.$$ Since this holds for every $M$, taking $M\to\infty$ gives $$\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}|a_{k,n}-b_n|^2\le \varepsilon^2.$$ Therefore $A_k-B\in\ell^2(\mathbb Z)$ and $$\|A_k-B\|_{\ell^2}\le \varepsilon \qquad \text{for all } k\ge K.$$ Hence $A_k\to B$ in $\ell^2(\mathbb Z)$.

solution_steps.label: Membership of the limit
solution_steps.content: Since $A_K\in\ell^2(\mathbb Z)$ and $B-A_K\in\ell^2(\mathbb Z)$, we have $$\|B\|_{\ell^2}\le \|B-A_K\|_{\ell^2}+\|A_K\|_{\ell^2}<\infty.$$ Hence $B\in\ell^2(\mathbb Z)$. Therefore every Cauchy sequence in $\ell^2(\mathbb Z)$ converges in $\ell^2(\mathbb Z)$, so $\ell^2(\mathbb Z)$ is complete.

items.label: SS 3.3.3 ŌĆö L┬▓-null but everywhere-divergent
items.statement: An $L^2$-null sequence that diverges at every single point.
items.formal_statement: Construct integrable $\{f_k\}$ on $[0,2\pi]$ with $\frac1{2\pi}\int_0^{2\pi}|f_k|^2\to0$ but $\lim_k f_k(\theta)$ failing to exist for every $\theta$.
items.intuition: Mean-square smallness controls average size, not values: a sweeping indicator of shrinking intervals vanishes in $L^2$ while pinging $1$ at every point forever.

solution_steps.label: Typewriter sequence
