solution_steps.content: Enumerate dyadic blocks: for $m\ge0$ and $0\le j<2^m$ take the interval $I_{m,j}=[\,2\pi j/2^m,\ 2\pi(j+1)/2^m\,]$, and list them as $I_1,I_2,\dots$ in order of increasing $m$. Let $f_k=\chi_{I_k}$.

solution_steps.label: $L^2$-null
solution_steps.content:
$|I_k|\to0$ as $k\to\infty$, so
$$\frac1{2\pi}\int_0^{2\pi}|f_k|^2\,d\theta=\frac{|I_k|}{2\pi}\to0.$$

solution_steps.label: Pointwise divergence everywhere
solution_steps.content: Every $\theta$ lies in exactly one block of each level $m$, hence in infinitely many $I_k$ (so $f_k(\theta)=1$ infinitely often) and outside infinitely many (so $f_k(\theta)=0$ infinitely often). Thus $\limsup_k f_k(\theta)=1\neq0=\liminf_k f_k(\theta)$, and $\lim_k f_k(\theta)$ exists for no $\theta$.

solution_steps.label: Step 4
solution_steps.content: This shows mean-square convergence does \emph{not} imply pointwise convergence (only a subsequence converges a.e.).

items.label: SS 3.3.4 ŌĆö The L┬▓-norm is only a seminorm on R
items.statement: Why the integral $L^2$-norm is only a seminorm on Riemann-integrable functions.
items.formal_statement: On the space $\mathcal R$ of integrable functions with $\|f\|=(\frac1{2\pi}\int_0^{2\pi}|f|^2)^{1/2}$: (a) find nonzero $f$ with $\|f\|=0$; (b) if $\|f\|=0$ then $f(x)=0$ at every point of continuity; (c) conversely, $f$ vanishing at all continuity points has $\|f\|=0$.
items.intuition: Integration cannot see a measure-zero set: $\|f\|=0$ pins down $f$ only up to its continuity points, which is why $L^2$ is built from equivalence classes.

solution_steps.label: (a)
solution_steps.content: Let $f(x_0)=1$ at a single point and $f\equiv0$ elsewhere. Then $f\neq0$ but $\int|f|^2=0$, so $\|f\|=0$.

solution_steps.label: (b)
solution_steps.content: Suppose $\|f\|=0$ and $f$ is continuous at $x$ with $f(x)\neq0$. By continuity $|f|\ge\frac12|f(x)|>0$ on some interval $(x-\delta,x+\delta)$, so $\frac1{2\pi}\int|f|^2\ge\frac1{2\pi}\cdot 2\delta\cdot\frac14|f(x)|^2>0$, contradicting $\|f\|=0$. Hence $f(x)=0$.

solution_steps.label: (c)
solution_steps.content: A Riemann integrable function is continuous except on a set of measure zero. If $f$ vanishes at every continuity point, then $f=0$ off a measure-zero set, so $|f|^2=0$ off a measure-zero set; a (bounded) Riemann integrable function that is $0$ except on a measure-zero set has $\int|f|^2=0$, i.e. $\|f\|=0$.

solution_steps.label: Step 4
solution_steps.content: So $\|\cdot\|$ is only a \emph{seminorm} on $\mathcal R$; passing to equivalence classes (equality at continuity points) makes it a norm ŌĆö the origin of $L^2$.

items.label: SS 3.3.8 ŌĆö Parseval evaluates $\zeta(4)$ and $\zeta(6)$
items.statement: Evaluate $\zeta(4)$ and $\zeta(6)$ (and odd-index sums) via Parseval.
items.formal_statement: (a) For $f(\theta)=|\theta|$ on $[-\pi,\pi]$, use Parseval to get $\sum_{n\ge0}(2n+1)^{-4}=\pi^4/96$ and $\sum_{n\ge1}n^{-4}=\pi^4/90$. (b) For the odd $f$ with $f(\theta)=\theta(\pi-\theta)$ on $[0,\pi]$, show $\sum_{n\ge0}(2n+1)^{-6}=\pi^6/960$ and $\sum_{n\ge1}n^{-6}=\pi^6/945$.
items.intuition: Parseval converts the easy integral of $f^2$ into an exact value of a zeta sum; the smoother $f$ (faster decay) reaches the higher even zeta value.

solution_steps.label: (a)
solution_steps.content:
For $f(\theta)=|\theta|$: $\hat f(0)=\frac\pi2$ and (Ch.2 Ex.6) $\hat f(n)=\frac{(-1)^n-1}{\pi n^2}$, i.e. $-\frac{2}{\pi n^2}$ for odd $n$, $0$ for even $n\neq0$. Parseval gives
$$\frac14\pi^2+2\sum_{n\ \mathrm{odd}\ge1}\frac{4}{\pi^2n^4}=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^\pi\theta^2\,d\theta=\frac{\pi^2}3.$$
So $\frac{8}{\pi^2}\sum_{n\ \mathrm{odd}}n^{-4}=\frac{\pi^2}3-\frac{\pi^2}4=\frac{\pi^2}{12}$, giving $\sum_{n\ \mathrm{odd}}n^{-4}=\frac{\pi^4}{96}$. Splitting even/odd, $\zeta(4)=\sum_{\mathrm{odd}}+\frac1{16}\zeta(4)$, so $\zeta(4)=\frac{16}{15}\cdot\frac{\pi^4}{96}=\frac{\pi^4}{90}$.

solution_steps.label: (b)
solution_steps.content:
For the odd $f(\theta)=\theta(\pi-\theta)$, the sine coefficients are $b_k=\frac{8}{\pi k^3}$ ($k$ odd), $0$ otherwise (Ch.2 Ex.4). Parseval for a sine series, $\|f\|^2=\frac12\sum_k b_k^2$, and $\|f\|^2=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^\pi f^2=\frac1{2\pi}\cdot\frac{\pi^5}{15}=\frac{\pi^4}{30}$ (since $\int_0^\pi\theta^2(\pi-\theta)^2d\theta=\frac{\pi^5}{30}$). Hence
$$\frac12\sum_{k\ \mathrm{odd}}\frac{64}{\pi^2k^6}=\frac{\pi^4}{30}\ \Rightarrow\ \sum_{k\ \mathrm{odd}}k^{-6}=\frac{\pi^6}{960}.$$
Splitting, $\zeta(6)=\sum_{\mathrm{odd}}+\frac1{64}\zeta(6)$, so $\zeta(6)=\frac{64}{63}\cdot\frac{\pi^6}{960}=\frac{\pi^6}{945}$.

items.label: SS 3.3.9 ŌĆö Parseval and the sum $\sum 1/(n+\alpha)^2$
items.statement: Derive $\sum 1/(n+\alpha)^2=\pi^2/\sin^2\pi\alpha$ from Parseval.
items.formal_statement: For non-integer $\alpha$, show the Fourier series of $\frac{\pi}{\sin\pi\alpha}e^{i(\pi-x)\alpha}$ on $[0,2\pi]$ is $\sum_{n}\frac{e^{inx}}{n+\alpha}$, and deduce $\sum_{n=-\infty}^\infty\frac1{(n+\alpha)^2}=\frac{\pi^2}{(\sin\pi\alpha)^2}$.
items.intuition: A single complex exponential profile has a tidy spectrum $1/(n+\alpha)$; Parseval then sums its squares to the classic cosecant-squared identity.

solution_steps.label: Coefficients
solution_steps.content:
With $g(x)=\frac{\pi}{\sin\pi\alpha}e^{i(\pi-x)\alpha}$,
$$c_n=\frac1{2\pi}\int_0^{2\pi}g(x)e^{-inx}dx=\frac{\pi}{\sin\pi\alpha}\cdot\frac{e^{i\pi\alpha}}{2\pi}\int_0^{2\pi}e^{-i(n+\alpha)x}dx.$$
Since $\int_0^{2\pi}e^{-i(n+\alpha)x}dx=\frac{e^{-2\pi i\alpha}-1}{-i(n+\alpha)}$ and $e^{i\pi\alpha}(e^{-2\pi i\alpha}-1)=e^{-i\pi\alpha}-e^{i\pi\alpha}=-2i\sin\pi\alpha$,
$$c_n=\frac{\pi}{\sin\pi\alpha}\cdot\frac1{2\pi}\cdot\frac{-2i\sin\pi\alpha}{-i(n+\alpha)}=\frac1{n+\alpha}.$$
So $g(x)\sim\sum_n\frac{e^{inx}}{n+\alpha}$.

solution_steps.label: Parseval
solution_steps.content:
$|g(x)|^2=\frac{\pi^2}{(\sin\pi\alpha)^2}$ (the exponential has modulus $1$), so
$$\sum_{n}\frac1{(n+\alpha)^2}=\sum_n|c_n|^2=\frac1{2\pi}\int_0^{2\pi}|g|^2\,dx=\frac{\pi^2}{(\sin\pi\alpha)^2}.$$
This is the partial-fractions identity for $\pi^2/\sin^2(\pi\alpha)$, recovered purely from orthonormality.

items.label: SS 3.3.12 ŌĆö The Dirichlet integral $\int \sin x / x = \pi/2$
items.statement: Evaluate the Dirichlet integral via the Dirichlet kernel and RiemannŌĆōLebesgue.
items.formal_statement: Prove $\int_0^\infty\frac{\sin x}{x}\,dx=\frac\pi2$.
items.intuition: The Dirichlet kernel has total mass $2\pi$; peeling off its $2/\theta$ singular part (the rest is killed by RiemannŌĆōLebesgue) leaves exactly the sine integral.

solution_steps.label: Solution
solution_steps.content:
Start from $\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^\pi D_N(\theta)\,d\theta=1$, i.e. $\int_{-\pi}^\pi\frac{\sin((N+\frac12)\theta)}{\sin(\theta/2)}\,d\theta=2\pi$. Write
$$\frac{1}{\sin(\theta/2)}=\frac{2}{\theta}+\Big(\frac1{\sin(\theta/2)}-\frac2\theta\Big),$$
where the bracket extends to a continuous (hence integrable) function on $[-\pi,\pi]$. By the RiemannŌĆōLebesgue lemma, $\int_{-\pi}^\pi\sin((N+\tfrac12)\theta)\big(\tfrac1{\sin(\theta/2)}-\tfrac2\theta\big)d\theta\to0$. Therefore
$$\int_{-\pi}^\pi\sin\big((N+\tfrac12)\theta\big)\frac2\theta\,d\theta\to2\pi\ \Longrightarrow\ \int_{-\pi}^\pi\frac{\sin((N+\frac12)\theta)}{\theta}\,d\theta\to\pi.$$
By evenness this is $2\int_0^\pi\frac{\sin((N+\frac12)\theta)}\theta d\theta\to\pi$, so $\int_0^\pi\frac{\sin((N+\frac12)\theta)}\theta d\theta\to\frac\pi2$. Substituting $x=(N+\tfrac12)\theta$ gives $\int_0^{(N+\frac12)\pi}\frac{\sin x}{x}\,dx\to\frac\pi2$, i.e.
$$\int_0^\infty\frac{\sin x}{x}\,dx=\frac\pi2.$$

items.label: SS 3.3.15 ŌĆö H├Člder regularity $\Rightarrow$ $O(1/|n|^\alpha)$ decay
items.statement: H├Člder continuity of order $\alpha$ gives sharp $O(|n|^{-\alpha})$ coefficient decay.
items.formal_statement:
Let $f$ be $2\pi$-periodic and Riemann integrable on $[-\pi,\pi]$.

\textbf{(a)} For $n\in\mathbb Z\setminus\{0\}$, show that
$$\widehat f(n)=-\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} f\!\left(x+\frac{\pi}{n}\right)e^{-inx}\,dx,$$
hence
$$\widehat f(n)=\frac1{4\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}\left[f(x)-f\!\left(x+\frac{\pi}{n}\right)\right]e^{-inx}\,dx.$$

\textbf{(b)} Now assume that $f$ satisfies a H├Člder condition of order $\alpha$, namely
$$|f(x+h)-f(x)|\le C|h|^\alpha$$
for some $0<\alpha\le1$, some $C>0$, and all $x,h$. Use part (a) to show that
$$\widehat f(n)=O\!\left(\frac1{|n|^\alpha}\right).$$

\textbf{(c)} Prove that the above result cannot be improved by showing that
$$f(x)=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}2^{-k\alpha}e^{i2^kx},\qquad 0<\alpha<1,$$
satisfies
$$|f(x+h)-f(x)|\le C|h|^\alpha,$$
and
$$\widehat f(N)=\frac1{N^\alpha}\qquad\text{whenever }N=2^k.$$

\textbf{Hint.} For (c), break up the sum $f(x+h)-f(x)$ into the parts $2^k\le1/|h|$ and $2^k>1/|h|$. To estimate the first sum, use $|1-e^{i\theta}|\le|\theta|$ whenever $\theta$ is small. To estimate the second sum, use $|e^{ix}-e^{iy}|\le2$.
items.intuition: The shift-by-half-period trick converts a modulus-of-continuity bound directly into coefficient decay; lacunary series saturate it, proving the exponent is exact.

solution_steps.label: (a)
solution_steps.content:
Substituting $x\mapsto x+\pi/n$ and using $e^{-i\pi}=-1$,
$$\hat f(n)=\frac1{2\pi}\int f(x+\tfrac\pi n)e^{-in(x+\pi/n)}dx=-\frac1{2\pi}\int f(x+\tfrac\pi n)e^{-inx}dx.$$
Averaging this with the defining formula $\hat f(n)=\frac1{2\pi}\int f(x)e^{-inx}dx$,
$$\hat f(n)=\frac1{4\pi}\int_{-\pi}^\pi\big[f(x)-f(x+\tfrac\pi n)\big]e^{-inx}\,dx.$$

solution_steps.label: (b)
solution_steps.content:
Hence
$$|\hat f(n)|\le\frac1{4\pi}\int_{-\pi}^\pi|f(x)-f(x+\tfrac\pi n)|\,dx\le\frac1{4\pi}\cdot2\pi\cdot C\Big(\frac\pi{|n|}\Big)^\alpha=\frac{C\pi^\alpha}{2}\,|n|^{-\alpha}=O(|n|^{-\alpha}).$$

solution_steps.label: (c)
solution_steps.content: For the lacunary series, only the term $k$ with $2^k=N$ contributes to $\hat f(N)$, giving $\hat f(2^k)=2^{-k\alpha}=N^{-\alpha}$ exactly. And $f$ is H├Člder-$\alpha$: splitting $f(x+h)-f(x)=\sum_{2^k\le1/|h|}+\sum_{2^k>1/|h|}$, the first sum is $\le\sum_{2^k\le1/|h|}2^{-k\alpha}2^k|h|\le C|h|^\alpha$ (using $|1-e^{i\theta}|\le|\theta|$), the second $\le\sum_{2^k>1/|h|}2\cdot2^{-k\alpha}\le C|h|^\alpha$ (geometric). So the decay rate $|n|^{-\alpha}$ cannot be improved.

items.label: SS 3.3.16 ŌĆö Lipschitz Ō¤╣ absolutely convergent series
items.statement: Lipschitz functions have absolutely (hence uniformly) convergent Fourier series.
items.formal_statement:
Let $f$ be a $2\pi$-periodic function satisfying a Lipschitz condition with constant $K$, that is,
$$|f(x)-f(y)|\le K|x-y|\qquad\text{for all }x,y.$$
This is the H├Člder condition with $\alpha=1$, so Exercise 15 gives $\widehat f(n)=O(1/|n|)$. Since the harmonic series $\sum 1/n$ diverges, this alone does not yet imply absolute convergence. The following outline proves that the Fourier series of $f$ converges absolutely and uniformly.

\textbf{(a)} For every positive $h$, define
$$g_h(x)=f(x+h)-f(x-h).$$
Prove that
$$\frac1{2\pi}\int_0^{2\pi}|g_h(x)|^2\,dx=\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty}4|\sin(nh)|^2|\widehat f(n)|^2,$$
and show that
$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty}|\sin(nh)|^2|\widehat f(n)|^2\le K^2h^2.$$

\textbf{(b)} Let $p$ be a positive integer. By choosing $h=\pi/2^{p+1}$, show that
$$\sum_{2^{p-1}<|n|\le 2^p}|\widehat f(n)|^2\le \frac{K^2\pi^2}{2^{2p+1}}.$$

\textbf{(c)} Estimate
$$\sum_{2^{p-1}<|n|\le 2^p}|\widehat f(n)|,$$
and conclude that the Fourier series of $f$ converges absolutely, hence uniformly. Hint: use the Cauchy--Schwarz inequality to estimate the sum.

\textbf{(d)} Modify the argument slightly to prove Bernstein's theorem: if $f$ satisfies a H├Člder condition of order $\alpha>1/2$, then the Fourier series of $f$ converges absolutely.
items.intuition: Dyadic frequency blocks + Parseval turn a Lipschitz modulus into square-summable blocks; CauchyŌĆōSchwarz then upgrades $\ell^2$ control to $\ell^1$ ŌĆö the threshold being $\alpha>1/2$.

solution_steps.label: (a)
solution_steps.content:
Since $\widehat{g_h}(n)=(e^{inh}-e^{-inh})\hat f(n)=2i\sin(nh)\hat f(n)$, Parseval gives
$$\frac1{2\pi}\int_0^{2\pi}|g_h|^2=\sum_n|\widehat{g_h}(n)|^2=\sum_n4\sin^2(nh)|\hat f(n)|^2.$$
Lipschitz: $|g_h(x)|=|f(x+h)-f(x-h)|\le K|2h|=2Kh$, so $\frac1{2\pi}\int|g_h|^2\le4K^2h^2$, whence $\sum_n\sin^2(nh)|\hat f(n)|^2\le K^2h^2$.

solution_steps.label: (b)
solution_steps.content:
Take $h=\pi/2^{p+1}$. For $2^{p-1}<|n|\le2^p$ we have $nh\in(\pi/4,\pi/2]$, so $\sin^2(nh)\ge\tfrac12$. Therefore
$$\frac12\sum_{2^{p-1}<|n|\le2^p}|\hat f(n)|^2\le\sum_n\sin^2(nh)|\hat f(n)|^2\le K^2h^2=\frac{K^2\pi^2}{2^{2p+2}},$$
i.e. $\sum_{2^{p-1}<|n|\le2^p}|\hat f(n)|^2\le K^2\pi^2/2^{2p+1}$.

solution_steps.label: (c)
solution_steps.content:
By CauchyŌĆōSchwarz over the dyadic block (which has $\le2^p$ terms),
$$\sum_{2^{p-1}<|n|\le2^p}|\hat f(n)|\le(2^p)^{1/2}\Big(\frac{K^2\pi^2}{2^{2p+1}}\Big)^{1/2}=\frac{K\pi}{2^{(p+1)/2}}.$$
Summing the geometric series over $p\ge1$ (plus the $n=0$ term) gives $\sum_n|\hat f(n)|<\infty$. By the Weierstrass $M$-test the Fourier series converges absolutely and uniformly (to $f$, by continuity).

solution_steps.label: (d) Remark (Bernstein)
solution_steps.content: The same dyadic argument with the exponent bookkeeping shows: H├Člder-$\alpha$ with $\alpha>1/2$ already forces absolute convergence.

items.label: SS 3.3.17 ŌĆö Monotone functions: O(1/|n|) decay
items.statement: Bounded monotone functions have $O(1/|n|)$ Fourier coefficients.
items.formal_statement:
If $f$ is a bounded monotonic function on $[-\pi,\pi]$, then
$$\widehat f(n)=O(1/|n|).$$
Hint: One may assume that $f$ is increasing and that $|f|\le M$. First check that the Fourier coefficients of the characteristic function of $[a,b]$ satisfy $O(1/|n|)$. Then show that a step function of the form
$$\sum_{k=1}^{N}\alpha_k\chi_{[a_k,a_{k+1}]}(x),$$
where
$$-\pi=a_1<a_2<\cdots<a_N<a_{N+1}=\pi,\qquad -M\le \alpha_1\le\cdots\le\alpha_N\le M,$$
has Fourier coefficients that are $O(1/|n|)$ uniformly in $N$. Summing by parts gives a telescopic sum $\sum(\alpha_{k+1}-\alpha_k)$, which can be bounded by $2M$. Finally, approximate $f$ by functions of the above type.
items.intuition: Monotonicity is bounded variation: total variation $\le2M$ caps the telescoped step-coefficients, and a single jump's $1/|n|$ decay sets the rate.

solution_steps.label: Solution
solution_steps.content:
Assume $f$ increasing, $|f|\le M$. First, the characteristic function of $[a,b]$ has
$$\widehat{\chi_{[a,b]}}(n)=\frac{e^{-ina}-e^{-inb}}{2\pi in},\qquad|\widehat{\chi_{[a,b]}}(n)|\le\frac1{\pi|n|},$$
so $O(1/|n|)$ with a constant independent of $a,b$. Next, a step function $s=\sum_{k=1}^N\alpha_k\chi_{[a_k,a_{k+1}]}$ with $-\pi=a_1<\dots<a_{N+1}=\pi$ and $-M\le\alpha_1\le\dots\le\alpha_N\le M$: writing $\chi_{[a_k,a_{k+1}]}=\chi_{[a_k,\pi]}-\chi_{[a_{k+1},\pi]}$ and summing by parts,
$$\hat s(n)=\sum_{k}(\alpha_k-\alpha_{k-1})\widehat{\chi_{[a_k,\pi]}}(n)+\text{(endpoint)},$$
so $|\hat s(n)|\le\frac1{\pi|n|}\sum_k(\alpha_k-\alpha_{k-1})\le\frac{2M}{\pi|n|}$ ŌĆö \emph{uniformly in $N$} (the increments telescope to $\le2M$). Finally, an increasing bounded $f$ is the uniform/$L^1$ limit of such increasing step functions $s_N$; their coefficients converge to $\hat f(n)$ while the bound $|\hat s_N(n)|\le\frac{2M}{\pi|n|}$ passes to the limit. Hence $\hat f(n)=O(1/|n|)$.

items.label: SS 3.4.2 ŌĆö Legendre polynomials: an orthogonal basis
items.statement: Legendre polynomials as an orthogonal (complete) basis of $L^2[-1,1]$.
items.formal_statement:
An important fact proved in Chapter 3 is that the family $\{e^{inx}\}_{n\in\mathbb Z}$ is orthonormal in $\mathcal R$ and complete in the sense that the Fourier series of $f$ converges to $f$ in the norm. In this problem, consider another family with the same properties. On $[-1,1]$, define
$$L_n(x)= \frac{d^n}{dx^n}(x^2-1)^n,\qquad n=0,1,2,\dots.$$
Then $L_n$ is a polynomial of degree $n$, called the $n$th Legendre polynomial.

	extbf{(a)} Show that if $f$ is indefinitely differentiable on $[-1,1]$, then
$$\int_{-1}^{1}L_n(x)f(x)\,dx=(-1)^n\int_{-1}^{1}(x^2-1)^n f^{(n)}(x)\,dx.$$
In particular, show that $L_n$ is orthogonal to $x^m$ whenever $m<n$. Hence $\{L_n\}_{n=0}^{\infty}$ is an orthogonal family.

	extbf{(b)} Show that
$$\|L_n\|^2=\int_{-1}^{1}|L_n(x)|^2\,dx=\frac{(n!)^2 2^{2n+1}}{2n+1}.$$
Hint: First note that
$$\|L_n\|^2=(-1)^n(2n)!\int_{-1}^{1}(x^2-1)^n\,dx.$$
Write $(x^2-1)^n=(x-1)^n(x+1)^n$ and integrate by parts $n$ times to calculate this last integral.

	extbf{(c)} Prove that any polynomial of degree $n$ that is orthogonal to $1,x,x^2,\dots,x^{n-1}$ is a constant multiple of $L_n$.

	extbf{(d)} Let $\widetilde L_n=L_n/\|L_n\|$ be the normalized Legendre polynomials. Prove that $\{\widetilde L_n\}$ is the family obtained by applying the Gram--Schmidt process to $\{1,x,\dots,x^n,\dots\}$, and conclude that every Riemann integrable function $f$ on $[-1,1]$ has a Legendre expansion
$$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\langle f,\widetilde L_n\rangle \widetilde L_n$$
which converges to $f$ in the mean-square sense.
items.intuition: Rodrigues' formula makes orthogonality fall out of repeated integration by parts; the polynomials give a complete orthonormal system on an interval, paralleling $\{e^{inx}\}$ on the circle.

solution_steps.label: (a)
solution_steps.content:
Integrate by parts $n$ times. The boundary terms involve $\frac{d^j}{dx^j}(x^2-1)^n$ for $j\le n-1$, each of which has the factor $(x^2-1)$ and so vanishes at $x=\pm1$. Hence
$$\int_{-1}^1 L_n(x)f(x)\,dx=\int_{-1}^1\Big(\tfrac{d^n}{dx^n}(x^2-1)^n\Big)f\,dx=(-1)^n\int_{-1}^1(x^2-1)^n f^{(n)}(x)\,dx.$$
Taking $f(x)=x^m$ with $m<n$ gives $f^{(n)}\equiv0$, so $\int_{-1}^1 L_n x^m=0$. Since every polynomial of degree $<n$ is a combination of such $x^m$, and $L_m$ has degree $m$, we get $\langle L_n,L_m\rangle=0$ for $m\neq n$: the family is orthogonal.

solution_steps.label: (b)
solution_steps.content:
With $f=L_n$ (degree $n$, leading coefficient $\frac{(2n)!}{n!}$), its $n$-th derivative is the constant $L_n^{(n)}=(2n)!$. So
$$\|L_n\|^2=(-1)^n\int_{-1}^1(x^2-1)^n L_n^{(n)}\,dx=(2n)!\int_{-1}^1(1-x^2)^n\,dx.$$
Using $\int_{-1}^1(1-x^2)^n dx=\frac{2^{2n+1}(n!)^2}{(2n+1)!}$ (Beta integral), and $\frac{(2n)!}{(2n+1)!}=\frac1{2n+1}$,
$$\|L_n\|^2=(2n)!\cdot\frac{2^{2n+1}(n!)^2}{(2n+1)!}=\frac{(n!)^2\,2^{2n+1}}{2n+1}.$$
Normalising, $\{\sqrt{\tfrac{2n+1}{2}}\,\frac{L_n}{2^n n!}\}$ is an orthonormal basis of $L^2[-1,1]$ ŌĆö a non-trigonometric complete orthonormal system.

items.label: SS 4.5.5 ŌĆö Powers of the golden ratio aren't equidistributed
items.statement: The golden-ratio powers cluster at $0$ and $1$, so they are not equidistributed.
items.formal_statement:
Prove that the sequence $\{\gamma_n\}_{n=1}^{\infty}$, where $\gamma_n$ is the fractional part of
$$\left( \frac{1+\sqrt5}{2}\right)^n,$$
is not equidistributed in $[0,1]$.

Hint: Show that, $$U_n=\left(\frac{1+\sqrt5}{2}\right)^n + \left(\frac{1-\sqrt5}{2}\right)^n$$ is the solution of the difference equation $$U_{r+1}=U_r+U_{r-1} \text{ with } \begin{cases} U_0=2 \\ U_1=1 \end{cases}$$. The $U_n$ satisfy the same difference equation as the Fibonacci numbers.
items.intuition: Algebraic integers betray themselves: $\varphi^n$ is an integer minus a vanishing term, so its fractional parts pile up at the ends rather than spreading out.

solution_steps.label: Solution
solution_steps.content:
Let $\varphi=\frac{1+\sqrt5}2$ and $\psi=\frac{1-\sqrt5}2$, the roots of $x^2=x+1$. Then $U_n=\varphi^n+\psi^n$ satisfies $U_{r+1}=U_r+U_{r-1}$ with $U_0=2,\ U_1=1$, so every $U_n$ is an \emph{integer} (the Lucas numbers). Since $|\psi|=\frac{\sqrt5-1}2\approx0.618<1$, we have $\psi^n\to0$, and
$$\varphi^n=U_n-\psi^n\quad\Longrightarrow\quad \langle\varphi^n\rangle=\langle-\psi^n\rangle\pmod1.$$
Now $\psi<0$, so $\psi^n>0$ for even $n$ and $<0$ for odd $n$, both $\to0$:
$$n\ \text{even}:\ \langle\varphi^n\rangle=1-\psi^n\to1^-,\qquad n\ \text{odd}:\ \langle\varphi^n\rangle=-\psi^n=|\psi|^n\to0^+.$$
Thus the sequence accumulates only at the endpoints $0$ and $1$ and never enters, say, $[\tfrac14,\tfrac34]$ for large $n$. Hence it is far from equidistributed (the limiting proportion in $[\tfrac14,\tfrac34]$ is $0\neq\tfrac12$).

items.label: SS 4.5.7 ŌĆö Weyl's criterion (necessity)
items.statement: Equidistribution implies the vanishing of all nonzero Weyl exponential sums.
items.formal_statement:
Prove the second part of Weyl's criterion: if a sequence of numbers $\xi_1,\xi_2,\dots$ in $[0,1)$ is equidistributed, then for every $k\in\mathbb Z\setminus\{0\}$,
$$\frac1N\sum_{n=1}^{N} e^{2\pi i k\xi_n}\to 0 \text{ as } N \to \infty.$$ Hint: It suffices to show that $$\frac1N\sum_{n=1}^{N}f(\xi_n)\to\int_0^1 f(x)\,dx$$ for all continuous $f$. Prove this first when $f$ is the characteristic function of an interval.
items.intuition: Equidistribution is exactly 'sample-average = integral' for indicators; squeezing continuous functions between step functions promotes it to all continuous test functions, the exponentials included.

solution_steps.label: Solution
solution_steps.content:
Equidistribution means, for every subinterval $[a,b)\subset[0,1)$,
$$\frac1N\sum_{n=1}^N\chi_{[a,b)}(\xi_n)\to b-a=\int_0^1\chi_{[a,b)}.$$
By linearity this extends to all step functions. Given a continuous $1$-periodic $f$ and $\varepsilon>0$, choose step functions $s_-\le f\le s_+$ with $\int_0^1(s_+-s_-)<\varepsilon$ (uniform continuity). Then
$$\int_0^1 f-\varepsilon\le\liminf_N\frac1N\sum f(\xi_n)\le\limsup_N\frac1N\sum f(\xi_n)\le\int_0^1 f+\varepsilon,$$
so $\frac1N\sum_{n\le N}f(\xi_n)\to\int_0^1 f$ for every continuous $f$. Applying this to the continuous function $f(x)=e^{2\pi ikx}$, whose integral $\int_0^1 e^{2\pi ikx}dx=0$ for $k\neq0$, gives
$$\frac1N\sum_{n=1}^N e^{2\pi ik\xi_n}\to0.$$

items.label: SS 4.5.8 ŌĆö $\langle an^\sigma\rangle$ is equidistributed $(0<\sigma<1)$
items.statement: Fractional parts of $an^\sigma$ ($0<\sigma<1$) are equidistributed.
items.formal_statement:
Show that for any $a\ne0$ and any $\sigma$ with $0<\sigma<1$, the sequence $\langle an^\sigma
angle$ is equidistributed in $[0,1)$.

Hint: Prove that
$$\sum_{n=1}^{N}e^{2\pi i b n^\sigma}=O(N^\sigma)+O(N^{1-\sigma})\qquad(b\ne0).$$
In fact, note the following estimate:
$$\sum_{n=1}^{N} e^{2\pi i b n^\sigma}-\int_1^N e^{2\pi i b x^\sigma}\,dx
=O\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} n^{-1+\sigma}\right).$$
items.intuition: Sub-linear, slowly turning phases dephase: the Weyl sum is dwarfed by $N$ because neither the discretisation error ($N^\sigma$) nor the oscillatory integral ($N^{1-\sigma}$) keeps up.

solution_steps.label: Argument
solution_steps.content: By Weyl's criterion it suffices to show $\frac1N\sum_{n=1}^N e^{2\pi i b n^\sigma}\to0$ for every $b\neq0$ (with $b=ka$, $k\neq0$). Compare the sum with the integral $I_N=\int_1^N e^{2\pi i b x^\sigma}dx$.

solution_steps.label: Sum vs. integral
solution_steps.content:
Writing $\phi(x)=e^{2\pi ibx^\sigma}$, $|\phi'(x)|=2\pi|b|\sigma x^{\sigma-1}$, so
$$\Big|\sum_{n=1}^N\phi(n)-\int_1^N\phi(x)\,dx\Big|\le\sum_{n=1}^N\sup_{[n,n+1]}|\phi'|=O\Big(\sum_{n=1}^N n^{\sigma-1}\Big)=O(N^\sigma).$$

solution_steps.label: The integral
solution_steps.content:
The phase derivative $2\pi b\sigma x^{\sigma-1}$ is monotone, so by van der Corput's lemma (integration by parts),
$$|I_N|\le\frac{C}{\min_x|2\pi b\sigma x^{\sigma-1}|}=\frac{C}{2\pi|b|\sigma\,N^{\sigma-1}}=O(N^{1-\sigma}).$$

solution_steps.label: Conclusion
solution_steps.content: Hence $\sum_{n=1}^N e^{2\pi ib n^\sigma}=O(N^\sigma)+O(N^{1-\sigma})$. Both exponents are $<1$, so dividing by $N$ gives $\to0$. By Weyl, $\langle an^\sigma\rangle$ is equidistributed.

items.label: SS 4.5.9 ŌĆö Ō¤©a log nŌ¤® is never equidistributed
items.statement: Fractional parts of $a\log n$ fail to equidistribute for every $a$.
items.formal_statement:
In contrast with the result in Exercise 8, prove that $\langle a\log n
angle$ is not equidistributed for any $a$.

Hint: Compare the sum
$$\sum_{n=1}^{N}e^{2\pi i b\log n}$$
with the corresponding integral.
items.intuition: A logarithmic phase turns too slowly: the Weyl sum keeps a fixed-size rotating tail instead of averaging away, so the orbit is biased.

solution_steps.label: Solution
solution_steps.content:
Test Weyl's criterion with $k=1$, so we examine $\frac1N\sum_{n=1}^N e^{2\pi i a\log n}=\frac1N\sum_{n=1}^N n^{2\pi i a}$. Compare with the integral $\int_1^N x^{2\pi ia}\,dx$; the difference is $O(\sup|\frac{d}{dx}x^{2\pi ia}|\cdot N)=O(1)$ relative error after dividing by $N$ ŌĆö more precisely EulerŌĆōMaclaurin gives
$$\sum_{n=1}^N n^{2\pi ia}=\int_1^N x^{2\pi ia}\,dx+O(1)=\frac{N^{1+2\pi ia}-1}{1+2\pi ia}+O(1).$$
Therefore
$$\frac1N\sum_{n=1}^N n^{2\pi ia}=\frac{N^{2\pi ia}}{1+2\pi ia}+o(1).$$
The factor $N^{2\pi ia}=e^{2\pi ia\log N}$ has modulus $1$ and rotates around the circle as $N\to\infty$, so the right-hand side has modulus $\to\frac1{|1+2\pi ia|}\neq0$ and does \emph{not} converge to $0$. Weyl's criterion fails, so $\langle a\log n\rangle$ is not equidistributed.

items.label: SS 4.5.10 ŌĆö Averaging along an equidistributed sequence
items.statement: Averages of a mean-zero periodic function along an equidistributed sequence vanish.
items.formal_statement:
Suppose that $f$ is a periodic function on $\mathbb R$ of period $1$, and $\{\xi_n\}$ is a sequence which is equidistributed in $[0,1)$. Prove the following.

	extbf{(a)} If $f$ is continuous and satisfies
$$\int_0^1 f(x)\,dx=0,$$
then
$$\lim_{N\to\infty}\frac1N\sum_{n=1}^{N}f(x+\xi_n)=0$$
uniformly in $x$.

Hint: Establish this result first for trigonometric polynomials.

	extbf{(b)} If $f$ is merely integrable on $[0,1]$ and satisfies
$$\int_0^1 f(x)\,dx=0,$$
then
$$\lim_{N\to\infty}\int_0^1\left|\frac1N\sum_{n=1}^{N}f(x+\xi_n)\right|^2 dx=0.$$
items.intuition: Equidistribution makes a moving average against a mean-zero profile cancel ŌĆö uniformly when $f$ is smooth (Fej├®r), and in mean-square when $f$ is rough (Parseval + dominated convergence).

solution_steps.label: (a)
solution_steps.content:
First take a trigonometric polynomial $f(y)=\sum_{0<|k|\le M}c_k e^{2\pi iky}$ (no constant term, as $\int f=0$). Then
$$\frac1N\sum_{n=1}^N f(x+\xi_n)=\sum_{0<|k|\le M}c_k e^{2\pi ikx}\Big(\frac1N\sum_{n=1}^N e^{2\pi ik\xi_n}\Big).$$
Each inner Weyl sum $\to0$ (Exercise 7); the outer sum is finite with $|e^{2\pi ikx}|=1$, so the whole expression $\to0$ \emph{uniformly in $x$}. For general continuous $f$, approximate uniformly by such a polynomial $T$ with $\int T=0$ and $\|f-T\|_\infty<\varepsilon$ (Fej├®r); then $|\frac1N\sum f(x+\xi_n)|\le\|f-T\|_\infty+|\frac1N\sum T(x+\xi_n)|<2\varepsilon$ for large $N$, uniformly in $x$.

solution_steps.label: (b)
solution_steps.content:
Let $g_N(x)=\frac1N\sum_{n=1}^N f(x+\xi_n)$. Its $k$-th Fourier coefficient is $\widehat{g_N}(k)=\hat f(k)\cdot\frac1N\sum_{n=1}^N e^{2\pi ik\xi_n}$. By Parseval,
$$\int_0^1|g_N|^2=\sum_{k\in\mathbb Z}|\hat f(k)|^2\Big|\frac1N\sum_{n=1}^N e^{2\pi ik\xi_n}\Big|^2.$$
The $k=0$ term vanishes ($\hat f(0)=\int f=0$). For $k\neq0$ the bracket $\to0$ (Exercise 7) and is $\le1$; since $\sum_k|\hat f(k)|^2<\infty$ (Bessel), dominated convergence gives $\int_0^1|g_N|^2\to0$.

items.label: SS 4.5.11 ŌĆö Heat flow converges to its data in L┬▓
items.statement: Heat-kernel smoothing converges to the initial data in $L^2$.
items.formal_statement: Show that if $u(x,t)=(f*H_t)(x)$, where $H_t$ is the heat kernel and $f$ is Riemann integrable, then $$\int_0^1 |u(x,t)-f(x)|^2\,dx\to 0 \text{ as } t\to0.$$
items.intuition: On the Fourier side heat flow is the multiplier $e^{-4\pi^2k^2t}\to1$; Parseval turns convergence of the data into termwise convergence dominated by Bessel's bound.

solution_steps.label: Solution
solution_steps.content:
On the circle $H_t(x)=\sum_{k}e^{-4\pi^2k^2t}e^{2\pi ikx}$, so convolution multiplies coefficients: $\widehat{u(\cdot,t)}(k)=\hat f(k)\,e^{-4\pi^2k^2t}$. By Parseval,
$$\int_0^1|u(x,t)-f(x)|^2\,dx=\sum_{k\in\mathbb Z}|\hat f(k)|^2\,\big|e^{-4\pi^2k^2t}-1\big|^2.$$
For each fixed $k$, $e^{-4\pi^2k^2t}-1\to0$ as $t\to0^+$. Moreover $0<e^{-4\pi^2k^2t}\le1$ gives $|e^{-4\pi^2k^2t}-1|^2\le1$, so the summand is dominated by $|\hat f(k)|^2$, which is summable (Bessel, since $f$ is Riemann integrable). By dominated convergence the series $\to0$, i.e.
$$\int_0^1|u(x,t)-f(x)|^2\,dx\to0\quad(t\to0^+).$$
So the heat semigroup recovers the initial data in the mean-square sense for any integrable $f$ ŌĆö even before pointwise statements (which need $f$ continuous and the good-kernel property).

items.label: SS 5.5.2 ŌĆö Transforms of the box and the triangle
items.statement: Fourier transforms of the indicator and the tent function.
items.formal_statement: Show $\widehat{\chi_{[-1,1]}}(\xi)=\frac{\sin2\pi\xi}{\pi\xi}$ and, for $g(x)=(1-|x|)\chi_{[-1,1]}$, $\hat g(\xi)=\big(\frac{\sin\pi\xi}{\pi\xi}\big)^2$ (with $\hat f(0)=2$, $\hat g(0)=1$).
items.intuition: The box transforms to a sinc; convolving the box with itself makes the triangle, which squares the sinc ŌĆö the cleanest illustration of the convolution theorem.

solution_steps.label: Box
solution_steps.content:
$$\widehat{\chi_{[-1,1]}}(\xi)=\int_{-1}^1 e^{-2\pi i x\xi}\,dx=\frac{e^{-2\pi i\xi}-e^{2\pi i\xi}}{-2\pi i\xi}=\frac{\sin2\pi\xi}{\pi\xi},$$
and $\hat f(0)=\int_{-1}^1 1\,dx=2$.

solution_steps.label: Triangle
solution_steps.content:
Since $g$ is even, $\hat g(\xi)=2\int_0^1(1-x)\cos(2\pi\xi x)\,dx$. Using $\int_0^1\cos=\frac{\sin2\pi\xi}{2\pi\xi}$ and $\int_0^1 x\cos(2\pi\xi x)dx=\frac{\sin2\pi\xi}{2\pi\xi}+\frac{\cos2\pi\xi-1}{(2\pi\xi)^2}$, the $\sin$ terms cancel and
$$\hat g(\xi)=2\cdot\frac{1-\cos2\pi\xi}{(2\pi\xi)^2}=\frac{2\cdot2\sin^2\pi\xi}{4\pi^2\xi^2}=\Big(\frac{\sin\pi\xi}{\pi\xi}\Big)^2,$$
with $\hat g(0)=1$. (Equivalently $g=\chi_{[-1/2,1/2]}*\chi_{[-1/2,1/2]}$, so $\hat g=(\widehat{\chi_{[-1/2,1/2]}})^2$.)

items.label: SS 5.5.3a ŌĆö Coefficient decay Ō¤╣ H├Člder regularity
items.statement: Spectral decay $O(|\xi|^{-1-\alpha})$ yields H├Člder-$\alpha$ continuity of $f$.
items.formal_statement: If $f$ has moderate decrease, $\hat f$ is continuous and $\hat f(\xi)=O(1/|\xi|^{1+\alpha})$ for some $0<\alpha<1$, prove $f$ is H├Člder-$\alpha$: $|f(x+h)-f(x)|\le M|h|^\alpha$.
items.intuition: Inversion writes a finite difference as a frequency integral against $e^{2\pi ih\xi}-1$; balancing the two regimes at $|\xi|\sim1/|h|$ converts spectral decay into a modulus of continuity.

solution_steps.label: Argument
solution_steps.content:
By Fourier inversion, $f(x)=\int_{\mathbb R}\hat f(\xi)e^{2\pi ix\xi}\,d\xi$, so
$$f(x+h)-f(x)=\int_{\mathbb R}\hat f(\xi)e^{2\pi ix\xi}\big(e^{2\pi ih\xi}-1\big)\,d\xi,\qquad |f(x+h)-f(x)|\le\int_{\mathbb R}|\hat f(\xi)|\,|e^{2\pi ih\xi}-1|\,d\xi.$$
Split at $|\xi|=1/|h|$ and use $|\hat f(\xi)|\le C|\xi|^{-1-\alpha}$:

solution_steps.label: Low frequencies
solution_steps.content:
$|\xi|\le1/|h|$: with $|e^{2\pi ih\xi}-1|\le2\pi|h||\xi|$,
$$\int_{|\xi|\le1/|h|}\!\!\!|\hat f||e^{2\pi ih\xi}-1|\le2\pi|h|\,C\!\int_{|\xi|\le1/|h|}\!\!|\xi|^{-\alpha}d\xi=2\pi C|h|\cdot\frac{2}{1-\alpha}|h|^{-(1-\alpha)}=C_1|h|^\alpha.$$

solution_steps.label: High frequencies
solution_steps.content:
$|\xi|\ge1/|h|$: with $|e^{2\pi ih\xi}-1|\le2$,
$$\int_{|\xi|\ge1/|h|}2\,|\hat f|\le 2C\int_{|\xi|\ge1/|h|}|\xi|^{-1-\alpha}d\xi=2C\cdot\frac2\alpha|h|^{\alpha}=C_2|h|^\alpha.$$
Adding, $|f(x+h)-f(x)|\le(C_1+C_2)|h|^\alpha$.

items.label: SS 5.5.4 ŌĆö Bump functions in S(ŌäØ)
items.statement: Construct compactly supported $C^\infty$ bump, ramp, and plateau functions.
items.formal_statement: (a) Show $f(x)=e^{-1/(x-a)}e^{-1/(b-x)}$ on $(a,b)$ (and $0$ outside) is $C^\infty$. (b) Build a $C^\infty$ $F$ with $F=0$ for $x\le a$, $F=1$ for $x\ge b$, strictly increasing on $[a,b]$. (c) Build a $C^\infty$ plateau: $g=0$ off $(a,b)$, $g=1$ on $[a+\delta,b-\delta]$.
items.intuition: $e^{-1/t}$ is the canonical flat-but-nonzero function; integrating it gives smooth ramps, and multiplying two ramps gives a smooth plateau ŌĆö the toolkit for cutoffs and partitions of unity.

solution_steps.label: (a)
solution_steps.content: The one-sided model is $\phi(t)=e^{-1/t}$ for $t>0$, $\phi(t)=0$ for $t\le0$. By induction $\phi^{(k)}(t)=P_k(1/t)e^{-1/t}$ for $t>0$ (a polynomial in $1/t$), and since $e^{-1/t}$ beats every power of $1/t$, all one-sided derivatives at $0$ vanish: $\phi\in C^\infty(\mathbb R)$. Now $f(x)=\phi(x-a)\,\phi(b-x)$ is a product of $C^\infty$ functions, positive on $(a,b)$ and identically $0$ (with all derivatives) outside ŌĆö hence $f\in C^\infty_c(\mathbb R)\subset\mathcal S(\mathbb R)$.

solution_steps.label: (b)
solution_steps.content: Let $c=\big(\int_a^b f\big)^{-1}$ and $F(x)=c\int_{-\infty}^x f(t)\,dt$. Then $F\in C^\infty$ ($F'=cf\ge0$), $F=0$ for $x\le a$, $F=1$ for $x\ge b$, and $F'=cf>0$ on $(a,b)$ so $F$ is strictly increasing there.

solution_steps.label: (c)
solution_steps.content: With $\delta>0$ small ($a+\delta<b-\delta$), take $g(x)=F_{a,a+\delta}(x)\,\big(1-F_{b-\delta,b}(x)\big)$ where $F_{p,q}$ is the part-(b) ramp on $[p,q]$. Then $g=0$ off $(a,b)$, $g\equiv1$ on $[a+\delta,b-\delta]$, and $g$ is $C^\infty$ and strictly monotone on the two transition zones.

items.label: SS 5.5.5 ŌĆö F╠é continuous, vanishing, and injectivity
items.statement: Continuity, decay, and injectivity of the Fourier transform on moderate-decrease functions.
items.formal_statement: Let $f$ be continuous of moderate decrease. (a) Prove $\hat f$ is continuous and $\hat f(\xi)\to0$ as $|\xi|\to\infty$. (b) If $\hat f\equiv0$, then $f\equiv0$.
items.intuition: The half-shift trick is RiemannŌĆōLebesgue in disguise; the multiplication formula then turns 'all moments against Schwartz functions vanish' into injectivity of the transform.

solution_steps.label: (a) Continuity
solution_steps.content:
$|f(x)e^{-2\pi ix\xi}|\le|f(x)|\in L^1$ (moderate decrease), and $\xi\mapsto f(x)e^{-2\pi ix\xi}$ is continuous; dominated convergence gives $\hat f$ continuous. \textbf{Decay.} Using $e^{-2\pi ix\xi}=-e^{-2\pi i(x-1/(2\xi))\xi}$ (since $e^{\pi i}=-1$), substitute $x\mapsto x-1/(2\xi)$ in half of the integral:
$$\hat f(\xi)=\tfrac12\int_{\mathbb R}\big[f(x)-f\big(x-\tfrac1{2\xi}\big)\big]e^{-2\pi ix\xi}\,dx.$$
As $|\xi|\to\infty$, $f(x)-f(x-\frac1{2\xi})\to0$ pointwise and is dominated by an $L^1$ function (moderate decrease + continuity), so $\hat f(\xi)\to0$ ŌĆö the RiemannŌĆōLebesgue lemma.

solution_steps.label: (b)
solution_steps.content: The multiplication formula $\int_{\mathbb R}f(x)\hat g(x)\,dx=\int_{\mathbb R}\hat f(y)g(y)\,dy$ holds for $g\in\mathcal S$ (Fubini). If $\hat f\equiv0$ then $\int f\hat g=0$ for all $g\in\mathcal S$. But $\{\hat g:g\in\mathcal S\}=\mathcal S$, which is rich enough (e.g. approximate identities) to force $f=0$ at every point of continuity; since $f$ is continuous, $f\equiv0$.

items.label: SS 5.5.6 ŌĆö Self-dual functions and FŌü┤ = I
items.statement: The Fourier transform satisfies $\mathcal F^4=I$; its eigenvalues are the 4th roots of unity.
items.formal_statement: $e^{-\pi x^2}$ is its own Fourier transform. Generate other (essentially) self-dual functions. Prove $\mathcal F^4=I$ and determine the possible eigenvalue constants.
items.intuition: Two transforms reflect, four restore ŌĆö so the Fourier transform is an order-4 unitary, its spectrum confined to the fourth roots of unity, with Hermite functions as the eigenbasis.

solution_steps.label: $\mathcal F^4=I$
solution_steps.content: For Schwartz $f$, Fourier inversion says $\mathcal F^2 f(x)=f(-x)$ (transforming twice reflects). Applying it again, $\mathcal F^4 f(x)=\mathcal F^2 f(-x)=f(x)$, so $\mathcal F^4=I$.

solution_steps.label: Eigenvalues
solution_steps.content: If $\mathcal F f=\lambda f$ with $f\neq0$, then $\lambda^4 f=\mathcal F^4 f=f$, so $\lambda^4=1$: the only possible eigenvalues are $\{1,i,-1,-i\}$ ŌĆö these are the constants that can occur.

solution_steps.label: Eigenfunctions
solution_steps.content: The Gaussian $h_0=e^{-\pi x^2}$ has $\mathcal F h_0=h_0$ ($\lambda=1$). The raising operator $(x-\frac1{2\pi}\frac{d}{dx})$ generates the Hermite functions $h_n$, and one checks $\mathcal F h_n=(-i)^n h_n$. Thus eigenvalue $1$ is hit by $h_0,h_4,\dots$; eigenvalue $-1$ by $h_2,h_6,\dots$, etc. Concretely $\big(x^2-\frac1{4\pi^2}\big)e^{-\pi x^2}$-type combinations are self-dual up to sign.

items.label: SS 5.5.7 ŌĆö Convolution preserves moderate decrease
items.statement: $\mathcal M(\mathbb R)$ is closed under convolution.
items.formal_statement: Prove that the convolution of two functions of moderate decrease is again of moderate decrease.
items.intuition: Whichever factor is far from the bulk supplies the $1/x^2$ tail; splitting the integral by which variable is large makes the decay transfer to the convolution.

solution_steps.label: Argument
solution_steps.content:
Let $|f(x)|,|g(x)|\le\frac{A}{1+x^2}$. Split the convolution at $|y|=|x|/2$:
$$(f*g)(x)=\int_{|y|\le|x|/2}f(x-y)g(y)\,dy+\int_{|y|\ge|x|/2}f(x-y)g(y)\,dy.$$

solution_steps.label: First integral:
solution_steps.content:
$|y|\le|x|/2\Rightarrow|x-y|\ge|x|/2$, so $|f(x-y)|\le\frac{A}{1+(x/2)^2}=O\big(\frac1{1+x^2}\big)$. Pulling this bound out,
$$\Big|\int_{|y|\le|x|/2}\Big|\le O\Big(\frac1{1+x^2}\Big)\int_{\mathbb R}|g|=O\Big(\frac1{1+x^2}\Big).$$

solution_steps.label: Second integral:
solution_steps.content: $|y|\ge|x|/2\Rightarrow|g(y)|\le\frac{A}{1+(x/2)^2}=O\big(\frac1{1+x^2}\big)$, so likewise $\big|\int_{|y|\ge|x|/2}\big|\le O(\frac1{1+x^2})\int|f|$. Hence $(f*g)(x)=O(1/(1+x^2))$; continuity of $f*g$ is standard (dominated convergence). So $f*g$ has moderate decrease.

items.label: SS 5.5.15 ŌĆö Poisson summation gives the cosecant┬▓ identity
items.statement: Poisson summation yields $\sum(n+\alpha)^{-2}=\pi^2/\sin^2\pi\alpha$ and the cotangent sum.
items.formal_statement: (a) Apply Poisson summation to $g$ of Exercise 2 to get $\sum_{n}\frac1{(n+\alpha)^2}=\frac{\pi^2}{\sin^2\pi\alpha}$ ($\alpha\notin\mathbb Z$). (b) Deduce $\sum_n\frac1{n+\alpha}=\frac{\pi}{\tan\pi\alpha}$.
items.intuition: Poisson summation trades a one-term left side (the tent samples to a spike) for the full $1/(n+\alpha)^2$ series ŌĆö recovering Euler's cosecant identity, whose integral is the cotangent.

solution_steps.label: (a)
solution_steps.content:
Apply Poisson summation in the modulated form $\sum_n g(n)e^{-2\pi i\alpha n}=\sum_n\hat g(n+\alpha)$ (modulation shifts the transform). With $g(x)=(1-|x|)\chi_{[-1,1]}$ we have $\hat g(\xi)=\big(\frac{\sin\pi\xi}{\pi\xi}\big)^2$ (Ex. 2). The left side collapses since $g(n)=0$ for $|n|\ge1$ and $g(0)=1$: LHS $=1$. On the right, $\sin\pi(n+\alpha)=(-1)^n\sin\pi\alpha$, so
$$1=\sum_n\frac{\sin^2\pi(n+\alpha)}{\pi^2(n+\alpha)^2}=\frac{\sin^2\pi\alpha}{\pi^2}\sum_n\frac1{(n+\alpha)^2}\ \Longrightarrow\ \sum_{n}\frac1{(n+\alpha)^2}=\frac{\pi^2}{\sin^2\pi\alpha}.$$

solution_steps.label: (b)
solution_steps.content:
The function $\alpha\mapsto\pi\cot\pi\alpha$ satisfies $\frac{d}{d\alpha}(\pi\cot\pi\alpha)=-\frac{\pi^2}{\sin^2\pi\alpha}=-\sum_n\frac1{(n+\alpha)^2}=\frac{d}{d\alpha}\sum_n\frac{-1}{n+\alpha}$ (differentiating the symmetric sum termwise). Both sides agree up to a constant; matching the odd symmetry about $\alpha=\tfrac12$ (or evaluating at $\alpha=\tfrac12$, where both vanish) fixes the constant to $0$:
$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^\infty\frac1{n+\alpha}=\pi\cot\pi\alpha=\frac{\pi}{\tan\pi\alpha}\qquad(\text{summed symmetrically}).$$

items.label: SS 5.5.16 ŌĆö Periodizing the line Dirichlet kernel
items.statement: The periodization of the line Dirichlet kernel is the modified periodic kernel.
items.formal_statement: For $D_R(x)=\frac{\sin2\pi Rx}{\pi x}$ (the line Dirichlet kernel) and integer $N$, show $\sum_n D_N(x+n)=D_N^*(x)$, the modified periodic Dirichlet kernel $D_N^*(x)=\sum_{|n|\le N-1}e^{2\pi inx}+\tfrac12(e^{-2\pi iNx}+e^{2\pi iNx})$.
items.intuition: Poisson summation samples a transform on the integers; periodizing the line Dirichlet kernel just reads off $\chi_{[-N,N]}$ at integers, with the half-weights at $\pm N$ producing the 'modified' kernel.

solution_steps.label: Solution
solution_steps.content:
$D_R$ is the inverse transform of $\chi_{[-R,R]}$: indeed $\int_{-R}^R e^{2\pi ix\xi}d\xi=\frac{\sin2\pi Rx}{\pi x}=D_R(x)$, so $\widehat{D_R}=\chi_{[-R,R]}$. Poisson summation gives
$$\sum_{n}D_N(x+n)=\sum_{k}\widehat{D_N}(k)\,e^{2\pi ikx}=\sum_{k}\chi_{[-N,N]}(k)\,e^{2\pi ikx}.$$
For integer $N$ the indicator $\chi_{[-N,N]}$ equals $1$ at the interior integers $|k|\le N-1$ and takes the boundary value at $k=\pm N$. Summing the defining integral symmetrically assigns each endpoint weight $\tfrac12$ (the value of a jump indicator at its discontinuity), so
$$\sum_n D_N(x+n)=\sum_{|k|\le N-1}e^{2\pi ikx}+\tfrac12\big(e^{-2\pi iNx}+e^{2\pi iNx}\big)=D_N^*(x).$$

items.label: SS 5.5.17 ŌĆö The Gamma function
items.statement: Convergence, the functional equation, and special values of $\Gamma$.
items.formal_statement: For $\Gamma(s)=\int_0^\infty e^{-x}x^{s-1}dx$: (a) the integral converges for $s>0$; (b) $\Gamma(s+1)=s\Gamma(s)$, so $\Gamma(n+1)=n!$; (c) $\Gamma(1/2)=\sqrt\pi$, $\Gamma(3/2)=\frac{\sqrt\pi}2$.
items.intuition: Gamma interpolates the factorial through the functional equation $\Gamma(s+1)=s\Gamma(s)$, and the Gaussian integral pins the half-integer values.

solution_steps.label: (a)
solution_steps.content: Near $0$, $e^{-x}x^{s-1}\le x^{s-1}$ and $\int_0^1 x^{s-1}dx=\frac1s<\infty$ (needs $s>0$). Near $\infty$, $e^{-x}x^{s-1}$ decays faster than any power, so $\int_1^\infty<\infty$. Both one-sided limits exist; $\Gamma(s)$ is well defined for $s>0$.

solution_steps.label: (b)
solution_steps.content:
Integrate by parts:
$$\Gamma(s+1)=\int_0^\infty e^{-x}x^{s}\,dx=\big[-e^{-x}x^s\big]_0^\infty+s\int_0^\infty e^{-x}x^{s-1}\,dx=s\,\Gamma(s).$$
Since $\Gamma(1)=\int_0^\infty e^{-x}dx=1$, induction gives $\Gamma(n+1)=n!$.

solution_steps.label: (c)
solution_steps.content:
Substituting $x=\pi u^2$ ($dx=2\pi u\,du$),
$$\Gamma(\tfrac12)=\int_0^\infty e^{-x}x^{-1/2}dx=\int_0^\infty e^{-\pi u^2}\frac{2\pi u}{\sqrt{\pi}u}\,du=2\sqrt\pi\int_0^\infty e^{-\pi u^2}du=2\sqrt\pi\cdot\tfrac12=\sqrt\pi,$$
using $\int_{-\infty}^\infty e^{-\pi u^2}du=1$. Then $\Gamma(\tfrac32)=\tfrac12\Gamma(\tfrac12)=\frac{\sqrt\pi}2$.

items.label: SS 5.5.18 ŌĆö The Riemann $\zeta$ŌĆō$\theta$ŌĆō$\Gamma$ integral identity
items.statement: The Mellin-transform identity linking $\zeta$, $\vartheta$, and $\Gamma$.
items.formal_statement: For $s>1$ verify $\pi^{-s/2}\Gamma(s/2)\zeta(s)=\frac12\int_0^\infty t^{s/2-1}(\vartheta(t)-1)\,dt$, where $\vartheta(t)=\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}e^{-\pi n^2 t}$.
items.intuition: Each $n^{-s}$ is a Gamma integral in disguise; summing them assembles $\zeta$, while the tail $\vartheta-1$ packages the whole series ŌĆö the bridge from theta's modularity to $\zeta$'s functional equation.

solution_steps.label: Computation
solution_steps.content:
For each $n\ge1$ substitute $u=\pi n^2 t$ in the Gamma integral:
$$\int_0^\infty t^{s/2-1}e^{-\pi n^2 t}\,dt=(\pi n^2)^{-s/2}\int_0^\infty u^{s/2-1}e^{-u}\,du=\pi^{-s/2}\Gamma(\tfrac s2)\,n^{-s}.$$
Now $\vartheta(t)-1=2\sum_{n\ge1}e^{-\pi n^2 t}$, so
$$\frac12\int_0^\infty t^{s/2-1}(\vartheta(t)-1)\,dt=\sum_{n\ge1}\int_0^\infty t^{s/2-1}e^{-\pi n^2 t}\,dt=\pi^{-s/2}\Gamma(\tfrac s2)\sum_{n\ge1}n^{-s}=\pi^{-s/2}\Gamma(\tfrac s2)\zeta(s).$$
Interchanging sum and integral is justified for $s>1$ by Tonelli (all terms positive, total finite).

solution_steps.label: Step 2
solution_steps.content: This integral is the gateway to the analytic continuation and functional equation of $\zeta$, via the modular transformation $\vartheta(1/t)=\sqrt t\,\vartheta(t)$.

items.label: SS 5.5.23 ŌĆö Heisenberg via the Hermite operator
items.statement: Operator (creation/annihilation) proof of the Heisenberg uncertainty principle.
items.formal_statement: Let $L=-\frac{d^2}{dx^2}+x^2$. (a) Show Heisenberg implies $(Lf,f)\ge(f,f)$ (i.e. $L\ge I$). (b) With $A=\frac{d}{dx}+x$, $A^*=-\frac{d}{dx}+x$, prove $(Af,g)=(f,A^*g)$, $A^*A=L-I\ge0$. (c) Using $A_t=\frac{d}{dx}+tx$, give the operator proof that $\big(\int x^2|f|^2\big)\big(\int|f'|^2\big)\ge\frac14$ when $\|f\|_2=1$.
items.intuition: Factoring the Hermite operator as $A^*A+I$ makes positivity automatic; the $t$-family turns the uncertainty principle into 'a nonnegative quadratic has nonpositive discriminant'.

solution_steps.label: (a)
solution_steps.content:
Integrating by parts, $(Lf,f)=\int(-f''+x^2f)\overline f=\int|f'|^2+\int x^2|f|^2=\|f'\|_2^2+\|xf\|_2^2$. By AMŌĆōGM and the core inequality $\|f\|_2^2\le2\|xf\|_2\|f'\|_2$ (the Heisenberg step),
$$(Lf,f)=\|f'\|_2^2+\|xf\|_2^2\ge2\|f'\|_2\|xf\|_2\ge\|f\|_2^2=(f,f).$$

solution_steps.label: (b)
solution_steps.content: \emph{(i)} $(Af,g)=\int(f'+xf)\overline g=\int f\,\overline{(-g'+xg)}=(f,A^*g)$ (boundary terms vanish for Schwartz $f,g$). \emph{(iii)} Compute $A^*A f=(-\frac{d}{dx}+x)(f'+xf)=-f''-(xf)'+xf'+x^2f=-f''+x^2f-f=(L-I)f$. \emph{(ii)} Hence $(A^*Af,f)=(Af,Af)=\|Af\|^2\ge0$, so $L-I=A^*A\ge0$, i.e. $L\ge I$ again.

solution_steps.label: (c)
solution_steps.content:
For real $t$, $0\le\|A_tf\|_2^2=\int|f'+txf|^2=\|f'\|_2^2+t^2\|xf\|_2^2+t\int x(|f|^2)'$. Since $\int x(|f|^2)'=-\int|f|^2=-\|f\|_2^2$ (parts), this is the quadratic in $t$
$$q(t)=\|xf\|_2^2\,t^2-\|f\|_2^2\,t+\|f'\|_2^2\ge0\quad\forall t.$$
A nonnegative quadratic has discriminant $\le0$: $\|f\|_2^4\le4\|xf\|_2^2\|f'\|_2^2$. With $\|f\|_2=1$, $\|xf\|_2^2\|f'\|_2^2\ge\frac14$, and via $\|f'\|_2=2\pi\|\xi\hat f\|_2$ this is the Heisenberg uncertainty bound $\ge\frac1{16\pi^2}$.

items.label: SS 5.6.1 ŌĆö A logarithmic variant of the heat equation
items.statement: Solve an Euler-type heat equation by reduction to the standard heat equation.
items.formal_statement: Solve $x^2u_{xx}+ax\,u_x=u_t$ on $x>0$, $t>0$, with $u(x,0)=f(x)$, via $x=e^{-y}$. Show $u(x,t)=\frac1{(4\pi t)^{1/2}}\int_0^\infty e^{-(\log(v/x)+(1-a)t)^2/(4t)}f(v)\,\frac{dv}{v}$.
items.intuition: A logarithmic change of variable turns the Euler-type operator $x^2\partial_x^2+ax\partial_x$ into the constant-coefficient heat operator, so the solution is Gaussian smoothing in $\log x$.

solution_steps.label: Reduction
solution_steps.content:
Put $x=e^{-y}$ ($-\infty<y<\infty$), $U(y,t)=u(e^{-y},t)$, $F(y)=f(e^{-y})$. Since $x\partial_x=-\partial_y$ and $x^2\partial_x^2=\partial_y^2+\partial_y$, the equation becomes the constant-coefficient
$$U_{yy}+(1-a)U_y=U_t,\qquad U(y,0)=F(y).$$

solution_steps.label: Fourier transform in $y$
solution_steps.content:
With $\hat U(\xi,t)=\int U(y,t)e^{-2\pi iy\xi}dy$, the symbols give $\hat U_t=\big(-4\pi^2\xi^2+(1-a)2\pi i\xi\big)\hat U$, so
$$\hat U(\xi,t)=\hat F(\xi)\,e^{(-4\pi^2\xi^2+(1-a)2\pi i\xi)t}.$$
The multiplier is the transform of a shifted Gaussian: $\int_{\mathbb R}e^{(-4\pi^2\xi^2+(1-a)2\pi i\xi)t}e^{2\pi i\xi v}d\xi=\frac1{\sqrt{4\pi t}}\,e^{-(v+(1-a)t)^2/(4t)}$ (complete the square). Hence $U=F*G_t$ with $G_t(v)=\frac1{\sqrt{4\pi t}}e^{-(v+(1-a)t)^2/(4t)}$:
$$U(y,t)=\frac1{\sqrt{4\pi t}}\int_{\mathbb R}e^{-(y'+(1-a)t)^2/(4t)}F(y-y')\,dy'.$$

solution_steps.label: Back-substitute
solution_steps.content:
$y=-\log x$, $F(y-y')=f(e^{-(y-y')})=f(x e^{y'})$, and set $v=xe^{y'}$ so $y'=\log(v/x)$, $dy'=dv/v$:
$$u(x,t)=\frac1{(4\pi t)^{1/2}}\int_0^\infty e^{-(\log(v/x)+(1-a)t)^2/(4t)}f(v)\,\frac{dv}{v}.$$

items.label: SS 5.6.2 ŌĆö The BlackŌĆōScholes equation
items.statement: Reduce BlackŌĆōScholes to the heat equation and read off the option price.
items.formal_statement: Reduce the BlackŌĆōScholes equation $V_t+rsV_s+\frac{\sigma^2s^2}2V_{ss}-rV=0$, $V(s,T)=F(s)$, to the heat equation via $V=e^{ax+b\tau}U$, $x=\log s$, $\tau=\frac{\sigma^2}2(T-t)$, and write the solution $V(s,t)$.
items.intuition: Option pricing is heat diffusion in disguise: log-price plus a discounting/drift gauge transform turns BlackŌĆōScholes into $U_\tau=U_{xx}$, whose Gaussian kernel is the log-normal risk-neutral density.

solution_steps.label: Change of variables
solution_steps.content:
Let $x=\log s$ and $\tau=\frac{\sigma^2}2(T-t)$ (so $\tau$ runs forward from $0$ at $t=T$). In the $x$ variable, $s\partial_s=\partial_x$ and $s^2\partial_s^2=\partial_x^2-\partial_x$; the final condition becomes an initial condition at $\tau=0$. Substituting $V=e^{ax+b\tau}U(x,\tau)$ with
$$a=\tfrac12-\tfrac r{\sigma^2},\qquad b=-\Big(\tfrac12+\tfrac r{\sigma^2}\Big)^2,$$
the first-order ($V_s$) and zeroth-order ($-rV$) terms are absorbed, leaving the pure heat equation
$$U_\tau=U_{xx},\qquad U(x,0)=e^{-ax}F(e^x).$$

solution_steps.label: Solve and invert
solution_steps.content:
The heat solution is $U(x,\tau)=\frac1{\sqrt{4\pi\tau}}\int_{\mathbb R}e^{-(x-x')^2/(4\tau)}e^{-ax'}F(e^{x'})\,dx'$. Undoing the substitutions ($x=\log s$, $4\tau=2\sigma^2(T-t)$, $s^*=e^{x'}$, $dx'=ds^*/s^*$) and simplifying the exponential prefactors gives
$$V(s,t)=\frac{e^{-r(T-t)}}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2(T-t)}}\int_0^\infty e^{-\big(\log(s/s^*)+(r-\sigma^2/2)(T-t)\big)^2/\big(2\sigma^2(T-t)\big)}\,F(s^*)\,ds^*.$$
The kernel is log-normal: the discounted ($e^{-r(T-t)}$) expectation of the payoff $F$ under the risk-neutral price distribution.

items.label: SS 6.6.1 ŌĆö Planar rotations as complex multiplication
items.statement: Identify 2D orthogonal matrices with complex multiplication/conjugation.
items.formal_statement: For a $2\times2$ matrix $R=\left(\begin{smallmatrix}a&b\\c&d\end{smallmatrix}\right)$: (a) write $R^t=R^{-1}$, $\det R=\pm1$ as equations in $a,b,c,d$; (b) find $\varphi$ with $a+ib=e^{i\varphi}$; (c) conclude a proper rotation is $z\mapsto e^{i\varphi}z$ and an improper one $z\mapsto e^{i\varphi}\bar z$.
items.intuition: In two dimensions the orthogonal group is just the unit complex numbers: proper rotations are multiplication by $e^{i\varphi}$, improper ones are that composed with conjugation.

solution_steps.label: (a)
solution_steps.content: $R^tR=I$ says the columns are orthonormal: $a^2+c^2=1$, $b^2+d^2=1$, $ab+cd=0$; and $\det R=ad-bc=\pm1$.

solution_steps.label: (b)
solution_steps.content: From orthonormality, $(a,c)$ is a unit vector, so $a^2+c^2=1$ and we may write $a=\cos\varphi$, $c=\sin\varphi$; then $a+ic=e^{i\varphi}$ (relabelling, $a+ib=e^{i\varphi}$ in the proper case below).

solution_steps.label: (c)
solution_steps.content:
\emph{Proper} ($\det=+1$): the conditions force $d=a$, $c=-b$ with $a^2+b^2=1$. Identifying $(x,y)\leftrightarrow z=x+iy$, $R$ acts by
$$(ax+by)+i(-bx+ay)=(a-ib)(x+iy)=e^{i\varphi}z,$$
a rotation by complex multiplication with $|e^{i\varphi}|=1$. \emph{Improper} ($\det=-1$): then $d=-a$, $c=b$, and $R$ acts as $z\mapsto e^{i\varphi}\bar z$ ŌĆö a reflection.

items.label: SS 6.6.2 ŌĆö Every 3D proper rotation has an axis
items.statement: Euler's theorem: every proper 3D rotation fixes an axis.
items.formal_statement: For a proper rotation $R:\mathbb R^3\to\mathbb R^3$: (a) show $p(t)=\det(R-tI)$ is a cubic and there is $\gamma\in S^2$ with $R\gamma=\gamma$; (b) show $R$ restricts to a rotation of the plane $P\perp\gamma$.
items.intuition: A real cubic has a real root and orthogonality pins it to $+1$ ŌĆö so a 3D rotation always fixes an axis and spins the perpendicular plane (Euler's theorem).

solution_steps.label: (a)
solution_steps.content: $p(t)=\det(R-tI)$ is a real cubic with leading term $-t^3$, so $p(t)\to-\infty$ as $t\to+\infty$, while $p(0)=\det R=1>0$. By the intermediate value theorem $p$ has a positive real root $\lambda$, and since $R$ is orthogonal all eigenvalues have modulus $1$, forcing $\lambda=1$. (Indeed the eigenvalues are $\{1,e^{\pm i\theta}\}$, their product being $\det R=1$.) Thus $R-I$ is singular: pick a unit vector $\gamma$ in its kernel, $R\gamma=\gamma$.

solution_steps.label: (b)
solution_steps.content: If $v\perp\gamma$ then $\langle Rv,\gamma\rangle=\langle Rv,R\gamma\rangle=\langle v,\gamma\rangle=0$ (orthogonality of $R$), so $Rv\in P$: $R$ maps $P=\gamma^\perp$ to itself. The restriction $R|_P$ is orthogonal with $\det(R|_P)=\det R/1=1$, hence a planar rotation (Exercise 1).

items.label: SS 6.6.3 ŌĆö Rotation invariance of surface measure
items.statement: Spherical surface measure is invariant under rotations.
items.formal_statement: Using $\int_{\mathbb R^d}F=\int_{S^{d-1}}\int_0^\infty F(r\gamma)r^{d-1}\,dr\,d\sigma(\gamma)$, prove $\int_{S^{d-1}}f(R\gamma)\,d\sigma(\gamma)=\int_{S^{d-1}}f(\gamma)\,d\sigma(\gamma)$ for any rotation $R$ and continuous $f$ on the sphere.
items.intuition: Surface measure inherits rotation invariance from Lebesgue measure: separate radius from direction, and the common radial factor cancels.

solution_steps.label: Solution
solution_steps.content:
Apply the polar formula to $F(x)=g(|x|)f(x/|x|)$ for a fixed radial profile $g\ge0$ with $0<\int_0^\infty g(r)r^{d-1}dr<\infty$:
$$\int_{\mathbb R^d}g(|x|)f(x/|x|)\,dx=\Big(\int_0^\infty g(r)r^{d-1}dr\Big)\Big(\int_{S^{d-1}}f(\gamma)\,d\sigma(\gamma)\Big).$$
Lebesgue measure on $\mathbb R^d$ is rotation invariant, and $|Rx|=|x|$, $(Rx)/|Rx|=R(x/|x|)$, so replacing $x\mapsto R^{-1}x$ shows the left side is unchanged if $f$ is replaced by $f\circ R$. The radial factor $\int_0^\infty g\,r^{d-1}dr$ is the same and nonzero, so cancelling it gives
$$\int_{S^{d-1}}f(R\gamma)\,d\sigma(\gamma)=\int_{S^{d-1}}f(\gamma)\,d\sigma(\gamma).$$

items.label: SS 6.6.4 ŌĆö Area and volume of spheres and balls
items.statement: Compute $A_d=2\pi^{d/2}/\Gamma(d/2)$ and $V_d=\pi^{d/2}/\Gamma(d/2+1)$.
items.formal_statement: With $A_d,V_d$ the area of $S^{d-1}$ and volume of the unit ball: (a) $A_d=\frac{2\pi^{d/2}}{\Gamma(d/2)}$; (b) $dV_d=A_d$, so $V_d=\frac{\pi^{d/2}}{\Gamma(d/2+1)}$.
items.intuition: The Gaussian integral, sliced radially, measures the sphere; the Gamma function carries the dimension, interpolating the familiar $2\pi,4\pi,\dots$ continuously.

solution_steps.label: (a)
solution_steps.content:
Evaluate $\int_{\mathbb R^d}e^{-\pi|x|^2}dx=1$ in polar coordinates:
$$1=\int_{S^{d-1}}\!\!\int_0^\infty e^{-\pi r^2}r^{d-1}\,dr\,d\sigma=A_d\int_0^\infty e^{-\pi r^2}r^{d-1}\,dr.$$
Substituting $u=\pi r^2$, $\int_0^\infty e^{-\pi r^2}r^{d-1}dr=\tfrac12\pi^{-d/2}\Gamma(d/2)$, so $1=A_d\cdot\tfrac12\pi^{-d/2}\Gamma(d/2)$, giving $A_d=\dfrac{2\pi^{d/2}}{\Gamma(d/2)}$ ($A_2=2\pi$, $A_3=4\pi$).

solution_steps.label: (b)
solution_steps.content:
$V_d=\int_0^1 A_d r^{d-1}dr=\dfrac{A_d}{d}$, and $d\,\Gamma(d/2)=2\Gamma(d/2+1)$ gives
$$V_d=\frac{2\pi^{d/2}}{d\,\Gamma(d/2)}=\frac{\pi^{d/2}}{\Gamma(d/2+1)}\quad(V_2=\pi,\ V_3=\tfrac{4\pi}3).$$

items.label: SS 6.6.5 ŌĆö Gaussian integral for a positive-definite form
items.statement: Anisotropic Gaussian integral equals $(\det A)^{-1/2}$.
items.formal_statement: For a real symmetric positive-definite $d\times d$ matrix $A$, show $\int_{\mathbb R^d}e^{-\pi(x,Ax)}dx=(\det A)^{-1/2}$.
items.intuition: Diagonalising the quadratic form decouples the Gaussian into one-dimensional pieces; the eigenvalues multiply into the determinant.

solution_steps.label: Solution
solution_steps.content:
By the spectral theorem write $A=RDR^t$ with $R$ a rotation and $D=\operatorname{diag}(\lambda_1,\dots,\lambda_d)$, $\lambda_i>0$ the eigenvalues. Substitute $y=R^tx$ (Jacobian $|\det R|=1$); since $(x,Ax)=(R^tx,DR^tx)=(y,Dy)=\sum_i\lambda_i y_i^2$, the integral factorises:
$$\int_{\mathbb R^d}e^{-\pi(x,Ax)}dx=\prod_{i=1}^d\int_{\mathbb R}e^{-\pi\lambda_i y_i^2}\,dy_i=\prod_{i=1}^d\lambda_i^{-1/2}=\Big(\prod_i\lambda_i\Big)^{-1/2}=(\det A)^{-1/2},$$
using $\int_{\mathbb R}e^{-\pi\lambda y^2}dy=\lambda^{-1/2}$ and $\det A=\prod_i\lambda_i$.

items.label: SS 6.6.6 ŌĆö Heisenberg uncertainty in d dimensions
items.statement: The $d$-dimensional Heisenberg uncertainty inequality.
items.formal_statement: For $\psi\in\mathcal S(\mathbb R^d)$ with $\int|\psi|^2=1$, show $\big(\int_{\mathbb R^d}|x|^2|\psi|^2\big)\big(\int_{\mathbb R^d}|\xi|^2|\hat\psi|^2\big)\ge\frac{d^2}{16\pi^2}$.
items.intuition: The divergence identity for $x|\psi|^2$ gives a factor $d$ on the left; CauchyŌĆōSchwarz plus Plancherel then spreads the uncertainty over all $d$ coordinates.

solution_steps.label: Solution
solution_steps.content:
Since $\operatorname{div}(x|\psi|^2)=d|\psi|^2+x\cdot
abla|\psi|^2$ integrates to $0$ (Schwartz decay),
$$d=d\int|\psi|^2=-\int x\cdot
abla|\psi|^2\,dx=-2\operatorname{Re}\int(x\cdot
abla\psi)\,\overline\psi\,dx.$$
By CauchyŌĆōSchwarz (and $|x\cdot
abla\psi|\le|x|\,|
abla\psi|$),
$$d\le2\Big(\int|x|^2|\psi|^2\Big)^{1/2}\Big(\int|
abla\psi|^2\Big)^{1/2}.$$
Plancherel coordinate-by-coordinate gives $\int|
abla\psi|^2=\sum_j\int|\partial_j\psi|^2=\sum_j4\pi^2\int\xi_j^2|\hat\psi|^2=4\pi^2\int|\xi|^2|\hat\psi|^2$. Substituting,
$$d\le2\Big(\int|x|^2|\psi|^2\Big)^{1/2}\cdot2\pi\Big(\int|\xi|^2|\hat\psi|^2\Big)^{1/2}\ \Longrightarrow\ \Big(\int|x|^2|\psi|^2\Big)\Big(\int|\xi|^2|\hat\psi|^2\Big)\ge rac{d^2}{16\pi^2}.$$

items.label: SS 6.6.7 ŌĆö The d-dimensional heat kernel solves the heat equation
items.statement: The heat kernel on $\mathbb R^d$ smooths data and attains the initial condition.
items.formal_statement: For $f\in\mathcal S(\mathbb R^d)$ and $H_t^{(d)}(x)=(4\pi t)^{-d/2}e^{-|x|^2/4t}$, show $u=f*H_t^{(d)}$ is $C^\infty$ for $t>0$, solves $u_t=\Delta u$, and is continuous up to $t=0$ with $u(x,0)=f(x)$.
items.intuition: Heat flow is the Fourier multiplier $e^{-4\pi^2t|\xi|^2}$: it smooths instantly (super-polynomial decay), realises $\partial_t=\Delta$, and relaxes to the identity as $t\to0$.

solution_steps.label: Argument
solution_steps.content:
On the Fourier side, $\widehat{H_t^{(d)}}(\xi)=e^{-4\pi^2t|\xi|^2}$, so
$$\hat u(\xi,t)=\hat f(\xi)\,e^{-4\pi^2t|\xi|^2}.$$

solution_steps.label: Smoothness
solution_steps.content: For $t>0$ the factor $e^{-4\pi^2t|\xi|^2}$ decays faster than any polynomial, and $\hat f\in\mathcal S$; hence $\xi^\alpha\partial_t^m\hat u\in L^1$ for all $\alpha,m$, so differentiation under the inversion integral is justified and $u\in C^\infty$.

solution_steps.label: Heat equation
solution_steps.content: Differentiating $\hat u$: $\partial_t\hat u=-4\pi^2|\xi|^2\hat u$, while $\widehat{\Delta u}=-4\pi^2|\xi|^2\hat u$. Since the transform is injective, $\partial_t u=\Delta u$.

solution_steps.label: Boundary value
solution_steps.content: As $t\to0^+$, $\hat u(\xi,t)\to\hat f(\xi)$ pointwise with $|\hat u|\le|\hat f|\in L^1$; by dominated convergence in the inversion formula $u(x,t)\to f(x)$ uniformly. So $u$ is continuous up to $t=0$ with $u(\cdot,0)=f$.

solution_steps.label: Step 5
solution_steps.content: (This is the $\mathbb R^d$ form of the approximate-identity statement; cf. the 2-D exam problem.)

items.label: SS 6.6.9 ŌĆö Spherical waves Ō¤║ radial data
items.statement: Radial initial data produce radial (spherical) solutions of the wave equation, and conversely.
items.formal_statement: A spherical wave is a solution $u(x,t)$ of the wave-equation Cauchy problem in $\mathbb R^d$ that is radial in $x$. Prove $u$ is a spherical wave iff the initial data $f,g\in\mathcal S$ are both radial.
items.intuition: Rotational symmetry passes through the Fourier transform and the radial wave multipliers, so the wave is round exactly when its launch data are round.

solution_steps.label: Argument
solution_steps.content:
The Fourier solution of $u_{tt}=\Delta u$, $u(\cdot,0)=f$, $u_t(\cdot,0)=g$ is
$$\hat u(\xi,t)=\cos(2\pi|\xi|t)\,\hat f(\xi)+\frac{\sin(2\pi|\xi|t)}{2\pi|\xi|}\,\hat g(\xi).$$
A function is radial iff its Fourier transform is radial (rotations commute with $\mathcal F$). The multipliers $\cos(2\pi|\xi|t)$ and $\frac{\sin(2\pi|\xi|t)}{2\pi|\xi|}$ are radial in $\xi$.

solution_steps.label: ($\Leftarrow$)
solution_steps.content: If $f,g$ are radial, then $\hat f,\hat g$ are radial, so $\hat u(\cdot,t)$ is radial for each $t$, hence $u(\cdot,t)$ is radial: $u$ is a spherical wave.

solution_steps.label: ($\Rightarrow$)
solution_steps.content: If $u(\cdot,t)$ is radial for all $t$, then so is $\hat u(\cdot,t)$. Setting $t=0$ gives $\hat f=\hat u(\cdot,0)$ radial; differentiating at $t=0$ gives $\hat g=\partial_t\hat u(\cdot,0)$ radial. Hence $f,g$ are radial.

solution_steps.label: Step 4
solution_steps.content: Equivalently: rotations commute with the wave operator, so by uniqueness $u(R\,\cdot,t)$ solves the problem with data $f(R\cdot),g(R\cdot)$; radial $u$ $\Leftrightarrow$ rotation-invariant data.

items.label: SS 7.3.1 ŌĆö Discrete Fourier coefficients approximate the true ones
items.statement: Discrete Fourier coefficients are $N$-periodic and converge to the true coefficients.
items.formal_statement: For $f$ on the circle let $a_N(n)=\frac1N\sum_{k=1}^N f(e^{2\pi ik/N})e^{-2\pi ikn/N}$ and $a(n)=\int_0^1 f(e^{2\pi ix})e^{-2\pi inx}dx$. (a) Show $a_N(n)=a_N(n+N)$. (b) If $f$ is continuous, $a_N(n)\to a(n)$ as $N\to\infty$.
items.intuition: Sampling on $N$ equally spaced points turns the Fourier integral into a Riemann sum; continuity makes the sum converge, while the sampling forces $N$-periodicity (aliasing).

solution_steps.label: (a)
solution_steps.content:
Replacing $n$ by $n+N$ multiplies the $k$-th term by $e^{-2\pi ikN/N}=e^{-2\pi ik}=1$, so
$$a_N(n+N)=\frac1N\sum_{k=1}^N f(e^{2\pi ik/N})e^{-2\pi ik(n+N)/N}=a_N(n).$$
The discrete spectrum is $N$-periodic (aliasing): only $N$ distinct coefficients exist.

solution_steps.label: (b)
solution_steps.content:
Fix $n$. The quantity $a_N(n)=\frac1N\sum_{k=1}^N\Phi(k/N)$ is precisely the right-endpoint Riemann sum, over the uniform partition of $[0,1]$ into $N$ pieces, of $\Phi(x)=f(e^{2\pi ix})e^{-2\pi inx}$. As $f$ is continuous, $\Phi$ is continuous on the compact circle, hence (uniformly continuous) its Riemann sums converge:
$$a_N(n)\to\int_0^1 f(e^{2\pi ix})e^{-2\pi inx}\,dx=a(n).$$

items.label: SS 7.3.2 ŌĆö C┬╣ decay of discrete coefficients
items.statement: $C^1$ functions have $O(1/|n|)$ discrete Fourier coefficients below Nyquist.
items.formal_statement: If $f$ is $C^1$ on the circle, prove $|a_N(n)|\le c/|n|$ for $0<|n|\le N/2$.
items.intuition: The same shift-and-difference trick as for ordinary coefficients: one derivative buys one power of $1/|n|$, valid up to the Nyquist frequency $N/2$.

solution_steps.label: Solution
solution_steps.content:
Shift the summation index by $\ell$: since the sum is over a full period mod $N$,
$$a_N(n)\big[1-e^{2\pi i\ell n/N}\big]=\frac1N\sum_{k=1}^N\big[f(e^{2\pi ik/N})-f(e^{2\pi i(k+\ell)/N})\big]e^{-2\pi ikn/N}.$$
For $0<|n|\le N/2$ choose the integer $\ell\approx N/(2n)$ so that $\ell n/N$ is as close to $\tfrac12$ as possible; then $|1-e^{2\pi i\ell n/N}|\ge c_0>0$ (bounded away from $0$). Because $f\in C^1$, $|f(e^{2\pi ik/N})-f(e^{2\pi i(k+\ell)/N})|\le \|f'\|_\infty\cdot\frac{2\pi\ell}{N}\le C\,\frac{\ell}{N}\le \frac{C'}{|n|}$. Averaging,
$$|a_N(n)|\,c_0\le\frac{C'}{|n|}\ \Longrightarrow\ |a_N(n)|\le\frac{c}{|n|}.$$

items.label: SS 7.3.3 ŌĆö C┬▓ decay and the discrete inversion formula
items.statement: $C^2$ decay yields the continuous Fourier inversion as the limit of the discrete one.
items.formal_statement: If $f$ is $C^2$ on the circle, show $|a_N(n)|\le c/|n|^2$ for $0<|n|\le N/2$, and deduce the inversion formula $f(e^{2\pi ix})=\sum_{n}a(n)e^{2\pi inx}$ from its finite version.
items.intuition: Two derivatives give summable $1/|n|^2$ decay, which makes the finite DFT inversion pass to the limit ŌĆö the continuous Fourier series emerges from its sampled version.

solution_steps.label: Decay
solution_steps.content:
Use the second symmetric difference: averaging the $+\ell$ and $-\ell$ shifts,
$$a_N(n)\big[2-2\cos(2\pi\ell n/N)\big]=\frac1N\sum_{k=1}^N\big[2f(e^{2\pi ik/N})-f(e^{2\pi i(k+\ell)/N})-f(e^{2\pi i(k-\ell)/N})\big]e^{-2\pi ikn/N}.$$
Choosing $\ell$ with $\ell n/N\approx\tfrac12$ makes $2-2\cos(2\pi\ell n/N)\ge c_0>0$. Since $f\in C^2$, the second difference is $O\big((\ell/N)^2\big)=O(1/|n|^2)$, so $|a_N(n)|\le c/|n|^2$ for $0<|n|\le N/2$.

solution_steps.label: Inversion
solution_steps.content:
The finite Fourier inversion on $\mathbb Z(N)$ (take $N$ odd) reads
$$f(e^{2\pi ik/N})=\sum_{|n|<N/2}a_N(n)\,e^{2\pi ikn/N}.$$
By Exercise 1, $a_N(n)\to a(n)$; by the bound $|a_N(n)|\le c/|n|^2$ (uniform in $N$) the tails are uniformly small, so letting $N\to\infty$ (dominated convergence on the summation) yields
$$f(e^{2\pi ix})=\sum_{n=-\infty}^\infty a(n)\,e^{2\pi inx},$$
the classical Fourier inversion recovered as a limit of the discrete one.

items.label: ├śving 1 ┬Ę P1 ŌĆö Modulus of continuity of C┬╣ functions
items.statement: Bound the modulus of continuity of a $C^1$ function by $\sup|f'|\cdot r$.
items.formal_statement: For $f\in C^1(\mathbb R)$ and $[a,b]$, with $\omega_{f,[a,b]}(r):=\sup\{|f(x)-f(y)|:x,y\in[a,b],\,|x-y|<r\}$, show $\omega_{f,[a,b]}(r)\le\big(\sup_{x\in[a,b]}|f'(x)|\big)\,r$.
items.intuition: A bound on the derivative is a Lipschitz bound: the FTC turns $\sup|f'|$ directly into a linear modulus of continuity.
items.solution:
Let $M=\sup_{[a,b]}|f'|$ (finite, since $f'$ is continuous on the compact $[a,b]$). For any $x,y\in[a,b]$ with $|x-y|<r$, the fundamental theorem of calculus gives
$$|f(x)-f(y)|=\Big|\int_y^x f'(t)\,dt\Big|\le M\,|x-y|<M\,r.$$
Taking the supremum over all such pairs, $\omega_{f,[a,b]}(r)\le M\,r=\big(\sup_{[a,b]}|f'|\big)r$. $\blacksquare$

items.label: ├śving 1 ┬Ę P2 ŌĆö Uniform continuity via P1
items.statement: Deduce uniform continuity of $x^2$ and $1/x$ from the derivative bound.
items.formal_statement: Use P1 to show $f$ is uniformly continuous on $[a,b]$ when (i) $f(x)=x^2$ (any $a,b\in\mathbb R$); (ii) $f(x)=1/x$ (any $a,b$ with $0\notin[a,b]$, including $a=-\infty$, $b=\infty$).
items.intuition: Uniform continuity reduces to a bounded derivative on the set; once $\sup|f'|<\infty$, P1 delivers a modulus independent of location.
items.solution:
\textbf{(i)} On a bounded $[a,b]$, $f(x)=x^2$ has $|f'(x)|=2|x|\le 2\max(|a|,|b|)=:M<\infty$. By P1, $\omega_f(r)\le Mr\to0$ as $r\to0$, which is exactly uniform continuity.

\textbf{(ii)} If $0\notin[a,b]$, let $d=\operatorname{dist}(0,[a,b])>0$. Then $|f'(x)|=1/x^2\le 1/d^2$ on $[a,b]$ (this stays valid on unbounded intervals like $[a,\infty)$ with $a>0$, where $\sup|f'|=1/a^2<\infty$). By P1, $\omega_f(r)\le r/d^2\to0$: uniformly continuous. $\blacksquare$

(Note: on all of $\mathbb R$, $x^2$ is \emph{not} uniformly continuous, and $1/x$ fails near $0$ ŌĆö the boundedness of $f'$ on the chosen set is precisely what P1 needs.)

items.label: ├śving 1 ┬Ę P3 ŌĆö Computing moduli of continuity
items.statement: Compute $\omega_f$ for $\sqrt{1+x^2}$ (linear) and $\sqrt x$ (H├Člder-┬Į).
items.formal_statement: Find $\omega_f$ when (i) $f(x)=\sqrt{1+x^2}$ on $\mathbb R$; (ii) $f(x)=x^{1/2}$ on $[0,1]$.
items.intuition: A bounded derivative gives a linear modulus ($\sqrt{1+x^2}$); an unbounded one near a point gives a genuine H├Člder modulus ($\sqrt x$ has $\omega_f(r)=\sqrt r$).
items.solution:
\textbf{(i)} $f'(x)=\dfrac{x}{\sqrt{1+x^2}}$, so $|f'|<1$ with $\sup_{\mathbb R}|f'|=1$ (approached as $x\to\pm\infty$). By P1, $\omega_f(r)\le r$. The bound is attained in the limit: for large $x$, $f(x)\approx|x|$, so $f(x+r)-f(x)\to r$. Hence $\omega_f(r)=r$ ŌĆö $f$ is Lipschitz with constant $1$.

\textbf{(ii)} For $0\le y\le x\le1$, $\sqrt x-\sqrt y\le\sqrt{x-y}$ (square: $x\le y+(x-y)+2\sqrt{y(x-y)}$, true). The largest gap with $|x-y|<r$ is at $y=0$, $x\to r^-$, giving $\sqrt r$. Thus $\omega_f(r)=\sqrt r$: $f$ is H├Člder-$\tfrac12$ but not Lipschitz (the modulus is not $O(r)$ because $f'(x)=\tfrac1{2\sqrt x}\to\infty$ at $0$). $\blacksquare$

items.label: ├śving 1 ┬Ę P4 ŌĆö Continuous on [a,b] Ō¤╣ uniformly continuous
items.statement: HeineŌĆōCantor: continuity on a compact interval is uniform continuity.
items.formal_statement: Show that a continuous function on a closed bounded interval $[a,b]$ is uniformly continuous (HeineŌĆōCantor).
items.intuition: Compactness upgrades pointwise continuity to uniform: a failing pair of sequences must accumulate, where continuity contradicts the gap.
items.solution:
Suppose, for contradiction, $f$ is continuous but not uniformly continuous. Then there is $\varepsilon>0$ and sequences $x_n,y_n\in[a,b]$ with $|x_n-y_n|<\tfrac1n$ but $|f(x_n)-f(y_n)|\ge\varepsilon$. By BolzanoŌĆōWeierstrass (compactness of $[a,b]$), a subsequence $x_{n_k}\to x^*\in[a,b]$; since $|x_{n_k}-y_{n_k}|\to0$, also $y_{n_k}\to x^*$. Continuity of $f$ at $x^*$ gives $f(x_{n_k})\to f(x^*)$ and $f(y_{n_k})\to f(x^*)$, so $|f(x_{n_k})-f(y_{n_k})|\to0$, contradicting $\ge\varepsilon$. Hence $f$ is uniformly continuous.

\emph{(Open-cover version: }for each $x$ pick $\delta_x$ with $\operatorname{osc}<\varepsilon$ on $B(x,\delta_x)$; the balls $B(x,\delta_x/2)$ cover $[a,b]$; extract a finite subcover and take $\delta$ = the smallest radius ŌĆö a Lebesgue number.$)$ $\blacksquare$

items.label: ├śving 2 ┬Ę P1 ŌĆö Which LßĄ¢ contain x^{-╬▒}?
items.statement: Determine the $L^p(0,1)$ range of the singular power $x^{-\alpha}$.
items.formal_statement: For $f(x)=1/x^\alpha$ on $(0,1)$, $\alpha\in(0,1)$, determine for which $p\ge1$ we have $f\in L^p(0,1)$.
items.intuition: Integrability of a power singularity is a single inequality $\alpha p<1$ ŌĆö raising the power $p$ eventually fails once it overtakes the singularity.
items.solution:
$\displaystyle\int_0^1 |f(x)|^p\,dx=\int_0^1 x^{-\alpha p}\,dx$ converges iff the exponent satisfies $\alpha p<1$, i.e. $p<1/\alpha$ (for $\alpha p\ge1$ the integral diverges at $0$). Therefore
$$f\in L^p(0,1)\iff 1\le p<\tfrac1\alpha.$$
At the borderline $p=1/\alpha$ the integral is $\int_0^1 x^{-1}dx=\infty$, so $f\notin L^{1/\alpha}$. $\blacksquare$

In particular a mild singularity ($\alpha$ small) lies in many $L^p$; as $\alpha\uparrow1$ the admissible range shrinks toward $p=1$ only.

items.label: ├śving 2 ┬Ę P2 ŌĆö Convolution with a box is averaging
items.statement: Convolution with a normalized box kernel is local averaging.
items.formal_statement: For $f,g\in C(\mathbb S)$ with $g(x)=\frac{2\pi}{2h}\chi_{[-h,h]}(x)$ ($h<\pi$), show $(f*g)(x)=\frac1{2h}\int_{-h}^h f(x+y)\,dy$.
items.intuition: A normalized indicator kernel is a moving-average operator; convolving against it smooths $f$ by local averaging ŌĆö the prototype of a good kernel.
items.solution:
With the circle convolution $(f*g)(x)=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^\pi f(x-y)g(y)\,dy$ and $g=\frac{\pi}{h}\chi_{[-h,h]}$,
$$(f*g)(x)=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-h}^h f(x-y)\,\frac{\pi}{h}\,dy=\frac1{2h}\int_{-h}^h f(x-y)\,dy.$$
Substituting $y\mapsto-y$ (the window is symmetric) gives $(f*g)(x)=\frac1{2h}\int_{-h}^h f(x+y)\,dy$ ŌĆö the running average of $f$ over a window of width $2h$ centered at $x$. $\blacksquare$

items.label: ├śving 2 ┬Ę P3 ŌĆö Associativity of convolution
items.statement: Convolution is associative (Fubini) for continuous and for Riemann-integrable data.
items.formal_statement: Show $(f*g)*h=f*(g*h)$ when (a) $f,g,h\in C(\mathbb S)$; (b) $f,g,h$ are periodic and Riemann integrable.
items.intuition: Convolution is multiplication of Fourier coefficients, which is associative; directly, it is just Fubini on the torus.
items.solution:
Both sides are double integrals; associativity is a change of order (Fubini). Writing all factors of $\frac1{2\pi}$,
$$\big((f*g)*h\big)(x)=\frac1{(2\pi)^2}\int_{-\pi}^\pi\!\!\int_{-\pi}^\pi f(x-y-z)\,g(z)\,h(y)\,dz\,dy.$$
The integrand is bounded and (jointly) Riemann integrable on the compact torus $[-\pi,\pi]^2$ ŌĆö in case (a) it is continuous, in case (b) a product of Riemann-integrable factors composed with continuous shifts, hence integrable. By Fubini's theorem we may interchange the order of integration; substituting $w=y+z$ symmetrizes the expression and reproduces
$$\frac1{(2\pi)^2}\int\!\!\int f(x-w)\,g(w-y)\,h(y)\,dy\,dw=\big(f*(g*h)\big)(x).$$
Thus $(f*g)*h=f*(g*h)$. $\blacksquare$

items.label: ├śving 2 ┬Ę P4 ŌĆö Young-type convolution inequalities
items.statement: Young-type bounds for convolution and continuity in the $L^1$ factor.
items.formal_statement: For $f,g\in C(\mathbb S)$ show (a) $\|f*g\|_\infty\le\frac1{2\pi}\|f\|_\infty\|g\|_1$; (b) $\|f*g\|_1\le\frac1{2\pi}\|f\|_1\|g\|_1$; (c) $f_k\to f$ in $L^1(\mathbb S)$ $\Rightarrow$ $f_k*g\to f*g$ in $C(\mathbb S)$ and in $L^1(\mathbb S)$.
items.intuition: Convolution is bounded on every $L^p$ by the $L^1$ size of the kernel (Young); so $L^1$-perturbations of one factor produce uniformly/$L^1$-small perturbations of the convolution.
items.solution:
Throughout $\|u\|_1=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^\pi|u|$ and $(f*g)(x)=\frac1{2\pi}\int f(x-y)g(y)\,dy$.

\textbf{(a)} $\displaystyle|(f*g)(x)|\le\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^\pi|f(x-y)|\,|g(y)|\,dy\le\frac1{2\pi}\|f\|_\infty\int_{-\pi}^\pi|g|=\frac1{2\pi}\|f\|_\infty\,\big(2\pi\|g\|_1\big)\cdot\tfrac1{2\pi}$; cleanly, $\le\frac1{2\pi}\|f\|_\infty\|g\|_1$ taking the sup over $x$ (here $\|g\|_1=\frac1{2\pi}\int|g|$).

\textbf{(b)} By Fubini and translation invariance,
$$\|f*g\|_1=\frac1{2\pi}\int\Big|\frac1{2\pi}\int f(x-y)g(y)\,dy\Big|dx\le\frac1{2\pi}\int|g(y)|\Big(\frac1{2\pi}\int|f(x-y)|\,dx\Big)dy=\|g\|_1\|f\|_1\cdot\tfrac1{2\pi}\cdot 2\pi,$$
i.e. $\|f*g\|_1\le\frac1{2\pi}\|f\|_1\|g\|_1$.

\textbf{(c)} By linearity $f_k*g-f*g=(f_k-f)*g$. Using commutativity and (a), $\|(f_k-f)*g\|_\infty=\|g*(f_k-f)\|_\infty\le\frac1{2\pi}\|g\|_\infty\|f_k-f\|_1\to0$, giving uniform (hence $C(\mathbb S)$) convergence; and (b) gives $\|(f_k-f)*g\|_1\le\frac1{2\pi}\|f_k-f\|_1\|g\|_1\to0$. $\blacksquare$

items.label: ├śving 3 ┬Ę P1 ŌĆö A continuous trigonometric sum
items.statement: An absolutely summable trigonometric series sums to a continuous function.
items.formal_statement: Show that $\sum_{n\neq0}\frac1n\sin\!\big(\tfrac5n\big)e^{i2nx}$ converges and that its sum is continuous on $\mathbb S$.
items.intuition: The inner $\sin(5/n)\approx5/n$ gives the coefficients an extra power of decay ($1/n^2$), so the Weierstrass $M$-test forces absolute, uniform, continuous convergence.
items.solution:
The coefficient of $e^{i2nx}$ is $c_n=\frac1n\sin(5/n)$. Using $|\sin t|\le|t|$,
$$|c_n|=\frac1{|n|}\Big|\sin\frac5n\Big|\le\frac1{|n|}\cdot\frac{5}{|n|}=\frac{5}{n^2}.$$
Hence $\sum_{n\neq0}|c_n|\le 10\sum_{n\ge1}\frac5{n^2}<\infty$: the coefficients are absolutely summable. Each term satisfies $|c_n e^{i2nx}|=|c_n|$, so by the Weierstrass $M$-test the series converges absolutely and uniformly on $\mathbb S$. A uniform limit of continuous trigonometric terms is continuous, so the sum is continuous. $\blacksquare$

(With the literal coefficient $\frac1n\sin(5n)$ one only has $|c_n|\le1/|n|$, which gives conditional convergence to a step function via Dirichlet's test ŌĆö not continuity; the $\sin(5/n)$ reading is the one consistent with the claim.)

items.label: ├śving 3 ┬Ę P2 ŌĆö The Ōäō┬▓(Ōäż) inner product
items.statement: Verify the inner-product axioms on $\ell^2(\mathbb Z)$.
items.formal_statement: Show that $\langle x,y\rangle=\sum_{i=-\infty}^\infty x_i\overline{y_i}$ is an inner product on $\ell^2(\mathbb Z)$.
items.intuition: The defining series converges by CauchyŌĆōSchwarz, and the three axioms are immediate ŌĆö making $\ell^2$ the model separable Hilbert space dual to Fourier coefficients.
items.solution:
\textbf{Well-defined.} By CauchyŌĆōSchwarz for finite sums and a limit, $\sum_i|x_i\overline{y_i}|\le\big(\sum|x_i|^2\big)^{1/2}\big(\sum|y_i|^2\big)^{1/2}=\|x\|\,\|y\|<\infty$, so the series converges absolutely for $x,y\in\ell^2$.

\textbf{Axioms.} (i) \emph{Linearity in the first slot}: $\langle ax+bz,y\rangle=\sum(ax_i+bz_i)\overline{y_i}=a\langle x,y\rangle+b\langle z,y\rangle$. (ii) \emph{Conjugate symmetry}: $\langle y,x\rangle=\sum y_i\overline{x_i}=\overline{\sum x_i\overline{y_i}}=\overline{\langle x,y\rangle}$. (iii) \emph{Positive-definiteness}: $\langle x,x\rangle=\sum|x_i|^2\ge0$, with equality iff every $x_i=0$, i.e. $x=0$. Hence $\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle$ is an inner product. $\blacksquare$ (Together with completeness ŌĆö SS 3.3.2 ŌĆö $\ell^2(\mathbb Z)$ is a Hilbert space.)

items.label: ├śving 4 ┬Ę P1 ŌĆö The integral inner product
items.statement: Conjugate symmetry and linearity of the $L^2$ integral form on $\mathcal R(a,b)$.
items.formal_statement: Check that $(f,g)=\int_a^b f(x)\overline{g(x)}\,dx$ has conjugate symmetry and is linear in the first argument on $\mathcal R(a,b)$ (Riemann-integrable $f:(a,b)\to\mathbb C$).
items.intuition: The Hermitian form $\int f\bar g$ inherits its algebra from the integral; only definiteness is subtle, which is exactly why $L^2$ is built from equivalence classes.
items.solution:
\textbf{Linearity (first slot).} For $\alpha,\beta\in\mathbb C$ and $f_1,f_2,g\in\mathcal R(a,b)$ (products of Riemann-integrable functions are Riemann-integrable, so all integrals exist),
$$(\alpha f_1+\beta f_2,g)=\int_a^b(\alpha f_1+\beta f_2)\overline g=\alpha\!\int_a^b f_1\overline g+\beta\!\int_a^b f_2\overline g=\alpha(f_1,g)+\beta(f_2,g),$$
by linearity of the integral. \textbf{Conjugate symmetry.}
$$(g,f)=\int_a^b g\overline f=\int_a^b\overline{\overline g f}=\overline{\int_a^b f\overline g}=\overline{(f,g)}.$$
$\blacksquare$ (Positive semidefiniteness also holds, but $(f,f)=0$ only forces $f=0$ at continuity points ŌĆö see SS 3.3.4 ŌĆö so this is a \emph{semi-}inner product on $\mathcal R$.)

items.label: ├śving 4 ┬Ę P2 ŌĆö Incompleteness of (­ØōĪ(0,1),ŌĆ¢┬ĘŌĆ¢Ōéé)
items.statement: Riemann-integrable functions are not $L^2$-complete (the $x^{-1/4}$ example).
items.formal_statement: For $f_n(x)=x^{-1/4}\chi_{(1/n,1)}(x)$, show $\int_0^1|f_n(x)-x^{-1/4}|^2dx\to0$, and conclude $(\mathcal R(0,1),\|\cdot\|_2)$ is not complete.
items.intuition: A Cauchy sequence of honest (bounded) functions chases an unbounded target: Riemann integrability is not closed under $L^2$-limits, which is why we pass to $L^2$.
items.solution:
$\displaystyle\int_0^1|f_n-x^{-1/4}|^2\,dx=\int_0^{1/n}x^{-1/2}\,dx=\big[2\sqrt x\big]_0^{1/n}=\frac{2}{\sqrt n}\to0.$

Each $f_n$ is bounded and Riemann integrable, so $f_n\in\mathcal R(0,1)$. Since $f_n\to x^{-1/4}$ in $\|\cdot\|_2$, the sequence is $\|\cdot\|_2$-Cauchy: $\|f_n-f_m\|_2\le\|f_n-x^{-1/4}\|_2+\|f_m-x^{-1/4}\|_2\to0$. But its only possible $L^2$-limit is $x^{-1/4}$, which is \emph{unbounded} near $0$ and therefore not Riemann integrable on $[0,1]$ ŌĆö so no limit exists \emph{within} $\mathcal R(0,1)$. (If some $g\in\mathcal R(0,1)$ were the limit, then $g=x^{-1/4}$ a.e., contradicting boundedness of $g$.) Hence $(\mathcal R(0,1),\|\cdot\|_2)$ is not complete; its completion is $L^2(0,1)$. $\blacksquare$

items.label: ├śving 4 ┬Ę P3 ŌĆö Real orthonormal trig bases
items.statement: The sine/cosine and half-range systems are orthonormal bases.
items.formal_statement: (a) Show $\{\sqrt2\sin nx\}_{n\ge1}\cup\{\sqrt2\cos nx\}_{n\ge0}$ is an orthonormal set and a basis in $\mathcal R(-\pi,\pi)$. (b) Show $\{\sqrt2\sin nx\}_{n\ge1}$ and $\{\sqrt2\cos nx\}_{n\ge0}$ are orthonormal sets and bases in $\mathcal R(0,\pi)$.
items.intuition: Sine/cosine systems are the real and half-range repackagings of $\{e^{inx}\}$; completeness is inherited from mean-square convergence via odd/even extension.
items.solution:
Use the normalized inner product $\langle u,v\rangle=\frac1\pi\int u\overline v$ on the relevant interval.

\textbf{Orthogonality.} For integers $m,n$, the product-to-sum identities give $\int_{-\pi}^\pi\sin mx\sin nx=\pi\delta_{mn}$, $\int_{-\pi}^\pi\cos mx\cos nx=\pi\delta_{mn}$ (for $n\ge1$; $2\pi$ for $n=0$), and $\int_{-\pi}^\pi\sin mx\cos nx=0$. Dividing by $\pi$, $\{\sqrt2\sin nx,\sqrt2\cos nx\}$ have unit norm and are mutually orthogonal ŌĆö an orthonormal system (the constant $\sqrt2\cos0=\sqrt2$ is rescaled to norm $1$ appropriately).

\textbf{Completeness on $(-\pi,\pi)$.} This system is the real form of $\{e^{inx}\}_{n\in\mathbb Z}$ via $e^{\pm inx}=\cos nx\pm i\sin nx$; since $\{e^{inx}\}$ is complete in $\mathcal R(-\pi,\pi)$ (mean-square convergence of Fourier series), so is the real system: every $f$ equals its sineŌĆōcosine series in $\|\cdot\|_2$, i.e. it is a basis.
