\textbf{Half-range on $(0,\pi)$.} Extending $f\in\mathcal R(0,\pi)$ \emph{oddly} to $(-\pi,\pi)$ shows $\{\sqrt2\sin nx\}_{n\ge1}$ alone is complete (the odd extension's series uses only sines); extending \emph{evenly} shows $\{\sqrt2\cos nx\}_{n\ge0}$ alone is complete. Orthonormality on $(0,\pi)$ follows from $\int_0^\pi\sin mx\sin nx=\tfrac\pi2\delta_{mn}=\int_0^\pi\cos mx\cos nx$ (so $\sqrt2$ normalizes with $\frac1\pi\int_0^\pi\cdot 2=1$). $\blacksquare$

items.label: ├śving 5 ┬Ę P1 ŌĆö Fourier series of x┬▓
items.statement: Full analysis of the Fourier series of $x^2$ (regularity, convergence, $\zeta(2)$).
items.formal_statement: Let $f(x)=x^2$ on $[-\pi,\pi]$. (a) Show $f\sim\frac{\pi^2}3+2\sum_{n\neq0}\frac{(-1)^n}{n^2}e^{inx}$. (b) It converges to $f$ in $L^2$. (c) Show $f\in C^\infty(-\pi,\pi)$, $f$ Lipschitz on $\mathbb S$, one-sided derivatives exist everywhere, but $f\notin C^1(\mathbb S)$. (d) Pointwise convergence on $\mathbb S$. (e) Absolute and uniform convergence on $\mathbb S$.
items.intuition: A parabola, periodized, is continuous with only a corner in its derivative ŌĆö exactly the regularity ($1/n^2$ decay) that makes the series converge uniformly and yields $\zeta(2)$.
items.solution:
\textbf{(a)} $\hat f(0)=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^\pi x^2dx=\frac{\pi^2}3$, and for $n\neq0$, two integrations by parts give $\hat f(n)=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^\pi x^2e^{-inx}dx=\frac{2(-1)^n}{n^2}$. Hence $f\sim\frac{\pi^2}3+\sum_{n\neq0}\frac{2(-1)^n}{n^2}e^{inx}$.

\textbf{(b)} $f$ is continuous (hence Riemann integrable) on the circle, so its Fourier series converges to $f$ in $L^2$ (mean-square convergence).

\textbf{(c)} On the open interval $f=x^2\in C^\infty(-\pi,\pi)$. The periodic extension is continuous ($f(-\pi)=f(\pi)=\pi^2$) and piecewise $C^1$ with $|f'(x)|=|2x|\le2\pi$, so $f$ is Lipschitz on $\mathbb S$. At the join $x=\pi$ the one-sided derivatives are $f'(\pi^-)=2\pi$ and $f'(\pi^+)=-2\pi$ (from the next period) ŌĆö both exist but differ, so $f\notin C^1(\mathbb S)$.

\textbf{(d)} $f$ is continuous and at every point either differentiable or possessing one-sided derivatives (a Dini/Dirichlet condition), so $S_N f\to f$ pointwise everywhere.

\textbf{(e)} $|\hat f(n)|=2/n^2$ is summable, so by the Weierstrass $M$-test the series converges absolutely and uniformly; the limit is the continuous $f$. $\blacksquare$

\emph{Byproduct.} Evaluating at $x=\pi$: $\pi^2=\frac{\pi^2}3+2\sum_{n\ge1}\frac1{n^2}$, giving $\sum 1/n^2=\pi^2/6$.

items.label: ├śving 5 ┬Ę P2 ŌĆö Coefficient decay Ō¤╣ smoothness
items.statement: Coefficient decay rates control the smoothness class of $f$.
items.formal_statement: Let $f$ be Riemann integrable on $\mathbb S$. (a) Show $|\hat f(n)|\le C/n^2$ implies $f\in C(\mathbb S)$. (b) Show $|\hat f(n)|\le e^{-|n|}$ implies $f\in C^\infty(\mathbb S)$.
items.intuition: Decay of $\hat f$ is smoothness of $f$: polynomial decay $1/n^2$ buys continuity, exponential decay buys infinite differentiability ŌĆö each derivative needs one more power summable.
items.solution:
\textbf{(a)} If $|\hat f(n)|\le C/n^2$ then $\sum_n|\hat f(n)|\le|\hat f(0)|+2C\sum_{n\ge1}n^{-2}<\infty$. By the Weierstrass $M$-test $g(x)=\sum_n\hat f(n)e^{inx}$ converges uniformly to a continuous function $g$, and $\hat g(n)=\hat f(n)$ for all $n$. A Riemann-integrable function and a continuous function with the same Fourier coefficients agree (the continuous representative), so $f\in C(\mathbb S)$.

\textbf{(b)} If $|\hat f(n)|\le e^{-|n|}$ then for every $k\ge0$, $\sum_n |n|^k|\hat f(n)|\le\sum_n|n|^k e^{-|n|}<\infty$. Thus the series differentiated term by term $k$ times, $\sum_n(in)^k\hat f(n)e^{inx}$, converges uniformly; so $f\in C^k$ for every $k$, i.e. $f\in C^\infty(\mathbb S)$. $\blacksquare$

items.label: ├śving 6 ┬Ę P1 ŌĆö A heat equation with a decay term
items.statement: A damped heat equation solved mode-by-mode via Fourier series.
items.formal_statement: For $u(x,t)=\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}\hat f(n)e^{-ct-\sigma^2n^2t}e^{inx}$: (a) show $u\in C^\infty((0,2\pi)\times(0,T))$; (b) show $u_t+cu-\sigma^2u_{xx}=0$ in $(0,2\pi)\times(0,T)$.
items.intuition: The Gaussian damping $e^{-\sigma^2n^2t}$ smooths instantly (super-polynomial decay), and the PDE is just the eigenvalue relation $\partial_t=\sigma^2\partial_{xx}-c$ acting mode-by-mode.
items.solution:
The coefficients $\hat f(n)$ are bounded, $|\hat f(n)|\le\|f\|_{L^1}$.

\textbf{(a)} Fix $t_0\in(0,T)$. For $t\ge t_0$ the factor $e^{-\sigma^2n^2t}\le e^{-\sigma^2n^2t_0}$ decays faster than any power of $n$, so for all $j,m$,
$$\sum_n\big|\hat f(n)\big|\,|n|^{j}\,(c+\sigma^2n^2)^{m}\,e^{-ct-\sigma^2n^2t}<\infty\quad\text{uniformly on }[t_0,T),$$
which dominates every term-by-term $x$- and $t$-derivative. Hence the series and all its partial derivatives converge uniformly on $(0,2\pi)\times[t_0,T)$, so $u\in C^\infty((0,2\pi)\times(0,T))$.

\textbf{(b)} Differentiating term by term (justified by (a)): $\partial_t$ brings down $(-c-\sigma^2n^2)$ and $\partial_{xx}$ brings down $(in)^2=-n^2$. The operator applied to the $n$-th term gives the factor
$$(-c-\sigma^2n^2)+c-\sigma^2(-n^2)=-c-\sigma^2n^2+c+\sigma^2n^2=0.$$
Every term vanishes, so $u_t+cu-\sigma^2u_{xx}=0$. $\blacksquare$

items.label: ├śving 7 ┬Ę P1 ŌĆö Scaling of the integral (SS Prop 1.1 iii)
items.statement: Dilation/scaling property of the integral on moderate-decrease functions.
items.formal_statement: Prove property (iii) of SS Proposition 1.1 (┬¦5.1.1): for $f\in\mathcal M(\mathbb R)$ (moderate decrease) and $\delta>0$, $\;\delta\int_{-\infty}^\infty f(\delta x)\,dx=\int_{-\infty}^\infty f(x)\,dx$.
items.intuition: Stretching the variable by $\delta$ rescales the integral by $1/\delta$ ŌĆö the change of variables that becomes the Fourier scaling law.
items.solution:
Both integrals exist because $f$ has moderate decrease ($|f(x)|\le A/(1+x^2)$). For finite $N$, substitute $u=\delta x$ ($du=\delta\,dx$):
$$\delta\int_{-N}^N f(\delta x)\,dx=\int_{-\delta N}^{\delta N}f(u)\,du.$$
Letting $N\to\infty$, the right side converges to $\int_{-\infty}^\infty f(u)\,du$ (the limit defining the integral of a moderate-decrease function), so
$$\delta\int_{-\infty}^\infty f(\delta x)\,dx=\int_{-\infty}^\infty f(x)\,dx.\qquad\blacksquare$$
This dilation rule underlies the scaling law $f(\delta x)\to\delta^{-1}\hat f(\delta^{-1}\xi)$ of the Fourier transform.

items.label: ├śving 7 ┬Ę P2 ŌĆö Continuity of translation in L┬╣ (SS Prop 1.1 iv)
items.statement: $L^1$-continuity of translation for moderate-decrease and $L^1$ functions.
items.formal_statement: Show property (iv) of SS Proposition 1.1, $\int_{-\infty}^\infty|f(x-h)-f(x)|\,dx\to0$ as $h\to0$, holds for (a) $f\in\mathcal R_m(\mathbb R)$ (Riemann-integrable, moderate decrease); (b) $f\in L^1(\mathbb R)$.
items.intuition: Translation is continuous in $L^1$: trivially true for smooth bumps, then transported to all of $L^1$ (and to moderate decrease) by density and the translation isometry.
items.solution:
\textbf{Continuous compactly supported core.} If $g\in C_c(\mathbb R)$, then $g$ is uniformly continuous and supported in some $[-R,R]$; for $|h|\le1$, $|g(x-h)-g(x)|<\varepsilon$ uniformly and the integrand is supported in $[-R-1,R+1]$, so $\int|g(\cdot-h)-g|\le\varepsilon\,(2R+2)\to0$.

\textbf{(b) $f\in L^1$.} $C_c(\mathbb R)$ is dense in $L^1$: pick $g\in C_c$ with $\|f-g\|_{L^1}<\varepsilon$. Translation is an $L^1$-isometry, so
$$\int|f(\cdot-h)-f|\le\int|f(\cdot-h)-g(\cdot-h)|+\int|g(\cdot-h)-g|+\int|g-f|=2\|f-g\|_{L^1}+\int|g(\cdot-h)-g|<3\varepsilon$$
for $|h|$ small. Hence the limit is $0$.

\textbf{(a) $f\in\mathcal R_m(\mathbb R)$.} A Riemann-integrable function of moderate decrease lies in $L^1$ and is approximable in $L^1$ by continuous compactly supported functions (truncate the tails using $|f|\le A/(1+x^2)$, then approximate the bounded RI piece by continuous functions). The same three-term estimate applies, giving $\int|f(\cdot-h)-f|\to0$. $\blacksquare$

items.label: ├śving 8 ┬Ę P1 ŌĆö Transform of the rescaled Gaussian
items.statement: Fourier transform of the unit-mass Gaussian family (self-dual at $\delta=1$).
items.formal_statement: Let $K(x)=e^{-\pi x^2}$ and $K_\delta(x)=\delta^{-1/2}K(\delta^{-1/2}x)$. Compute $\hat K_\delta$.
items.intuition: Scaling a Gaussian narrows it in space and widens it in frequency; the unit-mass family $K_\delta$ maps to the spreading multiplier $e^{-\pi\delta\xi^2}\to1$.
items.solution:
Start from the self-duality $\hat K=K$ (the Gaussian $e^{-\pi x^2}$ is its own transform) and the scaling law $\widehat{f(ax)}(\xi)=a^{-1}\hat f(a^{-1}\xi)$ for $a>0$. With $a=\delta^{-1/2}$,
$$\widehat{K(\delta^{-1/2}x)}(\xi)=\delta^{1/2}K(\delta^{1/2}\xi)=\delta^{1/2}e^{-\pi\delta\xi^2}.$$
Therefore $\hat K_\delta(\xi)=\delta^{-1/2}\cdot\delta^{1/2}e^{-\pi\delta\xi^2}=e^{-\pi\delta\xi^2}$. In particular at $\delta=1$ this recovers $\hat K=K$ ŌĆö the Gaussian is exactly self-dual ŌĆö while for general $\delta$ the unit-mass family $K_\delta$ (with $\int K_\delta=1$) transforms to $e^{-\pi\delta\xi^2}$. $\blacksquare$
(This $\hat K_\delta(\xi)=e^{-\pi\delta\xi^2}$ is precisely the multiplier used in P2.)

items.label: ├śving 8 ┬Ę P2 ŌĆö Gaussian multiplier and Fourier inversion
items.statement: Gaussian regularization and the limit underlying Fourier inversion.
items.formal_statement: (a) Show $\hat K_\delta(\xi)=e^{-\pi\delta\xi^2}\to1$ locally uniformly as $\delta\to0$. (b) For $f\in\mathcal S(\mathbb R)$, show $\int_{\mathbb R}\hat f(\xi)\hat K_\delta(\xi)\,d\xi\to\int_{\mathbb R}\hat f(\xi)\,d\xi$.
items.intuition: The spreading Gaussian multiplier $\to1$ boundedly, so it can be inserted and removed under the integral by dominated convergence ŌĆö the analytic heart of Fourier inversion.
items.solution:
\textbf{(a)} On a compact set $|\xi|\le R$, $0\le 1-e^{-\pi\delta\xi^2}\le\pi\delta R^2\to0$ as $\delta\to0$, so $\hat K_\delta\to1$ uniformly on $[-R,R]$ (locally uniformly).

\textbf{(b)} For $f\in\mathcal S$, $\hat f\in\mathcal S\subset L^1$. The integrands $\hat f(\xi)\hat K_\delta(\xi)$ converge pointwise to $\hat f(\xi)$ and are dominated by $|\hat f(\xi)|\in L^1$ (since $0<\hat K_\delta\le1$). By dominated convergence,
$$\int_{\mathbb R}\hat f(\xi)\hat K_\delta(\xi)\,d\xi\to\int_{\mathbb R}\hat f(\xi)\,d\xi.\qquad\blacksquare$$
Combined with the multiplication formula $\int\hat f K_\delta=\int f\hat K_\delta$ and $\hat K_\delta=K_\delta$-type approximate identity, this is the standard route to the Fourier inversion theorem (the Gaussian regularization).

items.label: ├śving 8 ┬Ę P3 ŌĆö Transform of 1/(a┬▓+x┬▓)
items.statement: Fourier transform of the Lorentzian $1/(a^2+x^2)$.
items.formal_statement: Show $\mathcal F\!\big[\tfrac1{a^2+x^2}\big](\xi)=\tfrac\pi a e^{-2\pi a|\xi|}$ for $a>0$.
items.intuition: The Lorentzian and the two-sided exponential are a Fourier pair (the line Poisson kernel); compute the easy direction and invert.
items.solution:
First compute the transform of the (even) two-sided exponential:
$$\mathcal F[e^{-2\pi a|x|}](\xi)=\int_{\mathbb R}e^{-2\pi a|x|}e^{-2\pi ix\xi}\,dx=2\int_0^\infty e^{-2\pi ax}\cos(2\pi\xi x)\,dx=\frac{2\cdot2\pi a}{(2\pi a)^2+(2\pi\xi)^2}=\frac{a/\pi}{a^2+\xi^2}.$$
So $\mathcal F[e^{-2\pi a|x|}](\xi)=\dfrac{a}{\pi(a^2+\xi^2)}$. Both functions are even and nice (moderate decrease / $L^1$), so Fourier inversion applies: applying $\mathcal F$ again (which reflects, but these are even) gives
$$\mathcal F\Big[\frac{a}{\pi(a^2+x^2)}\Big](\xi)=e^{-2\pi a|\xi|}\quad\Longrightarrow\quad \mathcal F\Big[\frac1{a^2+x^2}\Big](\xi)=\frac\pi a\,e^{-2\pi a|\xi|}.\qquad\blacksquare$$
(The pair $(\text{Lorentzian},\text{exponential})$ is the Poisson-kernel pair on the line.)

items.label: ├śving 8 ┬Ę P4 ŌĆö Moderate decrease Ō¤╣ uniformly continuous
items.statement: Continuous moderate-decrease functions are uniformly continuous.
items.formal_statement: Show every $f\in C_m(\mathbb R)$ (continuous, moderate decrease) is uniformly continuous on $\mathbb R$.
items.intuition: Decay tames the non-compact tails (values are tiny there), while compactness handles the middle ŌĆö together giving a single $\delta$ that works everywhere.
items.solution: Let $\varepsilon>0$. By moderate decrease choose $N$ with $|f(x)|<\varepsilon/2$ for $|x|>N$. On the compact $[-N-1,N+1]$, $f$ is uniformly continuous (HeineŌĆōCantor), so there is $0<\delta<1$ with $|f(x)-f(y)|<\varepsilon$ whenever $x,y\in[-N-1,N+1]$ and $|x-y|<\delta$. Now take any $x,y$ with $|x-y|<\delta$: if both lie in $[-N-1,N+1]$ we are done; otherwise at least one of them has modulus $>N$ and (since $|x-y|<1$) both do, giving $|f(x)-f(y)|\le|f(x)|+|f(y)|<\varepsilon$. Hence $f$ is uniformly continuous on all of $\mathbb R$. $\blacksquare$

items.label: ├śving 8 ┬Ę P5 ŌĆö Differentiating a convolution
items.statement: The derivative of a convolution falls on either factor.
items.formal_statement: For $f,g\in\mathcal S(\mathbb R)$ show $\frac{d}{dx}(f*g)(x)=(f*g')(x)$.
items.intuition: Convolution is smoothing by sliding $g$; differentiating the output just differentiates the moving copy of $g$, justified by dominated convergence.
items.solution:
Write $(f*g)(x)=\int_{\mathbb R}f(y)g(x-y)\,dy$. The difference quotient is
$$\frac{(f*g)(x+h)-(f*g)(x)}h=\int_{\mathbb R}f(y)\,\frac{g(x+h-y)-g(x-y)}h\,dy.$$
As $h\to0$ the inner quotient $\to g'(x-y)$ pointwise, and by the mean value theorem it equals $g'(x+\theta h-y)$ for some $|\theta|\le1$; since $g'\in\mathcal S$ is bounded and $f\in\mathcal S\subset L^1$, the integrand is dominated by $|f(y)|\,\|g'\|_\infty\in L^1$. Dominated convergence lets us pass the limit inside:
$$\frac{d}{dx}(f*g)(x)=\int_{\mathbb R}f(y)g'(x-y)\,dy=(f*g')(x).\qquad\blacksquare$$
(By symmetry it also equals $(f'*g)(x)$ ŌĆö derivatives may be placed on either factor.)

items.label: ├śving 8 ┬Ę P6 ŌĆö L┬╣ approximation by moderate-decrease functions
items.statement: Moderate-decrease functions are $L^1$-dense.
items.formal_statement: For $f\in L^1(\mathbb R)$, show there exist $f_n\in C_m(\mathbb R)$ with $\|f-f_n\|_{L^1(\mathbb R)}<1/n$.
items.intuition: Compactly supported continuous functions are both $L^1$-dense and trivially of moderate decrease, so $C_m$ is dense in $L^1$.
items.solution: Continuous compactly supported functions are dense in $L^1(\mathbb R)$: given $f\in L^1$, there is $f_n\in C_c(\mathbb R)$ with $\|f-f_n\|_{L^1}<1/n$ (standard approximation ŌĆö truncate, then mollify). Any $f_n\in C_c(\mathbb R)$ has moderate decrease: it is continuous and vanishes outside a compact $[-R,R]$, so $|f_n(x)|\le \|f_n\|_\infty\le \frac{A}{1+x^2}$ for a suitable $A$ (take $A=\|f_n\|_\infty(1+R^2)$). Hence $f_n\in C_m(\mathbb R)$ and $\|f-f_n\|_{L^1}<1/n$. $\blacksquare$

items.label: ├śving 9 ┬Ę P1 ŌĆö Three Fourier transforms
items.statement: Transforms of a parabolic bump, a Hermite-type Gaussian, and $x e^{-\pi a^2x^2}$.
items.formal_statement: Find the Fourier transforms of (a) $f_1(x)=(1-x^2)\chi_{[-1,1]}(x)$; (b) $f_2(x)=(1-x^2)e^{-\pi x^2}$; (c) $f_3(x)=x\,e^{-\pi a^2x^2}$.
items.intuition: Multiplication by $x$ and $x^2$ becomes differentiation on the transform side; combined with the self-dual Gaussian and the box, every transform here is a short calculation.
items.solution:
\textbf{(a)} With $\omega=2\pi\xi$ and the function even, $\hat f_1(\xi)=2\int_0^1(1-x^2)\cos(\omega x)\,dx=\frac{4}{\omega^3}(\sin\omega-\omega\cos\omega)$, i.e.
$$\hat f_1(\xi)=\frac{\sin(2\pi\xi)-2\pi\xi\cos(2\pi\xi)}{2\pi^3\xi^3}.$$

\textbf{(b)} Use $\hat g=g$ for $g(x)=e^{-\pi x^2}$ and the rule $\mathcal F[x^2g]=-\frac1{4\pi^2}\hat g''$. Since $\hat g''=(4\pi^2\xi^2-2\pi)e^{-\pi\xi^2}$, $\mathcal F[x^2g]=(-\xi^2+\tfrac1{2\pi})e^{-\pi\xi^2}$, so
$$\hat f_2(\xi)=\hat g-\mathcal F[x^2g]=\Big(1-\tfrac1{2\pi}+\xi^2\Big)e^{-\pi\xi^2}.$$

\textbf{(c)} For $g_a(x)=e^{-\pi a^2x^2}$, scaling gives $\hat g_a(\xi)=a^{-1}e^{-\pi\xi^2/a^2}$. Using $\mathcal F[xg]=\frac{i}{2\pi}\hat g'$,
$$\hat f_3(\xi)=\frac{i}{2\pi}\frac{d}{d\xi}\big(a^{-1}e^{-\pi\xi^2/a^2}\big)=-\frac{i\,\xi}{a^3}\,e^{-\pi\xi^2/a^2}.$$
$\blacksquare$ (Even real inputs give even real transforms; the odd input $f_3$ gives an odd, purely imaginary transform.)

items.label: ├śving 9 ┬Ę P2 ŌĆö L┬╣/L┬▓ membership and inverse transforms
items.statement: Classify sinc-type tails in $L^1/L^2$ and evaluate the inverse transform at $0,1$.
items.formal_statement: For (i) $f=\frac{\sin2\pi x}{x}\chi_{[0,\infty)}$, (ii) $f=\frac1x\chi_{[1,\infty)}$, (iii) $f=\frac{\sin2\pi x}{x^2}\chi_{[1,\infty)}$: decide $L^1$/$L^2$ membership; describe $g=\mathcal F^{-1}[\hat f]$ and $g(0),g(1)$.
items.intuition: Membership is decided by tail decay ($1/x$ fails $L^1$ but passes $L^2$); inversion returns $f$ a.e., and at a jump the symmetric inverse lands on the midpoint.
items.solution:
\textbf{Membership.} (i) near $0$, $\frac{\sin2\pi x}{x}\to2\pi$ (bounded); at $\infty$, $|f|\sim\frac1x\notin L^1$ but $|f|^2\sim\frac1{x^2}\in L^1$: so $f\in L^2\setminus L^1$. (ii) $\int_1^\infty\frac1x=\infty$, $\int_1^\infty\frac1{x^2}<\infty$: $f\in L^2\setminus L^1$. (iii) $|f|\le\frac1{x^2}$ on $[1,\infty)$: $f\in L^1\cap L^2$.

\textbf{Inverse transform.} On $L^2$ the transform is invertible, so in every case $g=\mathcal F^{-1}\hat f=f$ as an $L^2$ element (a.e.). Pointwise (symmetric inversion of a piecewise-smooth $f$) the value is the average of one-sided limits, $g(x_0)=\tfrac12\big(f(x_0^+)+f(x_0^-)\big)$:
- (i) $f(0^-)=0,\ f(0^+)=2\pi\Rightarrow g(0)=\pi$; $f$ continuous at $1$ ($\sin2\pi=0$) $\Rightarrow g(1)=0$.
- (ii) $f$ is $0$ near $0$ $\Rightarrow g(0)=0$; jump at $1$: $f(1^-)=0,f(1^+)=1\Rightarrow g(1)=\tfrac12$.
- (iii) $f\in L^1\cap L^2$, continuous (value $0$ at $1$) $\Rightarrow$ inversion holds pointwise, $g(0)=0,\ g(1)=0$. $\blacksquare$

items.label: ├śving 9 ┬Ę P3 ŌĆö Wave equation and d'Alembert
items.statement: Solve the 1-D wave equation by Fourier methods and derive d'Alembert's formula.
items.formal_statement: For $f\in\mathcal S(\mathbb R)$, $c\in\mathbb R$, let $u(x,t)=\mathcal F^{-1}\big[\hat f(\xi)\cos(2\pi c|\xi|t)\big](x)$. (a) Show $u\in C^\infty(\mathbb R\times(0,\infty))$ and $u_{tt}=c^2u_{xx}$. (b) Show $u\to f$, $u_t\to0$ as $t\to0^+$. (c) Show $u(x,t)=\tfrac12\big(f(x+ct)+f(x-ct)\big)$.
items.intuition: On the Fourier side the wave equation is the oscillator $\partial_{tt}=-(2\pi c\xi)^2$; the cosine multiplier splits into two translations, giving left- and right-moving copies of the data.
items.solution:
\textbf{(a)} $\hat u(\xi,t)=\hat f(\xi)\cos(2\pi c|\xi|t)$. Since $\cos(2\pi c|\xi|t)$ is even and smooth in $\xi$ and bounded by $1$, while $\hat f\in\mathcal S$, the product and all its $x,t$-derivatives are Schwartz in $\xi$ uniformly on $t$-compacts, so $u\in C^\infty$. Computing symbols: $\partial_{tt}\hat u=-(2\pi c|\xi|)^2\hat u=-4\pi^2c^2\xi^2\hat u$, while $\widehat{c^2u_{xx}}=c^2(2\pi i\xi)^2\hat u=-4\pi^2c^2\xi^2\hat u$. Equal, so $u_{tt}=c^2u_{xx}$.

\textbf{(b)} As $t\to0^+$, $\cos(2\pi c|\xi|t)\to1$ so $\hat u\to\hat f$, hence $u\to f$ (dominated convergence, $\hat f\in L^1$); and $\hat{u_t}=-2\pi c|\xi|\sin(2\pi c|\xi|t)\hat f\to0$, so $u_t\to0$.

\textbf{(c)} Write $\cos(2\pi c\xi t)=\tfrac12(e^{2\pi ic\xi t}+e^{-2\pi ic\xi t})$ (even, so $|\xi|\to\xi$ is harmless). Since $\mathcal F^{-1}[\hat f(\xi)e^{2\pi ic\xi t}](x)=f(x+ct)$,
$$u(x,t)=\tfrac12\big(f(x+ct)+f(x-ct)\big),$$
d'Alembert's formula. $\blacksquare$

items.label: ├śving 10 ┬Ę P1 ŌĆö Heisenberg for non-normalized f
items.statement: The scale-free form of the Heisenberg inequality.
items.formal_statement: Show Heisenberg's uncertainty principle can be written, for arbitrary $f$ (not assuming $\|f\|_2=1$), as $\|xf\|_2\,\|2\pi\xi\hat f\|_2=\|xf\|_2\,\|f'\|_2\ge\tfrac12\|f\|_2^2$.
items.intuition: Stripping the normalization shows uncertainty is a homogeneous inequality: the product of spreads is bounded below by $\tfrac12\|f\|_2^2$, scaling correctly under $f\mapsto\lambda f$.
items.solution:
Plancherel applied to $f'$ gives $\|f'\|_2=\|\widehat{f'}\|_2=\|2\pi i\xi\hat f\|_2=\|2\pi\xi\hat f\|_2$, which is the first equality. For the inequality, integrate $\|f\|_2^2=\int|f|^2$ by parts (boundary term vanishes for Schwartz $f$, as $x|f|^2\to0$):
$$\|f\|_2^2=-\int_{\mathbb R}x\,\frac{d}{dx}|f|^2\,dx=-2\,\mathrm{Re}\int_{\mathbb R}x\,\overline f\,f'\,dx\le 2\int_{\mathbb R}|xf|\,|f'|\,dx\le 2\,\|xf\|_2\,\|f'\|_2$$
(CauchyŌĆōSchwarz). Dividing by $2$,
$$\|xf\|_2\,\|f'\|_2\ge\tfrac12\|f\|_2^2,\qquad\text{equivalently}\qquad \|xf\|_2\,\|2\pi\xi\hat f\|_2\ge\tfrac12\|f\|_2^2.$$
$\blacksquare$ Normalizing $\|f\|_2=1$ recovers $\|xf\|_2^2\|\xi\hat f\|_2^2\ge\frac1{16\pi^2}$.

items.label: ├śving 11 ┬Ę P1 ŌĆö Fourier transforms in ŌäØ┬│
items.statement: Compute 3-D Fourier transforms of a separable and a radial function.
items.intuition: Product functions transform coordinate-wise (Lorentzian ŌåÆ exponential per axis); radial functions reduce to a single sine integral, turning $e^{-|x|}$ into a squared-Lorentzian.
items.solution:
\textbf{(i) Separable (tensor) function.} Since $f(x)=\prod_{j=1}^3\frac1{1+x_j^2}$ and $e^{-2\pi ix\cdot\xi}=\prod_j e^{-2\pi ix_j\xi_j}$, the transform factorizes into one-dimensional transforms. By ├śving 8 P3 (with $a=1$), $\mathcal F\big[\tfrac1{1+x^2}\big](\eta)=\pi e^{-2\pi|\eta|}$, so
$$\hat f(\xi)=\prod_{j=1}^3\pi e^{-2\pi|\xi_j|}=\pi^3\,e^{-2\pi(|\xi_1|+|\xi_2|+|\xi_3|)}.$$

\textbf{(ii) Radial function.} For radial $f$ in $\mathbb R^3$, the transform is radial with $\hat f(\xi)=\frac{2}{\rho}\int_0^\infty f(r)\,r\sin(2\pi r\rho)\,dr$, $\rho=|\xi|$ (from $\int_{S^2}e^{-2\pi ir\rho\cos\theta}\,d\sigma=\frac{2\sin(2\pi r\rho)}{r\rho}$). With $f(r)=e^{-r}$ and $b=2\pi\rho$, $\int_0^\infty e^{-r}r\sin(br)\,dr=\frac{2b}{(1+b^2)^2}$, so
$$\hat f(\xi)=\frac2\rho\cdot\frac{2b}{(1+b^2)^2}=\frac{8\pi}{(1+4\pi^2|\xi|^2)^2}.$$
$\blacksquare$
